**1. :fas-info-circle: Java Coding Standard: Lambda-style switch**
</div>

In the [Java Coding Standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html), the following is stated:

> Lambda-style switch statements/expressions can have indented case blocks (as shown below):

'''java
switch (condition) ``{``
    case ABC -&gt; method("1");
    case DEF -&gt; method("2");
    case XYZ -&gt; method("3");
    default -&gt; method("0");
``}``
int size = switch (condition) ``{``
    case ABC -&gt; 1;
    case DEF -&gt; 2;
    case XYZ -&gt; 3;
    default -&gt; 0;
``}``
'''

Could I clarify if "can have" means that it is also **ok (not a violation)**, if the case blocks are not indented?
For example, this is acceptable and not a violation:
'''java
switch (condition) ``{``
case ABC -&gt; method("1");
case DEF -&gt; method("2");
case XYZ -&gt; method("3");
default -&gt; method("0");
``}``
</panel>

**8. :fas-info-circle: Duplicate Bug Labels**
</div>

I understand that:
> the type.*, severity.*, response.* and assignees of the original issue are inherited by (and override) those of the duplicate -- i.e., the duplicate's own different labels/assignees (if any) are ignored.

Also if the duplicate bug is rejected, it still inherits from the original bug:
> If a bug's 'duplicate' status was rejected later (i.e., the tester says it is not really a duplicate and the teaching team agrees with the tester), it will inherit the response/type/severity/assignees from the 'original' bug that it was claimed to be a duplicate of.

**Question:** I would just like to clarify that despite the above, can we still change the labels (i.e. severity, type or add our own labels) of the duplicate bug for our own triaging and reference (although they will be ignored)?
</panel>

**13. :fas-info-circle: PE-D Qualities of a Good Bug Report**
</div>

I would like to clarify whether we should include suggestions to improve the product we are testing in our bug reports.

Based on this section it mentioned that good bug reports can point out a good way to improve the product:

> For reference, here are what we consider as qualities of a good bug report:
> * has a descriptive title
> * has enough details e.g., steps to reproduce, expected, actual, and screenshots.
> * severity/type labels chosen are not too far off
> * is written in a non-confrontational tone
> * points out a potentially problematic behavior (or a **good way** to improve the product)

However, the following section also states not to post suggestions:
> Do not post suggestions but if the product is missing a critical functionality that makes the product less useful to the intended user, it can be reported as a bug of type Type.FeatureFlaw. The dev team is allowed to reject bug reports framed as mere suggestions or/and lacking in a convincing justification as to why the omission or the current design of that functionality is problematic.
</panel>

**15. :fas-info-circle: Equivalence Partitions**
</div>

Can an equivalent partition be further broken down into sub equivalence partitions? or do we consider those "sub equivalence partitions" to be the equivalence partitions to begin with.

**18. :fas-info-circle: Usage of Exceptions and Assertions**
</div>

I understand that:
* Assertions should be used to check for developer errors. 
* Exceptions should be used to check for user or environment errors. 
* Assertions should not be used for user or environment errors because they can be turned-off.

However, are there situations where it is appropriate to use exceptions, which cannot be turned-off, to check for developer errors?
</panel>

**19. :fas-info-circle: Week 9 to 10 Briefing: CCDs**
</div>

I have a follow-up question on the following CCD with regards to the usage of association class

The given problem is as follows:
> A course has a name and a code. A course is read by 10 or more students, and taught by one or more instructors one of whom is the coordinator.

<img width="663" height="342" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9fb77459-53ec-46c3-aefc-780f7cc53a72" />

The above CCD was mentioned as a correct problem domain model. Another alternative way to represent the association class would be not using the dotted line, and instead having solid lines from Course to Role, and Role to Person which allows us to show multiplicity.

However, for the alternative way, if we were to have a Course having `1..*` Roles (this represents a `Course` must have 1 or more `Role`) and Person having `*` Roles (this represents a `Person` can have 0 or more `Role`), it would still be possible for a course to have no coordinators (as we cannot enforce that one of the `Role` must have `isCoordinator = True`). Would this then be not accurately representing the part on "one of whom is the coordinator" in the problem description?

Thank you.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/541" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**20. :fas-info-circle: Satisfying LSP**
</div>

**Under [LSP](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week9/topics.html#principles-liskov-substitution-principle) the following example was given:**

> The Rectangle#resize() method can take any integers for height and width. This contract is violated by the subclass Square#resize() because it does not accept a height that is different from the width.

'''java
class Rectangle ``{``
    ...
    /** sets the size to the given height and width*/
    void resize(int height, int width) ``{``
        ...
    ``}``
``}``


class Square extends Rectangle ``{``

    @Override
    void resize(int height, int width) ``{``
        if (height != width) ``{``
            //error
       ``}``
    ``}``
``}``
'''

> Now consider the following method that is written to work with the Rectangle class.

'''java
void makeSameSize(Rectangle original, Rectangle toResize) ``{``
    toResize.resize(original.getHeight(), original.getWidth());
``}``
'''
> This code will fail if it is called as makeSameSize(new Rectangle(12, 8), new Square(4, 4)). That is, the Square class is not substitutable for the Rectangle class.


**Question:** If Rectangle extends Square instead, do we consider that it satisfies LSP?

**Argument 1:** Square is more restrictive, and Rectangle (the subclass) is less restrictive. Hence, it satisfies LSP.

**Argument 2:** If another class calls method `Square.resize(2, 4)`, expecting an exception to be thrown, but `Rectangle` is substituted for `Square` and does not throw an exception, then there could be an undesirable behaviour. Would this violate LSP?

</panel>

**22. :fas-info-circle: Week 8 Test Coverage Clarification**
</div>

For the [Test Coverage](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week8/topics.html#quality-assurance-testing-test-coverage-what) section:

> if(x > 2 && x &lt; 44) is considered one decision point but two conditions.
> For 100% branch or decision coverage, two test cases are required:
> 
> 1. (x > 2 && x &lt; 44) == true : [e.g., x == 4]
> 2. (x > 2 && x &lt; 44) == false : [e.g., x == 100]
> 
> For 100% condition coverage, three test cases are required:
> 
> 1. (x > 2) == true , (x &lt; 44) == true : [e.g., x == 4] [see note 1]
> 2. (x &lt; 44) == false : [e.g., x == 100]
> 3. (x > 2) == false : [e.g., x == 0]

**Qn 1:** Would it be more accurate for the 2nd test case of condition coverage to state:
`(x > 2) == true, (x &lt; 44) == false : [e.g., x == 100]`.

Whereas, the 3rd test case need not state the second condition (since short circuit evaluation means that the 2nd condition will not be checked).

**Qn 2:** In general, is it correct to say that branch/decision coverage is only concerned about the final decision, whereas for condition coverage we need test cases to check **all possible permutations** of each expression (e.g. for 2 expression in a condition, then we need to test FF, FT, TF and TT, where some of them may be omitted due to short circuiting)?

</panel>

**24. :fas-info-circle: Week 8 Quiz Part 1 Clarifications**

<img width="480" height="433" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b62568ce-f057-4881-92df-edbeec1bbaf5" />

1) For Q14, wouldn't it be possible for option (b) to be an acceptable representation as well? Since `Player` class code isn't shown, it is possible that it inherits `Movable`. This is because in Java, even if the subclass inherits the superclass that implements an interface, the subclass can still implement the interface explicitly (though redundant).


</br>
<img width="553" height="386" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2cb871f2-1fc9-40f4-88f0-60b11f9cd440" />

2) For Q19, just to clarify **if** there was an option (d), that is similar to option (b) but all three instances do not have names (i.e. omit the instance variable name adam, ben and charlie), would option (d) still be correct (which omit both instance names and variable values)?


</br>
<img width="460" height="272" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/60b83cfb-d8a6-4df0-b71c-18cf55aa886a" />

3) For Q22, is there a rule that states `alt` paths must have at least 2 frames?
Otherwise, for option (c), wouldn't it be correct as the use of `alt` satisfies the definition:
> No more than one alternative partitions be executed in an alt frame. That is, it is **acceptable for none of the alternative partitions to be executed** but it is not acceptable for multiple partitions to be executed.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/427" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**25. :fas-info-circle: Clarifications on Use Case Extensions**
</div>

```
System: Online Banking System (OBS)
Use case: UC23 - Transfer Money
Actor: User
MSS:
  1. User chooses to transfer money.
  2. OBS requests for details of the transfer.
  3. User enters the requested details.
  4. OBS requests for confirmation.
  5. User confirms.
  6. OBS transfers the money and displays the new account balance.
  Use case ends.
Extensions:
  3a. OBS detects an error in the entered data.
      3a1. OBS requests for the correct data.
      3a2. User enters new data.
      Steps 3a1-3a2 are repeated until the data entered are correct.
      Use case resumes from step 4.

  3b. User requests to effect the transfer in a future date.
      3b1. OBS requests for confirmation.
      3b2. User confirms future transfer.
      Use case ends.

  *a. At any time, User chooses to cancel the transfer.
      *a1. OBS requests to confirm the cancellation.
      *a2. User confirms the cancellation.
      Use case ends.

  *b. At any time, 120 seconds lapse without any input from the User.
      *b1. OBS cancels the transfer.
      *b2. OBS informs the User of the cancellation.
      Use case ends.
```

Given the following [example](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week7/topics.html#requirements-specifying-requirements-use-cases-details), I would like to clarify the following:
1) Do we strictly use alphabets (a, b, etc.) to denote either a or b will happen (but not both), whereas use numbers (1, 2, 3, etc.) to denote sequential ordering.

2) For the extension `*a.`, in the example above, why is it that `*a.` can happen **after** step 6 when OBS already transferred the money?

3) Are there any notations we can use if we want to mention the idea of `[1-5]a.` where this extension can happen only after steps 1, 2, 3, 4 or 5?
</panel>

**30. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Object Deletion in Exercise**
</div>

For the exercise [Explain Sequence Diagram (ParserFactory)](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week6/topics.html#design-modelling-modelling-behaviors-sequence-diagrams-intermediate)

> Explain the interactions depicted in this sequence diagram.

<img width="550" height="399" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4ea721ac-a784-4812-8c8a-0afc0037d26a" />

1) Since the object deletion of `:Parser` occurs before the `createParser()` method's activation bar ends, does it mean that the `Parser` instance was not assigned to a variable in the method? hence we consider it as deleted soon after the `set()` or `reset()` method was called?

**33. :fas-info-circle: Code Quality: Usage of Default Branch**
</div>

In [W5.4n](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week5/topics.html#implementation-code-quality-error-prone-practices-basic-use-the-default-branch), it was mentioned:

> This also applies to the final else of an if-else construct. That is, the final else should mean 'everything else', and not the final option. Do not use else when an if condition can be explicitly specified, unless there is absolutely no other possibility.

I understand the example given:
'''java
if (red) print "red";
else if (blue) print "blue";
else error("incorrect input");
'''

However, is explicitly specifying the if condition still needed in this scenario, where there are only 2 possible options:
'''java
if (x > 0) do task1;
else if (x &lt;= 0) do task2;
else error("incorrect input"); // never reachable
'''

**35. :fas-info-circle: UML Labels vs Stereotypes**
</div>

Under the [Stereotypes section](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week4/topics.html#tools-uml-stereotypes), the following example was given: 

> UML stereotypes are a lightweight extension mechanism for adding domain-specific meaning to existing UML elements without changing the core UML language. They label elements such as classes, objects, or relationships with roles or semantics beyond their default interpretation (for example, `<<entity>>`, `<<create>>`, `<<facade>>`), helping readers understand intent, architectural responsibilities, or usage patterns at a glance. Stereotypes do not change the fundamental structure of the model; instead, they enrich it with contextual meaning, often forming part of a UML profile tailored to a particular problem domain.

<img width="203" height="69" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/81b151e7-ba81-4530-b1ff-e26a07bd9f2f" />

**Question:** In the diagram shown, why don't we use a label "create" rather than the stereotype `<<create>>`? How do we ascertain when to use a Label vs Stereotype?
</panel>

**36. :fas-info-circle: UML Aggregation: Does it need to be at the concept level?**
</div>

Under UML [Composition](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week4/topics.html#modeling-composition), it was mentioned that

> Cascading deletion alone is not sufficient for composition. Suppose there is a design in which Person objects are attached to Task objects and the former get deleted whenever the latter is deleted. This fact alone does not mean there is a composition relationship between the two classes. For it to be composition, a Person must be an integral part of a Task in the context of that association, at the concept level (not simply at implementation level).

Could I clarify if the above also applies for Aggregation (i.e. in order to have an aggregation association, there needs to be a conceptual container-containee relationship and not simply at implementation level)?

**39. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Java Coding Standard for Lambda Expressions**
</div>

When running the CheckStyle script, it highlighted that the following code violates the coding standard:
Example 1:
'''java
    @Test
    public void deserialiseTask_invalidTodo_exceptionThrown() ``{``
        assertThrows(ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.class,
                () -&gt; ``{`` Storage.deserialiseTask("T|0"); ``}``
        );
    ``}``
'''
CheckStyle Violations Shown:
- '(' should be on the previous line. [SeparatorWrap]. This refers to the left bracket of the lambda expression
- '``{``' at column should have line break after. [LeftCurly]. This refers to the first curly brace after ->

&lt;br>

However, the following does not violate the coding standard:
Example 2:
'''java
    public void deserialiseTask_invalidTodo_exceptionThrown() ``{``
        assertThrows(ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.class, (
                ) -&gt; ``{``
            Storage.deserialiseTask("T|0");
        ``}``
        );
    ``}``
'''

Could I clarify if lambda expressions also need to follow the Egyptian brackets mentioned in our coding standard, or would Example 1 meet the coding standard in this case? (i.e. the Java Coding Standard given to us does not mention specifically for lambda expressions, and we are free to choose our own style as long as it is consistent)
</panel>

**50. :fas-info-circle: Explicit declaration of unchecked exception in throws clause**
</div>

In the [[Key Exercise] parse rectangle descriptor](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week1/topics.html#key-exercise-parse-rectangle-descriptor), the Partial solution for `calculateArea` method indicates the clause `throws IllegalShapeException` which I understand as a checked exception is subject to the **_Catch or Specify Requirement_**.

While I understand unchecked exceptions need not follow the **_Catch or Specify Requirement_**, are there best practices of whether to specify them in the `throws` clause?

For example, would the following full solution be correct:
```
public class Main ``{``

    public static void printArea(String descriptor)``{``
        try ``{``
            System.out.println(descriptor + "=" + calculateArea(descriptor));
        ``}`` catch (NumberFormatException e) ``{``
            System.out.println("WIDTH or HEIGHT is not a number: " + descriptor);
        ``}`` catch (IndexOutOfBoundsException e) ``{`` // We catch and handle here
             // print error message  
        ``}`` catch (IllegalShapeException e) ``{`` // We catch and handle here
             // print error message  
        ``}``
    ``}``

    private static int calculateArea(String descriptor) throws IllegalShapeException ``{`` // Do not need to declare unchecked exceptions here
        String[] dimensions = descriptor.split("x");

        //throw IllegalShapeException here if dimensions.length > 2

        return Integer.parseInt(dimensions[0]) * Integer.parseInt(dimensions[1]);

        // Integer.parseInt already throws NumberFormatException and attempting to access an invalid index/element in the
        // dimensions array would cause IndexOutOfBoundsException to be thrown. 
        // Hence our method does not need to throw either of these unchecked exceptions here. Would this be correct?
    ``}``


``}``

```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/22#issuecomment-3773459911" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**59 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I believe point 1 from this [T4L1 extract](https://git-mastery.org/lessons/show/index.html#t4l1-examining-a-commit) below answers part of your question:

> **Although each commit represents a copy of the entire working directory, Git uses space efficiently in two main ways:**
> **1. Reuse of unchanged data:** If a file hasn’t changed since a previous commit, the commit simply points to the already stored version of that file instead of making another copy. This means only new or changed files take up extra space, while unchanged files are reused.
> **2. Compression:** Git also compresses all the files and data it stores using an algorithm (zlib). So, even the objects that are stored (whether reused or new) take up less disk space because they are saved in a compressed format.

From my understanding, files that are unchanged will not be in the index/staging area, but rather the commit will point to these unchanged files (as specified in the extract above) so that the commit can function as a full snapshot to recreate the working directory if required. 

With regards to file tracking, when we stage a file, git will automatically track the file (if it is not already being tracked) for the repository (and hence will continue to be tracked for all future commits). Here is an extract from [T1L4](https://git-mastery.org/lessons/stage/):
> **Staging applies regardless of whether a file is currently tracked.**
> * Staging an untracked file will both begin tracking the file and include it in the next snapshot.
> * Staging an already tracked file will simply mark its current changes for inclusion in the next commit.

Additionally, in order to stop git from tracking the files in the repository, we can specify it in a `.gitignore` file (you may refer to [T2L6 ](https://git-mastery.org/lessons/ignore/) for further information).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96#issuecomment-3822909592" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**64 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Other than the suggestions above, another alternative I can think of is for the `Storage` class to have a `loadTasks(TaskList)` method. This way, by passing an instance of TaskList, the `Storage` class will still be responsible for reading from storage (i.e. loading archived data from the file to the TaskList instance) and can call an `addTask` instance method to load into the TaskList instance.

`Storage` remains responsible for reading from file, while `TaskList ` is responsible for adding tasks to the list. This allows for the possibility of modifying the internal data structure of `TaskList` in the future, without needing to modify `Storage` (e.g. the return type of the `loadTasks` method).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/92#issuecomment-3822967672" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**65 :fas-comment:**
</div>

From the perspective of both the designer and a user, I feel that if the storage file is detected as corrupted, the chatbot could initialise and start afresh (i.e. not utilising the corrupted storage file, and potentially overriding or archiving it) while notifying the user about the storage file corruption. 

This allows the user to:
1. Be aware of the corruption (and why previously saved tasks aren't loading).
2. Still use the application.

And from the designer's point of view: 
1. There are less risks of further corruption in the storage file inadvertently affecting the correctness of the application and the chatbot's behaviour.
</panel>

**Dependency:** Able to use only stereotypes
**Association:** Able to use either labels or stereotypes

When to use:
* Use Stereotype to define custom elements (e.g. when there are multiple places we would like to indicate the stereotype `&lt;&lt;create>>` such that it could be repeated several times)
* Use labels to add information to element (may not be repetitive)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/144#issuecomment-3847835005" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**71 :fas-comment:**

> If compartments may be omitted, and the attributes/operations themselves can also be omitted if deemed irrelevant to the purpose of the diagram, then how will we represent a class with no attributes (and/or operations)?

I believe that you are right to conclude that it would be difficult to differentiate a class with no attributes/operations from one that does but omits it. My understanding is that the UML diagram is not meant to specify exactly all the attributes/operations of the class but rather those that are relevant for the purpose of what that specific UML diagram is trying to illustrate/communicate.


> By the same token, when looking at a UML class diagram, is it correct to say that even if the class diagram has an empty attribute compartment, we cannot conclude that the class has no attributes, right?

I agree with you on this.
</panel>

My view is that the static methods can be made more general, such as accepting the message to be printing through parameters. That would allow flexibility and would not require a new method if there is a new message that needs to be printed. 

Alternatively, instance-level methods would be appropriate if the message is passed into the constructor of the `Ui` class, and the methods would refer to the instance fields for the messages to be printed.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177#issuecomment-3876973270" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**74 :fas-comment:**
</div>

A concrete example I can think of is when your program is calling a method say `method1(String)`, and your program is such a way that the method should never be called with a `null `parameter, regardless of the user input (i.e. user input validation has already been performed before the method is called). Then, Java assertions can be used to validate that indeed, the argument received should never be `null`.

To verify that `assert` is used appropriately, the logic of the code should not break if the `assert` statement is deleted or turned off.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3904508420" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**77 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Adding on to this discussion, I would like to elaborate on two areas:

> Let's say I have a local file empty.txt, I stage and commit it in my local repo, push it to a remote repo, add one line "hi" to it, then stage and commit it in my local repo again.
> Am I right to say that:
> 1. Reverting the commit will basically make a new commit that erases the line in my local repo, but not in the remote repo until it (the new commit) is pushed. When I open the txt locally now, it will be empty.

First, on point (1), my understanding is that since the **second commit was not pushed to the remote repository yet**, reverting the commit locally will not affect the remote repo, but at this point the **remote repo is actually in the same state as the local repo** (both has empty.txt without any lines in it).

Second, for the scenario of point (1), performing a hard reset back to the first commit might be cleaner, as it will not create extra commits in the remote repo. Whereas reverting, once pushed to the remote repo, will cause the remote repo to have an extra 2 commits (second commit where `hi` was added and third commit to revert).

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**85 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, from my understanding, the course suggests that we aim to reduce coupling as much as possible, but that does not mean there can be no dependencies (otherwise, our application's features would not function cohesively).

For example, it would be right in the microservices example you provided that each service might require information from another service. However, each service is responsible for doing only 1 thing, and its responsibility not split across different services. These services should communicate with one another through another service's APIs when they require something that another service is responsible for.

Hope this helps!
</panel>

Thanks Prof. Could I clarify if the following summary is a correct understanding:

**Done by developers (hence is a type of developer testing):**
- Unit Testing
- Integration Testing

**Done by QA Team:**
- System Testing

**Done by a team representing the users / users themselves:**
- Acceptance Testing
- Alpha Testing
- Beta Testing

**Done by developers / employees in developers' own company:**
- Dogfooding

In addition, can Exploratory vs Scripted Testing **approach** be used for any of the above testing **categories**?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/507#issuecomment-4021320453" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**88 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Branch/decision coverage is about each sub-condition being evaluated to true and false at least once. It does not require all permutations. So, if the experession is C1 && C2 && C3, the following four test cases are enough to achieve branch coverage.

Hi Prof, with regards to the branch/decision coverage, if each sub-condition must be evaluated to true and false at least once, would the example given in the textbook then not result in 100% branch/decision coverage?

This is because if x == 4 and x == 100, then in both the test cases, x > 2 is always true. Since x &lt; 2 == false is never tested, then 100% branch/decision coverage would not be achieved.

**Example in Textbook:**
> if(x > 2 && x &lt; 44) is considered one decision point but two conditions. For 100% branch or decision coverage, two test cases are required:
> 
> 1. (x > 2 && x &lt; 44) == true : [e.g., x == 4]
> 2. (x > 2 && x &lt; 44) == false : [e.g., x == 100]
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/514#issuecomment-4053061944" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**89 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> System testing only tells us if we have satisfied system specification and not our user requirements.

I agree with the point mentioned above! To further elaborate on this, the [textbook](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week8/topics.html#quality-assurance-testing-acceptance-testing-acceptance-versus-system-testing) provides an example of how system specification might not be aligned with what the user wanted:
> The system might conform to the system specification but could fail to solve the problem it was supposed to solve for the user, due to flaws in the system design.

Perhaps this could be due to wrong system specifications drafted by the product designers, when translating the requirement specification to system specification.


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/732#issuecomment-4303426744" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**92 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @Echoandland! I had a similar question in issue #125 and my understanding from the discussion there is as follows:

**Labels:** Are more generic, and can be used for most cases.
**UML Stereotypes:** However, we choose to use stereotypes when we want to indicate a more specialised type of a UML entity in the diagram (Note that stereotypes are optional information).

I also had a question on whether the number of times we use the stereotype matters when deciding when to use it. However, it is not the main reason why we choose to use stereotypes, although being able to reuse it adds value. 

**Correction:** Build-in classifier notation like `&lt;&lt;interface>>` and `&lt;&lt;enumeration>>` are not stereotypes. They are more like "keywords" which contain important (non-optional) information.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/752" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Activity Diagram Branch Node**
</div>

Hi everyone, I'm confused on why it's okay to omit `[Else]` condition as mentioned in the [textbook](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/se-book-adapted/chapters/modeling.html#activity-diagrams-basic).

> Some acceptable simplifications (by convention):
Omitting the [Else] condition.

Since I thought that exactly one, not none and not more than one of the guard conditions on the branch node should be true at any given branch node? 

> Exactly one of the guard conditions should be true at any given branch node.

Does "Omitting the [Else] condition" only apply in examples like `[x &lt; 5]` `[x >= 5]` where all other possibilities have been handled so there can't be an else condition, hence it is okay to omit? 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/735" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: LoD: What does objects created indirectly mean? Is this a violation?**
</div>

Hi everyone, 

I'm confused with the definition of LoD as the textbook doesn't have an example for it but it does say methods from objects created/instantiated directly/indirectly can be invoked.

Does indirectly specifically mean this is allowed? Or what does indirectly mean?

'''java
void foo(B b) ``{``
    A a = b.initA(); // assume this method only initializes A and returns it and does nothing else
    a.doSomething();
``}``
'''

Related part of the [textbook](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/se-book-adapted/chapters/principles.html#law-of-demeter):

<img width="1011" height="186" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7636162f-4465-4b7c-9c4e-ecfd7a680c25" /> 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/565" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Clarifications on pdf friendly constraints for User Guide**
</div>

Hi everyone, 

My team and I wanted to check if using expandable panels and tabs are allowed on the user guide as long as the contents are all expanded and viewable in the pdf version? 

This is referring to the pdf friendly constraint https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/tp-constraints.html#constraint-pdf-friendly
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/564" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Capitalization in Git Commit Messages**
</div>

Hi everyone,

I realised the Git conventions specifies examples such as `chore: Update release date` https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/git.html

Could I check if `chore: update release date` would be following the Git conventions?

May be related to https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/64 but specifically, 'update' isn't capitalized I'm not sure if that's okay since it is also mentioned that we have to capitalize the first letter of subject line. 


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/213" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6. :fas-info-circle: Clarifications on Magic Literals in JUnit Tests**
</div>

Hi everyone, I'm curious about the use of magic literals in JUnit test code. From a code quality perspective, is it generally considered bad practice to include magic literals directly in tests, or is it acceptable in certain scenarios?

In particular, for text-heavy applications like ours, many tests naturally involve a lot of string values especially those that are only used once. At what point does converting all these literals into constants actually make the test code less readable or harder to maintain? 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/45#issuecomment-3790175376" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

To add on, it may be because it follows topological ordering across multiple branches and git has multiple starting points to walk backwards from. 

https://git-scm.com/docs/git-log
--all
Pretend as if all the refs in refs/, along with HEAD, are listed on the command line as the commit.

This may provide more context https://stackoverflow.com/questions/60283861/git-commits-log-information-in-a-wrong-order
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/92#issuecomment-3814675684" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with the above on steps to make it more user friendly. Adding on, you mentioned corrupted with invalid or wrong entries. If the file contains invalid or wrong entries (but is still readable), the application could attempt to skip or recover valid entries and reinitialise the file using those. I think we can also have stronger validation before persisting entries too.

However, if we're talking about truly corrupted files (e.g. cannot be opened or parsed at all), recovery may not be feasible for a plain text file, and reinitialising a new file with a clear error message might be more reasonable. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/84#issuecomment-3814701176" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I also agree that it's meant to be for colon used in switch statements, referring to `case x:` instead.

Normally, we keep the operators separated with whitespace on both sides from other variables/operators but in the case it should be attached to the value instead.

'''java
switch (value) ``{``
    case x: // colon attached to x instead of case x :
        // some code here
        break;
``}``
''' 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815166134" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think its a good idea to default to non-static classes, some classes need state (use non-static) while others don't (use static).

Storage class would be better as a non-static class since the location of the file is a state that relates to an instance of the Storage class.

If it were static, can we create another instance of Storage class for a file in another location? 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815179325" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

A clearer example for what I mentioned, let's take Storage vs Parser.

'''java
Storage mainStorage = new Storage("duke.txt");
Storage backupStorage = new Storage("backup.txt"); // notice this would not be possible if Storage were static

mainStorage.save(tasks);       // writes to duke.txt
backupStorage.save(tasks);     // writes to backup.txt
'''

And for Parser we have something like this.

'''java
Parser.parse("todo read book");
Parser.parse("deadline submit report /by 2026-01-30");
'''

Since Parser holds no state, it's suitable to be written as a static class.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/94#issuecomment-3815760893" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

From your screenshot, it seems that there's no gradle files eg. `build.gradle` and the error also tells us that the project was not initialised as a Gradle project.

Could you check if you have merged the `add-graddle-support` branch into master? And are you currently in master after the merge is done?

This was written on the website for reference
<img width="1057" height="295" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c01f1f0d-3ee1-4dd8-8e17-e3472a3ac434" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96#issuecomment-3817753093" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Personally, I used the Storage class within TaskList by passing a Storage instance when creating a TaskList.

This way, TaskList remains the abstraction responsible for managing tasks, while Storage is only concerned with persistence (reading/writing to disk). The underlying data structure (e.g. ArrayList&lt;Task>) stays hidden inside TaskList, which improves encapsulation and reduces coupling, still some degree of coupling though.

I think your approach works well too. By returning a TaskList instead of a generic collection, you’re reinforcing abstraction and keeping implementation details hidden. Though as the above comments mention, there might be issues if TaskList methods change.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/107#issuecomment-3827268129" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/109#issuecomment-3827863965" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I would think it's still better to use imports without wildcards. In the event that we update the code to not use all classes in the package in the future, we might not realise that.

If it were imports without wildcards, our IDE would be able to tell that we have unused imports. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/116#issuecomment-3828145258" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Personally, I think I would use saveTransaction() making it start with is/has makes it seem like it doesn't mutate/change attributes/state.

I think it's common to return true/false or some integer to show success so it wouldn't need to be in the name or at least it is how its written in some database libraries, but I do think also trySaveTransaction works or maybe returning an integer works too? 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/117#issuecomment-3830145485" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It seems the reason is because of the different commit histories, I looked into your commit history and it seems you do not have the commits from the ip repo. Could I ask how you forked the repository? Did you potentially clear all commits, didn't fetch the commits or did a force push initially? 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/117#issuecomment-3830373371" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The force push likely did an overwrite of all the previous commits hence the different commit histories. There were likely merge commits preventing the push initially that you had to resolve.

You could check out `git reflog` to retrieve the previous commit before your force push assuming git garbage collector hasn't removed it. Would be a good point to create a new branch with your current commits before you reset to the point before your force push. Then, merge it back into master.

I'm not sure if this is recommended for this course, specifically whether it might change the timestamps of your commit. 
</panel>

Adding on, I also did something similar to the suggestions above essentially to use `Platform.exit()` in the `MainWindow.java` file. Agree with the suggestions above! You may want to add a Response class to handle the inputs and exits separately

'''java
if (resp.isExitRequested()) ``{`` // i used a Response class to handle input and exits
    Platform.runLater(() -&gt; ``{``
        PauseTransition delay = new PauseTransition(Duration.millis(500)); // to allow time to view the response
        delay.setOnFinished(evt -&gt; Platform.exit());
        delay.play();
    ``}``);
``}``
'''
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**32 :fas-comment:**

Adding on, I think it doesn't violate SLAP because all of the logical parts of the code have been abstracted into separate methods at the same level of abstraction which is valid. 

I agree with @QR-Phua and @minwaiphyo and as long as we have everything at the same level of abstraction it would not violate SLAP so if **all** 3 of the methods you called were not abstracted out (eg. low level) it would still not violate SLAP.

Adding on to the discussion,

1. I agree with the comments above that System.out.println is not meaningful to be added to a sequence diagram. A sequence diagram should show interactions between entites in the system. We could show :Ui object interactions instead of those if it's essential as @NigelGM mentioned
2. If the sequence diagram doesn't miss too much information I would think it's also okay to omit the return arrow since printing something isn't crucial to understanding how entities interact with each other.

How would self creation work? An object that has already been created creating the same instance if itself? If we're talking about a class having a method to create another instance of the same class we shoukd be able to show it as a separate entity.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/525#issuecomment-4044035077" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**54 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Adding on, I'm also not completely sure but here's my understanding

I agree with @andrewsoonqn, that an association means a long term link, eg. A holds a reference to B or vice versa or both and an aggregation is a container-containee relationship which only defines that eg. Category contains Books.

For composition, Prof Damith mentioned "A book can exist without chapters (if it is not divided into chapters) but a chapter cannot exist without belonging to a book." https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/520 I believe composition only refers to the part-of relation and not cascading deletion. Whole can exist without a part, part cannot exist without belonging to a whole, and there's no cyclical links whole cannot be a part of its own part.

For the difference between association and composition/aggregation, I would think it's better to show composition when it's known/asked for because it shows more than just association.

For the difference in implementation of composition and aggregation, it was mentioned in the Textbook that the distinction between them in UML diagrams is sometimes ambiguous, so I would think that there may not be a clear difference in implementation too. eg. Category contains book aggregation (could be implemented as a list of books), and book is composed of chapters (could also be implemented as a list of chapters). There could be some difference with validation to ensure chapter doesn't exist without a book though. 

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/514#issuecomment-4044247266" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**55 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with the points mentioned above! Acceptance testing is always still needed with system testing, to ensure that it is what our users actually want.

System testing only tells us if we have satisfied system specification and not our user requirements. I think its also important to note that Acceptance testing is also done on deployed/close to deployed environments which may also be quite different from development or staging environments.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/505#issuecomment-4044302189" expanded>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/505#issuecomment-4044319363" expanded>

**57 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/534#issuecomment-4054215464" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**58 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think its a great point when a task belongs to multiple projects, composition doesn't seem to describe the scenario properly. 

I'm not quite sure if composition implies exclusive ownership, though I would think so since it's described as a whole-part relationship. If that's the case, I would think that aggregation may be more suitable here (though that could also imply that task can exist without being assigned to a project, which may still be valid given that the code does not show validation in that aspect). 

However, we also aren't told that a task can belong to multiple projects/a task can exist without being assigned to a project so I would think composition should still be used. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/547#issuecomment-4067538327" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**59 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Agree with the comments above! I would think this example is readable and easy to understand.

I would only consider splitting the stream into multiple steps if the logic represents distinct stages that benefit from being separated, or if the chain becomes long enough that readability starts to suffer. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/546#issuecomment-4067583369" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**60 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I would think freezing the sprint backlog helps make each iteration effective. Sprints aren't usually too long and keeping changes between sprints rather than during them would help make the scope of each sprint more stable, meaning the team gets a more focused window to build, test and learn from the increment.

If requirements kept changing mid-sprint, I would think there's a possibility the team might end up partially working on multiple things without finishing usable components.

I would think depth first iteration could be more effective for complex core components, though I'm not entirely sure, would be interested to hear everyone's experiences.
</panel>

**68 :fas-comment:**

Thank you! I don't quite understand directly/indirectly in the context of instantiation. Does "Objects created instantiated in m (directly or indirectly)" then mean this?

directly: `A a = new A();`
indirectly: `A a = b.initA();  // returns instance variable a`

I believe the first doesn't violate LoD, but the second isn't so clear to me? Since `b.getA().doSomething();` violates LoD, I would think that `b.initA().doSomething();` where initA only initializes and returns A also violates LoD but that seems to conflict with how "Objects created instantiated in m (directly or indirectly)" are allowed?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/733#issuecomment-4317887068" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**69 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think that gray-box testing as you said is specification-based, with some knowledge of implementation.

The example in the [textbook](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/se-book-adapted/chapters/testCaseDesign.html#black-box-vs-glass-box) may help
> Gray-box approach: test case design uses some important information about the implementation. For example, if the implementation of a sort operation uses different algorithms to sort lists shorter than 1000 items and lists longer than 1000 items, more meaningful test cases can then be added to verify the correctness of both algorithms.
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**72 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think this isn't that

>print() be called by Quote

it's rather that we are calling `print()` on the `Quote` object from an unknown caller object, as `m.print();` means we call `print()` on the quote object `m`. Hence, we have the lifeline on the left calling `print()` on the `Quote` object. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/248" expanded>

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/breezuu/ip/releases/tag/A-Release
* Which OSes to test on: Windows | Mac | Linux
* Things to check specifically:
1. App works on Java 17 (and above)
2. All commands work as intended in the User Guide
3. Error messages correctly reflect the invalid command entered
4. Any other quality-of-life (QoL) issues, such as GUI window resizing issues
5. Tasks can be saved and displayed properly after re-opening the application (_data persistence_)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing
* Link to User Guide: https://breezuu.github.io/ip/

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you, everyone 😄 
</panel>

**7 :fas-comment:**

<img width="898" height="166" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9741868b-0e51-4fcc-8b12-b18e209786e5" />

Are the following files similar to the ones in your fork of `gitmastery-progress`?

For me, I am using Git for Windows (**_v2.52.0_**) with an SSH key. My version of GitHub CLI is **_v2.85.0_** (_windows_amd64_). Not sure if they could have affected the outcome.

If not, the only remaining potential issues I can think of are:

1. `gitmastery.exe` not added to Windows System Variable `PATH`
2. Folder used is controlled by OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, etc.

If you still run into issues, you could try renaming the directory with issues to `gitmastery-exercises-old`, and then creating a new directory called `gitmastery-exercises` (which is the default folder name used during setup) to run `gitmastery setup` and attempt to sync your progress again. 

Hope this fixes your issue!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/57#issuecomment-3794226708" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Windows by default uses backslashes `\`, which can be seen when you open up Command Prompt/PowerShell.

However, if you were to use a Macbook, its Terminal uses forward slashes `/` by default.

Step 1 in the 'Setting Up' section likely assumes you are using a Windows machine, thus using a backslash `\` instead of a forward slash `/`.

It is also why there is both `runtest.bat` and `runtest.sh` available for you to run tests. The `.bat` file is used by those in Windows environments, while the `.sh` file is used by those in Unix environments _(e.g. Terminal on MacBooks)_.
</panel>

</div>

Hi, there is a similar concern brought up in [this link on Git conventions](https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/110) which you can check out. The link on Git conventions should give you an idea on how the message in your last 5 commits should look like.

Commit #4 below flouts the Git conventions because the message is more than the hard limit of 72 characters. That could be why you are failing to meet the Git Standard increment.

_4. Create a new type of command "find" to give users a way to find a task by searching for a keyword in the task description_

Hope this helps!

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/17cb7005-7be7-4c0c-8196-16b324539bf9)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/141#issuecomment-3851091142" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @b2-4ac, is using a newer version of Gradle a solution you might want to consider?

You can configure Gradle to use a newer version which supports Java 21 (_major version 65_), which can hopefully circumvent this issue of `Unsupported class file major version 65`.

To do this, you can head to `gradle/wrapper/gradle-wrapper.properties` and edit accordingly/as required, with the image below as a reference:

<img width="659" height="179" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3adce6f3-70e1-424f-9be8-908e0233aa01" />

I am currently using Gradle 8.5, which has helped me to get rid of this Java version incompatibility issue.

Synchronize your Gradle with the new changes made, or run `.\gradlew clean build` and `.\gradlew clean run` to see if your program runs now, while forcing Gradle to install the new version.

Hope this fixes your issue!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/160#issuecomment-3857719372" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, perhaps you could do something like this in the `handleUserInput()` method in your `MainWindow.java` file?

```
    @FXML
    private void handleUserInput() ``{``
        // ... other code in handleUserInput()

        userInput.clear();

        if (input.equalsIgnoreCase("bye")) ``{``
            PauseTransition delay = new PauseTransition(Duration.seconds(1.5));

            delay.setOnFinished(event -&gt; Platform.exit());

            delay.play();
        ``}``
    ``}``
```

When the command `bye` is typed, the window will stay for 1.5 seconds before closing.

Hope this helps!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/165#issuecomment-3858902610" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @gwhrg, 

Have you tried adding a browser-specific (_e.g. Safari/Chrome_) keyboard shortcut to override the default shortcuts set by the website? 

I personally use a Windows machine, but there should be a Mac-equivalent way to set keyboard shortcuts on Safari/Chrome, depending on the browser you are using.

I did a Google search and the results seem to point towards heading to `System Settings > Keyboard > Keyboard Shortcuts > App Shortcuts`. You should then be able to add an application-specific keyboard shortcut for the `Find...` function.

<img width="714" height="594" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0dcac676-f2f8-4fb3-8c14-4d49856a23b1" />

Perhaps [this link from Apple's support website](https://support.apple.com/en-gb/guide/mac-help/mchlp2271/mac) could help you to find a workaround for your issue?

Hope this helps!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/172#issuecomment-3864067846" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

 > and causes the program (gui part) to not open

Hi @bingmybongg, are there any other issues brought up by your terminal when running Gradle on your program, besides the 'unnamed module' line?

Could you also check if any issues on the command line/terminal appear after you run the command `.\gradlew shadowJar`?

Since your JDK and JavaFX versions are 17, I would pinpoint the possible issue to either the configuration file `build.gradle`, or maybe you _might_ have missed out on something from the JavaFX tutorial while editing your program files.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/172#issuecomment-3864089745" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> So i can do ./gradlew run now but when i do the shadowJar and try to do java -jar , this error appears 


When you do `.\gradlew run`, does the GUI appear perfectly without any issues? Does anything else appear in the command line which could potentially pinpoint the issue?

Just to make sure that everything is working before the JAR file is created, try running `.\gradlew clean run` instead, and verify that the GUI still displays properly for you.
</panel>

**40 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @synh, upon closer inspection of your code, your MainWindow.java is pointing to non-existent images.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7635073c-388c-4396-ba18-9ebec5c89a47)

The error thrown by the program in the screenshot above shows that Line 35 of your `MainWindow.java` code is having issues.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b36f0c4b-7f53-4907-b8d9-80bc292efa5b)

You have referred to images with the filenames as `user.jpg` and `sage.jpg`, but the image files in `src\main\resources\images` reflect otherwise.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2802149f-b947-420d-8e00-775c6581be5a)

Try changing the filename of the images in `MainWindow.java` accordingly and create your JAR file again.

Hope this fixes your issue! 😃 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/381#issuecomment-3933271774" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**41 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Thanks so much guys! It turns out that my changes when I swapped the files didn't save in git because I deleted and added the files externally in file explorer. Does this mean I should never do this and use git bash for all file changes?

If I understand your concern correctly, you made changes to the files via File Explorer, and that caused your modified files to not appear in the list of items to be changed when you ran `git status`. Is that right?

Personally, I have yet to run into such an issue, but I always run `git status` before running `git add .` via Git Bash to ensure the changes I want to commit appear as expected.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/757" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Exam Readiness Quiz gives wrong Course Website link**
</div>

In the first question, the link given is wrong. It links to last semester's guidelines which have subtle changes from this semesters (e.g. no need to bring in A4 paper for this semesters exam).

<img width="1262" height="323" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7e864aa6-bb39-4b56-b577-4e4f2cace14d" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/607" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: v1.5 Jar Release Deadline**
</div>

On the dashboard, there is a task for releasing v1.5 JAR by **Week 11**

Is this accurate, or not considering that the v1.5 deadline is said to be extended by one week 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/249" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/Omgeta/ip/releases/tag/A-Release
* Link to User Guide: https://omgeta.github.io/ip/
* Which OS'es to test on: Windows | Mac | Linux
* Things to check specifically: 
1. Commands work as specified in the user guide
2. GUI works as expected and with good UX
3. Data persistence through file system
4. Errors and edge case bugs
5. Issues with personality/phrasing (especially for errors)


Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/87" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Similarity of CheckStyle to 2103 Coding Standard**
</div>

Does anyone know how similar the CheckStyle requirements are to the actual coding standard used by the course?

In Week 4 Project Section, the below is mentioned:
> In some areas, the sample Checkstyle rules file ([recommended by the SE-EDU guide](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/checkstyle.html)) may be slightly more permissive/restrictive than (but not contradictory to) the given Java coding standard. You are free to tweak the Checkstyle file if you wish. It is fine as long as your code is compliant with the given coding standard.

However, these differences aren't specified anywhere (afaik), and I want to make sure that my Checkstyle file is atleast as restrictive as the coding standard (at least in terms of the more objective metrics such as indentation, formatting, etc) so that I don't run into any trouble later on.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/63#issuecomment-3794815492" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yep, facing the same issue on my end. Might just be a temporary thing though
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/73#issuecomment-3799998318" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

If your Storage class is written properly, you should be able quite easily to achieve both cases. I would say start with writing the Storage class to be independent so that you can call save() wherever you need.

Trivially, you can achieve case 1 by just performing the Storage.save() on every operation (or only on write operation to reduce disk writes). For case 2, you just perform Storage.save() on the bye command. If you wanted, you could just do both and personally, that seems like the best choice to me. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/35#issuecomment-3800039078" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As others have already mentioned, IntelliJ already allows easy swapping between JDKs, so you can just rely on that for your IntelliJ projects.

For your personal projects, you could write your own tool or use https://sdkman.io/
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/86#issuecomment-3805958155" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You should be able to just ignore it and move on, since there is no mentioned requirement for runtest.sh modification. I didn't make any changes either, since I sort of guessed that new features would keep coming in that would need to be tested in a full test.

Sidenote: technically, it shouldn't be ignored even after the JUnit integration, since it seems to me that runtest.sh is meant to be a full end-to-end test, while JUnit is only for unit testing of decoupled components.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/92#issuecomment-3815484344" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think you could feasibly write a more user-friendly handler to skip over corrupted entries and recover by asking the user to fix it etc., but since it wasn't specified, I just went for the easier solution to output an error and just reinitalize the list. At the end of the day, it's up to you, and I didn't think the chance of corruption was high enough or important enough to warrant writing a more complicated handler for a toy project like this.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96#issuecomment-3817712766" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Personally, I decoupled `TaskList` and `Storage` so that errors in one wouldn't affect the other (i.e. in this case, errors with the TaskList wouldn't affect Storage). I believe this is generally best practice since it separates the two classes as much as possible and lets us treat the two classes independently.

I think your rationale works fine for the current implementation where `TaskList` is essentially just an `ArrayList` wrapper, but if we did later want to swap out the underlying implementation or interface of `TaskList`, then changes would have to be made to `Storage` as well.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/87#issuecomment-3817725913" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Closing, but maybe reopen in future if anyone wants to document the actual differences between checkstyle.xml and the coding standard (even if it doesn't affect grading).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/106#issuecomment-3824386775" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It's mentioned in the [admin info](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/participation.html) that you need to submit it within 4 days of the briefing (i.e. by Tuesday) to get the 3rd point. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/103#issuecomment-3824420950" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/136#issuecomment-3840265891" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I used 11.0.1 since I assumed the patch version wouldn't affect much, and it works fine so far
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/174#issuecomment-3866637600" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Adding onto what the others have already mentioned, you could possibly have different `Validator`s for different purposes to help split the validation logic across different parts of the code that need validation. But yeah, generally I would choose Option 1 rather than have a decentralized system where unexpected inconsistency/bugs could occur.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/168#issuecomment-3866663198" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You can see the iP grading criteria [here](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/ip-grading.html) on the course website (Week 6 technically). But yes, the published grading criteria include acceptable coding standards for the 10 implementation marks.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/139#issuecomment-3866723492" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Since we're working with Java, it's probably better to just go full OOP and apply the Strategy pattern as @andrewsoonqn mentioned. Since you already have a Parser class, it might be better to think of it as an InputParser which just has a single public `.parse()` method returning a parsed Command class. In fact, this is exactly the pattern used in the example code in [Week 3 A-MoreOOP](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week3/project.html#4-add-increments-a-moreoop-a-packages-a-gradle-a-junit-a-jar). 

As for the argument of making the file system hard to navigate, I think it's subjective, but Java is already messy enough so my opinion is for most non-trivial projects to just embrace it and plan code in a "OOP" way which lends itself better to future extension.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/154#issuecomment-3866852605" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**19 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I used to overuse `static` because I wanted a lot of "pure" functions, but I realised state kept getting introduced to classes and I would have to refactor so I think it's (generally) much better to just default to non-static, unless you know you're working with some stateless utility class, constants or parsers/helpers. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/181#issuecomment-3873247054" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**20 :fas-comment:**
</div>

There's a difference between performance and readability. Here, the original code chooses (hand-written) performance using a single for-loop, while the new code chooses readability. It's also possible for the compiler to later optimize readable code anyway. But generally, since this course doesn't focus on performance, it might be a better idea to focus on readable code, so it can be easily read and maintained.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3876205037" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**21 :fas-comment:**
</div>

They're amazing for automating JavaDoc and inline comments (with supervision of course). For JavaDoc particularly, I usually write a few fully hand-written comments following the coding standard requirements, then let Copilot fill in the rest automatically to above repetition in similar methods/classes. Generally, it works out quite well, especially when methods use SLAP well, but for longer methods a manual hand-written comment may still be required
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177#issuecomment-3876295924" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**22 :fas-comment:**
</div>

@Joulenergy's answer is really good.

In my own use case, I write assertions when i'm writing a method to check for invariants, preconditions and postconditions for programming errors (generally private methods).

This helps me to avoid focus on the primary goal of the method first. As @song-yiyang mentioned, you can always remove these asserts in prod once you are sure the invariants are maintained. 

Meanwhile, throwing Exceptions are generally used in my code for user-input handling so I can catch it somewhere else and display to the user.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/179#issuecomment-3878974414" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**23 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As the others have already mentioned, your issue seems to be from making a function that tries to do everything.

You may need to:
- Let Parser.parse return a Command object instead of a String
  - Your Command object may need an `execute()` or equivalent method defined on it
  - Your Command object could store necessary fields (e.g. `taskNum`) as a private field at construction time
- Call Command.execute() and return the String response (if you choose to keep this design instead of using a separate UI class)
- Handle any errors thrown (if any) with some error message

With these changes, your `getResponse()` will get reduced to just a few lines. If you want, you can apply wishful thinking/top-down approach and make these changes first, then start working on the dependent classes/methods.
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**33 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the moment you ask about real projects, the answer is just "it depends".

You wouldn't really want to spend a lot of time (and company money) to overengineer something to be loosely coupled if you just wanted to iterate fast and ship.  There might also be cases where tighter coupling gives you more performance, which might be better for certain (rare) applications even if it's not "good practice". But obviously, if you can get away with fast decoupling early on in the design, then that's going to be very valuable in the long-term.
</panel>

The priority should be for the reader to understand the flow and expected *visible* behaviour, and not on the implementation details or the minutae which just slow the reader down.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/687" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Clarification regarding the use of `response.IssueUnclear` label**
</div>

Regarding Phase 2 of the PE, suppose my team receives a bug report that only contains a title and a single screenshot. There is no justification provided for the title, and the screenshot is unrelated to the title.

Based on the instructions provided [here](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/tp-pe.html#pe-phase-2-developer-response), we can use the `response.IssueUnclear` label.

<img width="1404" height="144" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/40b588bf-7a91-4c91-a5f9-a1f01d022a25" />

However, what are the possible implications if we apply this label? Do we still need to justify our response for the bug report based on either the screenshot/title provided?

Thank you!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/544" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Attribution of AI Generated Image For tP App Icon**
</div>

Hi, I would like to ask how we should give credit for AI generated images. I have checked the &lt;a href = "https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/appendixB-policies.html#giving-credit-for-reused-work">course policies&lt;/a>, and this seems to be a somewhat-grey area.

It seems that for reused images, we can credit the source at the location where the GUI screenshot appears in the UG. On the other hand, for AI-generated code, we can cite the usage under the Acknowledgements section of the DG.

In our group's case, if we follow the instructions for reused images, it would result in poorer readability which can confuse new users, especially when our GUI screenshot is located in the middle of the Quick Start section.

As such, I would like to know if citing the usage of the AI generated image in the Acknowledgements section of the DG, as well as README.md, is sufficient. Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/202" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* **Link to JAR file:** &lt;a href = "https://github.com/DenseLance-alt/ip/releases/tag/A-Release">here&lt;/a>
* **User Guide:** &lt;a href = "https://denselance-alt.github.io/ip/">here&lt;/a>
* **Which OS'es to test on:** Mac | Linux

**Things to check specifically:** 
* Check if program can be executed normally using `java -jar chatbot.jar`.
* Check if the following test cases work.

| Test Case | Command | Expected Output | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `list` | `You have no tasks!` |  |
| 2 | `deadline return book /by 28/2/2026 18:00` | Task should be added successfully to task list. |  |
| 3 | `deadline homework /by 29/2/2026 23:59` | `INVALID PARAMETER - Datetime provided is invalid.` | There is no 29 February this year. |
| 4 | `event meet with friends /from 20/2/2026 11:00 /to 20/2/2026 13:00` | Task should be added successfully to task list. |  |
| 5 | Run the command `sort`, followed by the command `list` | `meeting with friends` should come before `return book` in the task list. | Tasks are sorted in chronological order. |
| 6 | `bye` | Program closes automatically after ~2 seconds. | |
| 7 | Run the program again and check whether task list is retained with the command `list`. | Task list should be restored with the 2 tasks. | |

**Guide for testers:**
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests (possible to combine 2-3 testcases into a single screenshot)
* Mention which OS you used for testing

Thank you for the help!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/11#issuecomment-3762545361" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> `Integer.parseInt` already throws `NumberFormatException` and attempting to access an invalid index/element in the dimensions array would cause `IndexOutOfBoundsException` to be thrown. Hence our method does not need to throw either of these unchecked exceptions here. Would this be correct?

From &lt;a href = "https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week1/topics.html#c-to-java-exceptions-how-to-use-exceptions"> the notes&lt;/a>, it mentions that it is optional to follow the Catch or Specify Requirement for unchecked exceptions. So we don't actually need to specify a `throws` clause for `NumberFormatException` and `IndexOutOfBoundsException` in this case, and your code will still compile. In general, we don't catch or specify an unchecked exception because it usually cannot be anticipated and recovered from.

In my opinion, I would say that specifying a `throws` clause for unchecked exception is more of a design choice. For example, your method `printArea` is already anticipating that an invalid user input would cause the method to fail, and it uses `try-catch` to recover from it. That means `NumberFormatException` and `IndexOutOfBoundsException` behave more similarly to a checked exception rather than an unchecked exception in this scenario. You could specify the `throws` for `calculateArea` as an indication for other programmers to be prepared for this failure, but the downside would be that it &lt;a href= "https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/essential/exceptions/runtime.html">reduces clarity&lt;/a>. If you defined a custom exception that is anticipated and can recovered from, then you should convert it into a checked exception instead.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/11#issuecomment-3762835942" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Another way to motivate the "non-need" to throw and catch unchecked exceptions comes from CS2030S, in the notes here: https://nus-cs2030s.github.io/2425-s1/22-exception.html#unchecked-exceptions
> 
> Unchecked errors like `IndexOutOfBoundsException` should never occur if the perfect programmer checks all indices before retrieving elements from that index, but no programmer can anticipate, at the time of coding, if a certain file (whose filename is given by a user) will be found on disk. Hence, checked exceptions such as `FileNotFoundException` have to be handled, by using exception handling code.
> 
> Hence, to answer your question about throwing and checking unchecked exceptions, if I were to write code to throw/catch unchecked exceptions, I would question why that exception would arise in the first place and add if-guards (e.g. checking if a variable is non-null before unpacking it).
> 
> Just my two cents :)

I agree with this point. It's generally good programming practice to use `if-else` as opposed to `try-catch`for better clarity and performance, but in this case we would just need to follow the exercise instructions (to catch the 2 unchecked exceptions). Also, we should not use exceptions as a control flow mechanism, as stated in the CS2030S notes (just use `if-else`). 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/19#issuecomment-3766456798" expanded>
