<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/31#issuecomment-3783871429" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I would like to recommend the `--oneline` flag for `git log`, then just check if the first few values of the SHA hash match. This should still show the commit message's subject (which is needed here).

You can also do git `--no-pager` log to print out the entire commit log without using the pager.

**Note:** You should try to do `gitmastery reset progress` first just in case the SHA values were modified by something, for example a `git rebase`.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/45#issuecomment-3790164512" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, you may see the following page: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-log#_commit_ordering

The ordering should be in chronological order like what @QR-Phua mentioned. If you are unsure if it is in the correct order, try using the `--date-order` flag.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/60#issuecomment-3794526847" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> For IP Level-5, are we supposed to terminate the program after an error occured (by throwing exception), or merely display an error message but continue running the program?

I believe that you should display the error message then ask the user for the next command.

> Also, I am confused about how a custom DukeException class would be useful. Right now I am making invalid user inputs throw a DukeException, but I am not sure how that would be different from just throwing a regular exception.

There's quite a few reasons why a command would be invalid, like an invalid parameter or the wrong flag (e.g. `/by`) was used. You can split these different errors into separate exceptions so that they return different meaningful error messages. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/92#issuecomment-3814545487" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

From my understanding, the design should be up to you (i.e. whether the chatbot prints an error message or continues to run when the saved file is corrupted).

However, it is likely that a file that is corrupted will remain corrupted as the user would not be able to fix it manually. Therefore reinitializing a new saved file makes more sense in this case. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/99#issuecomment-3821203500" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

A true getter would mean something like this:

'''java
Item getItem() ``{``
    return this.item;
``}``
'''
So if your getter deviates from this behaviour, it is likely that you want to include a JavaDoc since the user may misunderstand what it is meant to do. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/108#issuecomment-3827607131" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Spaces should be used instead of tabs. 
> Indentation for wrapped lines should be 8 spaces (i.e. twice the normal indentation of 4 spaces) more than the parent line.

This is supposing that your Q16 is this question:
> When wrapping a long statement (an example given below), one should indent lines using two tabs instead of the usual one tab.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/125#issuecomment-3830949938" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I believe the stereotype `&lt;&lt;create>>` in this case is to just help the user to understand what the dependency means in that particular context. You can remove stereotypes from your UML diagram and the relationship will still be valid, with the exception of `&lt;&lt;interface>>` and `&lt;&lt;enumeration>>`.

Labels are used mainly for associations if I'm not mistaken, and they define the meaning of the association. Labels should not be removed if we want to differentiate between objects in object diagrams. In class diagrams, they should be kept if the user must know what is the exact relationship between two classes.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/125#issuecomment-3832433089" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>


> Would my understanding below be correct: 
> **Dependency:** Able to use only stereotypes
> **Association:** Able to use either labels or stereotypes

From my understanding, this sounds correct.

> When to use:
>     * Use Stereotype to define custom elements (e.g. when there are multiple places we would like to indicate the stereotype `&lt;&lt;create>>` such that it could be repeated several times)
>     * Use labels to add information to element (may not be repetitive)

Kind of true, with some caveats. Both can add info to a specific association, but stereotypes _identify_ the "type" of association (that is usually optional) and labels are more specific in _explaining_ the relationship between 2 classes (that is usually required).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/203#issuecomment-3902093055" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**22 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on my understanding, magic literals exist because it is not well explained and seemingly appears out of thin air. By using `enum`, you associate the literal to the enumeration value. This describes what the literal means, making it no longer a magic literal. In fact, using `enum` might be better in certain cases, especially when magic literals can be logically grouped together as a set.

However, I would not name the enumeration class as `Command`, but something like `ValidCommandRegex` instead which would tell us what these strings are used for.


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/504#issuecomment-4016308225" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**36 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi there, regarding your questions:

1. [Class Diagram] Keywords such as ```{``abstract``}``` are usually appended to the back, like `methodName(): returnType ``{``abstract``}```.
2. [Class Diagram] Like what @archonward mentions, consider a chain of tasks. The first task does not have a previous task, so it uses `previous = null` (as such, each task can have either 0 or 1 previous task). Generally speaking, `0..1` is used unless there is a comment that says "each object must have this component".
3. [Object Diagram] <a href = "https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/se-book-adapted/chapters/oop.html#navigability">Note that two unidirectional associations in opposite directions do not add up to a single bidirectional association</a>. The object diagram will no longer match the class diagram if you merge the two unidirectional associations into one bidirectional association.
4. [Class/Object Diagram] There is a way to draw such an association, as seen <a href = "https://stackoverflow.com/a/69419997">here</a> and <a href = "https://stackoverflow.com/a/47864609">here</a>, although I'm not sure if this is correct for this course. They suggest the following:

<img width="386" height="61" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e69e32f6-e9b5-49af-9082-2361a01d5bb6" />

5. [Overall] I do agree that it can sometimes be subjective when we are trying to show composition/association multiplicity. What I think might work would be to check whether a comment/sentence has described the details of an association explicitly. For example, "All messages have a header" implies that header is a part and message is a whole, and the multiplicity for the header's side is 1.
6. [Sequence Diagram] For anyone visiting this post, do correct me if I'm wrong. While the diagram that you have drawn looks correct logically, it does not model the actual sequence of steps taken, since based on the code given, the return statements are within the if-else block rather than outside it.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/560#issuecomment-4095412325" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**41 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The &lt;a href = "https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week4/topics.html#tools-uml-stereotypes">notes&lt;/a> describe the following:

> UML stereotypes are a lightweight extension mechanism for adding domain-specific meaning to existing UML elements without changing the core UML language. They label elements such as classes, objects, or relationships with roles or semantics beyond their default interpretation (for example, `&lt;&lt;entity>>, &lt;&lt;create>>, &lt;&lt;facade>>`), helping readers understand intent, architectural responsibilities, or usage patterns at a glance. Stereotypes do not change the fundamental structure of the model; instead, they enrich it with contextual meaning, often forming part of a UML profile tailored to a particular problem domain.

UML stereotypes are merely contextual labels for existing UML elements and should not change the core UML language (class name is defined as part of the UML language). You'll also need the actual class names to identify what that entity is.

You can also see the &lt;a href = "https://www.uml-diagrams.org/stereotype.html">UML specifications for stereotypes&lt;/a>, which says the following:

> A stereotype cannot be used by itself. ... The name of the applied stereotype is shown within a pair of guillemets above or before the name of the model element.
---
**EDIT:** The above is referring to the UML diagrams that we usually work with. From the perspective of drawing diagrams for design patterns, it should be OK to use such notations in the solution since we want to provide a general solution/sketch instead of specific names dependent on context (e.g. `Invoker` instead of `CommandQueue`). As mentioned in the notes:

> It is important to note that the solution presented only includes the most general details, which may need further refinement for a specific context.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/549#issuecomment-4095463817" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**42 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, there are several ways you can use to circumvent this issue.

The first method is pretty obvious, which is to create test cases. For example, in your case, we can have a `SampleDataUtilTest` class that tests the sample address book. Alternatively, you can consider adding a sample JSON that matches your sample address book, and test it in `JsonSerializableAddressBookTest`.

The second method is proposed in #529, where you can add a `codecov.yml` config to &lt;a href = "https://docs.codecov.com/docs/ignoring-paths">ignore specific directories&lt;/a>. For example, in your case, you can add the following and store the YAML file in the root of the repository.
'''text
ignore:
  - "src/main/java/seedu/address/model/util/SampleDataUtil.java"
'''

You can choose the delete the YAML file in another PR after you are done.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/583#issuecomment-4126704987" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**43 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, these are some interesting approaches that you have come up with! Definitely (1) is the easiest to implement out of the 2, although I'm not sure what you mean by "The confirmation stage can also be skipped by typing `confirm clear` directly". For (2), you can refer to the &lt;a href = https://se-education.org/addressbook-level4/DeveloperGuide.html#undo-redo-feature>`undo`/`redo` feature in AB-4&lt;/a> for implementing states, so it's not really a big departure in changes.

---

However, I would just like to mention that in the &lt;a href = "https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/tp-constraints.html#recommendation-cli-first">tP constraints&lt;/a>, it is mentions the following:
> One-shot commands are faster over multi-step commands. If you provide a multistep command to help new users, it is recommended that you also provide a one-shot equivalent for regular/expert users.

> Multistep-command approach refers to giving the user a series of prompts to enter various data elements required to perform an action.

Both approaches, from what I see, involve multi-step commands to clear the address book, which may violate the tP constraints. I think there are better alternatives to make `clear` "less destructive":
1. The trivial solution - just put a warning text box in the UG which tells user that this is a destructive command that is **irreversible**.
2. Implement an `archive` command - ask the user save a backup before proceeding. This does not violate the multistep constraint since keying in the `archive` command before `clear` is optional.
3. Implement the `undo`/`redo` commands used in AB-4.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/583#issuecomment-4132606782" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**44 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Ah I see what you mean! Based on what I've seen, there were some groups in previous batches that asks the user to type in another command format (e.g. `confirm clear`) instead of `clear` to wipe all data. For example, &lt;a href = https://ay2526s1-cs2103t-w09-2.github.io/tp/UserGuide.html#clearing-database>this group&lt;/a> uses the format `i-understand-and-confirm-purge-database-will-lose-all-data`.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/595#issuecomment-4140914893" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**45 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, here are some of my opinions, which could be wrong: 
1. It should be up to you and the group member who modified it to decide (since it is a git conflict, someone must have modified that line in the first place). From what I found, the change was done by your group member. If you deem the change as significant, then you should leave it as such (i.e. code is attributed to your team member).

<img width="1322" height="258" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73f96f39-2c4e-49bd-b07e-574a07839d1a" />

2. Code will be attributed to the person who "touched" the code last. However, if you are sure you didn't write it, you should ask your team members first if they want to claim that line. Once you have ensured that none of your team members have written that line of code, you can use the empty author tag as follows:
'''java
//@@author
<CODE-WRITTEN-BY-UNKNOWN-PERSON>
//@@author
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/595#issuecomment-4140985261" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**46 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Well definitely you can attribute the line back to yourself, but please do consult the team member that currently owns the line first before proceeding.

You may find the attribution unnecessary/strange since it involves one line. Instead, you can attribute the entire method/class to yourself:
'''java
//@@author gwhrg
/**
 * Some JavaDoc comment, if any
*/
@Test
public void func() ``{``
   ...
``}``
//@@author
'''

See &lt;a href = https://reposense.org/ug/usingAuthorTags.html>here&lt;/a> for more details.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/604#issuecomment-4168544408" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**47 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think we can think about the following scenario. 

Suppose we are doing black-box testing and we have a set of equivalence partitions. Next, we are tasked to do white/grey-box testing, where we learn more about how the code behaves. Since now we have more information about the path that each parameter takes, it is likely that some of the equivalence partitions that we have created previously can be broken down (as the paths taken by some inputs in the original partitions can be different).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/612#issuecomment-4188706780" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**48 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi there, your sequence diagram does not need to match your code 1-for-1. You can abstract out some stuff such as names of methods and arguments, as long as it makes sense to the reader. The operation invoked also does not necessarily have to be the actual function call. 

> Informal operation descriptions such as those given in the example below can be used, if more precise details are not required for the task at hand.

See &lt;a href=https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week6/topics.html#w6-1-modeling-sequence-diagrams>here&lt;/a>, under `Sequence Diagrams → Minimal Notation`.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/651#issuecomment-4230851717" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**49 :fas-comment:**
</div>

These are my opinions, so take it with a grain of salt.

I think for limitations, they can be placed under known issues or FAQ so that users know that this is the intended usage. If this functionality is reported as a bug, then you can claim that it is not in scope and should not be reported since how it works is already well-documented.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/616" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: 💡 Add GitHub Action to automate assigning of PR milestones from linked issues**
</div>

Hi all, usually my PRs are linked to a GitHub issue, and I've been encountering the workflow of after creating a PR, I still need to manually assign a milestone to the PR.so I decided to create a GitHub Action to automate this process and wanted to share it here.

## Prerequisites
When creating a PR, the PR description must link to a GitHub issue using a [supported GitHub closing keyword](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/writing-on-github/working-with-advanced-formatting/using-keywords-in-issues-and-pull-requests), and that linked issue must already have a milestone assigned, for example 'v1.5'.

If these conditions are met, the workflow will automatically copy the milestone from the linked issue to the PR.

## Future PRs
- [ ] Ensure the linked GitHub issue already has a milestone assigned
- [ ] Add the linked issue in the PR description using a supported closing keyword: 'close', 'closes', 'closed', 'fix', 'fixes', 'fixed', 'resolve', 'resolves', 'resolved'.
- [ ] Example: 'Close #123'

## Code

Add the following 'assign-pr-milestone.yml' file at '.github/workflows/assign-pr-milestone.yml':

'''
name: Assign PR Milestone

on:
  pull_request_target:
    types:
      - opened
      - edited
      - reopened
      - synchronize
      - ready_for_review

permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write
  issues: write

jobs:
  assign-milestone:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Copy milestone from linked issue
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const pr = context.payload.pull_request;

            if (!pr) ``{``
              core.setFailed("This workflow must run from a pull request event.");
              return;
            ``}``

            if (pr.milestone) ``{``
              core.info(
                'PR #$``{``pr.number``}`` already has milestone "$``{``pr.milestone.title``}``" (#$``{``pr.milestone.number``}``); leaving it unchanged.'
              );
              return;
            ``}``

            const query = '
              query ($owner: String!, $repo: String!, $number: Int!) ``{``
                repository(owner: $owner, name: $repo) ``{``
                  pullRequest(number: $number) ``{``
                    closingIssuesReferences(first: 100) ``{``
                      nodes ``{``
                        number
                        title
                        repository ``{``
                          nameWithOwner
                        ``}``
                        milestone ``{``
                          number
                          title
                        ``}``
                      ``}``
                    ``}``
                  ``}``
                ``}``
              ``}``
            ';

            const result = await github.graphql(query, ``{``
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              number: pr.number
            ``}``);

            const linkedIssues =
              result.repository.pullRequest.closingIssuesReferences.nodes ?? [];
            const currentRepo = '$``{``context.repo.owner``}``/$``{``context.repo.repo``}``';

            if (linkedIssues.length === 0) ``{``
              core.info('PR #$``{``pr.number``}`` does not close any linked issues.');
              return;
            ``}``

            const milestoneByNumber = new Map();

            for (const issue of linkedIssues) ``{``
              if (issue.repository.nameWithOwner !== currentRepo) ``{``
                core.info(
                  'Ignoring cross-repo linked issue $``{``issue.repository.nameWithOwner``}``#$``{``issue.number``}``.'
                );
                continue;
              ``}``

              if (!issue.milestone) ``{``
                core.info('Linked issue #$``{``issue.number``}`` has no milestone.');
                continue;
              ``}``

              milestoneByNumber.set(issue.milestone.number, issue.milestone.title);
            ``}``

            if (milestoneByNumber.size === 0) ``{``
              core.info('No linked issues for PR #$``{``pr.number``}`` have a milestone.');
              return;
            ``}``

            if (milestoneByNumber.size > 1) ``{``
              const milestones = [...milestoneByNumber.entries()]
                .map(([number, title]) => '"$``{``title``}``" (#$``{``number``}``)')
                .join(", ");

              core.info(
                'PR #$``{``pr.number``}`` closes issues from multiple milestones: $``{``milestones``}``. Skipping assignment.'
              );
              return;
            ``}``

            const [milestoneNumber, milestoneTitle] = [...milestoneByNumber.entries()][0];

            await github.rest.issues.update(``{``
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: pr.number,
              milestone: milestoneNumber
            ``}``);

            core.info(
              'Assigned milestone "$``{``milestoneTitle``}``" (#$``{``milestoneNumber``}``) to PR #$``{``pr.number``}``.'
            );
'''


Given below was the prompt used to create the plan in Codex (AI):

Prompt:
'''
is it possible to create a github action that automate the assignment of milestone in PR? can i decide which milestone or what can i do for automation
'''

Response:


# Automate PR Milestone Assignment

## Summary
- Yes, GitHub Actions can assign a milestone to a pull request automatically.
- In this repo, the cleanest default is: copy the milestone from the issue the PR closes.
- Add a separate workflow so existing CI in '.github/workflows/gradle.yml' stays unchanged.

## Implementation Changes
- Add '.github/workflows/assign-pr-milestone.yml'.
- Trigger on 'pull_request_target' for 'opened', 'edited', 'reopened', 'synchronize', and 'ready_for_review'.
- Use minimal permissions: 'contents: read' and 'issues: write'.
- Implement the job with 'actions/github-script' only; do not check out PR code.
- Workflow behavior:
  - Read the PR’s closing issue references.
  - If the PR already has a milestone, leave it unchanged.
  - If no linked issue has a milestone, do nothing.
  - If all linked issues with milestones resolve to one distinct milestone, assign that milestone to the PR.
  - If linked issues resolve to different milestones, skip assignment and log a clear message.

## Interfaces
- No application code changes.
- The control interface is the PR body: use normal closing keywords like 'Fixes #123' or 'Closes #123'.
- The source of truth for milestone choice is the linked issue’s milestone.

## Test Plan
- PR closes one issue with a milestone: PR gets that milestone.
- PR closes one issue without a milestone: PR stays unset.
- PR closes multiple issues with the same milestone: PR gets that milestone.
- PR closes multiple issues with different milestones: workflow skips assignment.
- PR already has a manually set milestone: workflow does not overwrite it.
- Forked PR: workflow still works because it uses 'pull_request_target' without checking out PR code.

## Assumptions
- Milestones are managed on issues before PRs are opened.
- Manual milestone assignment on the PR should win over automation.
- If you later want a different rule, the same workflow can be changed to branch-based, label-based, or fixed-release assignment before falling back to linked issues.






</panel>

**14. :fas-info-circle: Cmd + F triggers video fullscreen instead of browser find on course website page after video interaction**
</div>

# Description of bug

On the course website page, if the last thing I interact with is a video (play/pause), pressing Cmd + F opens the video in fullscreen instead of triggering the browser’s text search. This feels unexpected and kind of disruptive, as I have to click on anywhere outside the video before I can search for text.

# Expected Behavior

Pressing Cmd + F should always trigger the browser’s Find in Page functionality, regardless of the last interacted element.


# Steps to Reproduce

1. Open the website
2. Click on a video element (play or pause)
3. Press Cmd + F
4. Observe that the video enters fullscreen instead of opening the browser's text search

> Can i check if I have to recitfy the commits for level 9 now or can I just commit when Im done with week 4 task or is there like a time frame where I have to fix the git standard?

Hi, for reference in the [week 3 iP](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week3/project.html#ip), it states

> Only future commits need to follow the Git standard. Editing past commit messages (to make them comply with the Git standard) is discouraged as it changes the commit timestamp, which can alter your iP progress timeline (as seen by progress tracking scripts).

and I think the dateline to fix the git standard is the final submission in week 6 [(source)](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week6/project.html#5-submit-the-final-version-fri-feb-20th-2359)


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/140#issuecomment-3842777869" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hey @TehDrink , thanks for helping to clone and debug.

I think I figured out the issue: it's similar to #126 

I had Java 21 on my system before this course and I followed the course's [Mac installation guide of SDKMAN](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/javaInstallationMac.html) for Java 17.

The issue is every time the IDE/terminal reopens, the Java's version resets to Java 21. After debugging for awhile with ChatGPT... it seems that in `~/.zshrc`, the following 2 line code was added

```
#THIS MUST BE AT THE END OF THE FILE FOR SDKMAN TO WORK!!!
export SDKMAN_DIR="$HOME/.sdkman"
[[ -s "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh" ]] && source "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh"
```

but the terminal does not seem to follow the `sdk default java 17.0.14.fx-zulu` that was set in the guide.

## Commands for checking if SDKMAN works and the current Java version

```
sdk version
java -version
```

## A few workaround based on what I found:

### Solution 1: set those 2 lines of `export SDKMAN...` code in `~/.zprofile`.

convo from ChatGPT:
```
SDKMAN is installed and your .zshrc has the right lines, but zsh is not actually sourcing that .zshrc when Terminal starts.
macOS Terminal login-shell gotcha (very common)
macOS Terminal opens login shells by default, which can change which files are read.
zsh startup order (simplified):
/etc/zshenv
~/.zshenv
~/.zprofile ← login shells
~/.zshrc ← interactive shells
~/.zlogin
Sometimes .zshrc is skipped depending on how Terminal is configured.
✅ Bulletproof fix (recommended)
Add the SDKMAN init also to ~/.zprofile.

Open ~/.zprofile (same way you opened .zshrc) and add:

export SDKMAN_DIR="$HOME/.sdkman"
[[ -s "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh" ]] && source "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh"

This ensures SDKMAN loads for login shells too.
Then:
Save
Restart Terminal
```

### Solution 2: run the same installation commands every time IDE/terminal is reopened. commands from: [Mac installation guide of SDKMAN](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/javaInstallationMac.html):

```
curl -s "https://get.sdkman.io" | bash
source "$HOME/.sdkman/bin/sdkman-init.sh"
```

it will say 

<img width="737" height="240" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/44189f22-3297-46a5-904a-14ac0b40509b" />

and once you run `sdk version`, the SDKMAN is in use when you see:
```
SDKMAN!
script: 5.20.0
native: 0.7.16 (macos aarch64) 
```

instead of `zsh: command not found: sdk`

### Solution 3: update your JAVA_HOME / environment path according to #126 (I have not verified this)

----

If anyone know of any other better ways, feel free to share!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/157#issuecomment-3935681872" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**29 :fas-comment:**
</div>

An alternative code AI suggested:

changing `scrollPane.vvalueProperty().bind(dialogContainer.heightProperty());` to 

```
        //Scroll down to the end every time dialogContainer's height changes.
        dialogContainer.heightProperty().addListener((observable) -&gt; scrollPane.setVvalue(1.0));
```

From what I understand, the `bind()` binds the scroll position to a fixed height value, so the scroll does not work.

The fix reacts to height changes by listening when the command new messages are entered and does not do any `bind()`.

As for why the value `1.0` was used:

## Why `vvalue` Should Be `1.0` Instead of `0.0`

### Understanding ScrollPane `vvalue`

In JavaFX `ScrollPane`, the `vvalue` (vertical scroll value) is a **normalized value** that represents the scroll position:

- **`0.0`** = Top of the content
- **`1.0`** = Bottom of the content  
- **`0.5`** = Middle of the content

### Why `1.0` for Chat Applications

In a chat application, new messages are added at the **bottom** of the dialog container. Therefore, we want to scroll to the **bottom** (`1.0`), not the top (`0.0`).

┌─────────────────┐
│ Old Message 1 │ ← Top (vvalue = 0.0)
│ Old Message 2 │
│ Old Message 3 │
│ ... │
│ New Message │ ← Bottom (vvalue = 1.0) ← We want to scroll here!


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/603#issuecomment-4163886452" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**38 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I think one way is to look at this is whether you are intending to assume what the user gave (normalisation) or guiding the user to the expected outcome.

for myself, I'd lean towards keeping the parser strict and providing suggestions as a middle ground, since it avoids unintended behaviour from normalisation. These suggestions can be implemented by handling the common small mistakes like the missing spaces you mentioned, as compared to going for fuzzy matching, which may require more complexity and implementation effort in determining how similar the two commands are before suggesting a match.


</panel>

It would be not considered a bug only if it qualifies for `response.NotInScope` by specifiying in either the UG or the Appendix: Planned Enhancements of DG (see screenshot below on criterias).

For Planned Enhancements, do note that the enhancement can be reported as `type.FeatureFlaw` if the enhancements themselves are flawed/inadequate too: [link](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/tp-pe.html?cv-highlight=W3sidCI6IlAiLCJpIjoxMCwicCI6InBlLXBoYXNlcyIsInMiOiJbUEUtb25seSAobm90IGFwcGxpY2FibGUgdG8gUEUtRCIsImgiOi05MTc5Nzk2MDF9XQ%3D%3D#b-what-to-test)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/747#issuecomment-4327468401" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**50 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Since the question mention packaging data and related behavior together, it would be encapsulation instead of abstraction.

Example of Person class:
```
class Person ``{``
    private String name;
    private LocalDate dateOfBirth;

    int getAge() ``{``
        // calculate age
    ``}``
``}``
```

Encapsulation: 
- packaging data + behavior together, e.g. a `Person` object contains data variables like `name`/`dateOfBirth` and related methods like `getAge()`
- information hiding, where the object's internal data is accessed through the methods provided (`person.getAge()`) rather than directly accessing `dateOfBirth`

Abstraction:
- more about working with a higher level concept instead of lower level details, e.g. calling `person.getAge()` without needing to know the internal details of how `getAge()` method is implemented.


Link to reference in [course website](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week1/topics.html?cv-highlight=W3sidCI6IlAiLCJpIjowLCJwIjoidG9waWMtVzEuMWMiLCJzIjoiVGhlIGNvbmNlcHQgb2YgT2JqZWN0cyBpbiBPT1AgaXMiLCJoIjoxMDM0NjkwMjAzfSx7InQiOiJQIiwiaSI6MiwicCI6InRvcGljLVcxLjFkIiwicyI6IjEuIFRoZSBwYWNrYWdpbmcgYXNwZWN0OiBBbiBvYmplIiwiaCI6MTY2NTg0Nzc3OH1d):

<img width="1000" height="679" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/304d3fb9-9cbc-410e-876c-f2b0830a3b70" />
</panel>

</panel>

**2. :fas-info-circle: About when a method should be shown in UML class diagram**
</div>

2. When concrete implementation is given in `A`.
3. When `A` inherits from abstract class (with `bar()`), concrete implementation of `bar()` is already given in parent class.

> [@LZH201023](https://github.com/LZH201023) This is a loop, which is a special case of alternative paths (that's why the merge node doesn't match what you see in regular alternative paths). With the diamond, you can maintain the 'normal' case of each action having one incoming and one outgoing edge. Without it, the `c` will have two incoming edges (which is still OK). So, either way is fine.

thanks very much for your answer!
</panel>

> [@LZH201023](https://github.com/LZH201023) It's not revealed. We want the severity determination to be based on criteria and not penalty involved.
> 
> If the penalty weightage for VeryLow is X, the weightage for High is no more than 5X -- so, they are not that far apart.

Thank you for the reference. Would it also be possible to get some clarification on how the 'certain amount of bugs are forgiven' policy works?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/700#issuecomment-4277513623" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**45 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> > Thank you for the reference. Would it also be possible to get some clarification on how the 'certain amount of bugs are forgiven' policy works?
> 
> [@LZH201023](https://github.com/LZH201023) This is based on the fact that no work is perfect and there can be minor problems in the work done by any person. So, we forgive some of the minor deficiencies in the work before we start penalising a student. For each student, based on the amount of work done by that student, we decide what is the amount of bug penalty that we can forgive. For example, one team member who has done less work might be forgiven the first -0.5 -- i.e., If the total penalty for that student is -2.5, the actual penalty will be -2.0. Another student in the same team who did more work might be forgiven a higher amount of penalty, for example, the first -1.0.

Thank you!
</panel>

</div>

> > I think in this example, we have `ProgressWatcher` as an abstract class, it doesn't have to implement all methods inherited, so we don't have `+update(int)`, only the concrete class `UiWidget` needs to implement so we show it as a method.
> 
> [@jolenechong](https://github.com/jolenechong) is correct.
> 
> In a UML diagram of a real project, we always show only the methods that are relevant to the purpose of the diagram and omit the ones that are not relevant. So any method can be omitted.
> 
> However, suppose in an exam question you are asked to show all known methods. In that case, the answers are as follows.
> 
> > 1. When abstract/interface `A` inherits from abstract class/interface (with `bar()`), but no concrete implementation of `bar()` is given in `A`.
> 
> Don't show, as there is no `bar()` implementation is `A`. That is, don't show methods that are simply inherited and not implemented.
> 
> > 2. When concrete implementation is given in `A`.
> 
> Show it. As a method implementation exist in the class, the class diagram should show it.
> 
> > 3. When `A` inherits from abstract class (with `bar()`), concrete implementation of `bar()` is already given in parent class.
> 
> Show it if class `A` overrides the method `bar()`. Otherwise, don't show it.
> 
> **In summary, show methods that are implemented in the class. Don't show methods merely inherited and not implemented in the class.** This way, one can differentiate between methods that are inherited from methods that are implemented in a class.

Thank you very much!
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/148#issuecomment-3864052642" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi Sean,

The short answer is no; the instruction to "show attributes" does not force you to downgrade your object references into text. In UML, a class's properties are split into two categories: primitive attributes (like int, boolean), which go inside the class box, and associations (structural connections to other classes), which are drawn as lines. The style guide's warning strictly prefers the line notation for objects because it better represents the structural relationship.

Regarding access modifiers, you are not required to use text attributes to show them. As seen in the cheat sheet's key, visibility symbols like - (private) and + (public) are standard notations. You simply place the modifier directly in front of the role name on the association line (e.g., writing - lid at the end of the line connecting to the Lid class). This allows you to satisfy the requirement of "showing attributes and access modifiers" without cluttering the class box or violating the "line preferred" rule.

For the code you posted, totalItems is the only thing that should be written as text inside the Item box (since it's a static primitive). All other fields (parts, spareItem, lid) should be drawn as association lines with their respective private modifiers (-) and role names labelled on the lines themselves.

Hope that clears it up!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/113#issuecomment-3864063870" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

For Question 1, you are correct that multiplicity is fundamentally about the conceptual relationship, not just the code implementation. Even if the Pet class does not have a variable to store the Person, the real-world domain rule "a Pet must have one Owner" still exists. Therefore, we write 1 on the Person side to show that every Pet instance is conceptually linked to exactly one Person, regardless of whether the code allows you to navigate that link in that specific direction.

As for Question 8, to show visibility (access modifiers) on an association line, you simply place the visibility symbol (like -, +, or #) directly in front of the role name at the target end of the line. For example, if Class A has a private association with Class B, you would draw a line from A to B and label the B end with - roleName. This is the standard way to combine "attributes" and "lines" without duplicating the information in the class box.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/303#issuecomment-3931342045" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Since return arrows are optional in sequence diagrams, is it also the same (ie. optional) for self-called methods?

As mentioned by Prof. Damith, these elements are not strictly required. However, it can still be helpful to include them if the method returns a specific value or if there are multiple nested calls, as that adds clarity to the logic. Since it's a high-level overview, you have the flexibility to omit them to keep the diagram clean.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/264#issuecomment-3931348411" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi Jaejun! Great questions. Based on our module's focus on high-level clarity on similar issues, here is some input:

- System.out.println(): For high-level diagrams, we usually omit calls to standard library classes like System.out unless the printing mechanism is a core part of the logic you are trying to demonstrate. If it is essential, you can show a call to a generic :UI or UserInterface object instead of the specific Java class to keep it notation-neutral.

- Void Returns vs. Important Prints: Since return arrows are generally optional, you can omit them for void methods. However, if the printed value is critical to the user's workflow, you can represent this as a message arrow labeled with the output string (e.g., display "Success") going to a :UI actor or object.

- Object Deletion in Java: Since Java uses garbage collection, explicit "delete" arrows are rarely necessary. Both options you mentioned are typically accepted, but Option 2 (omitting the delete arrow) is usually preferred for Java code to accurately reflect that the language doesn't have an explicit delete keyword.

- Self-creation vs. Self-invocation: Self-creation is quite rare. If an object is responsible for its own instantiation logic, you can draw it as a self-invocation arrow returning to the same lifeline. However, remember that for self-calls, Prof. Damith has clarified that both the activation bar and the return arrow are optional.

- Architecture Diagram Notation: Since there is no fixed standard, using UML-lite (basic boxes and lines) is the safest bet for our class. For component-level views, sticking to standard UML notation ensures consistency and makes it easier for the team and the TAs to review.
</panel>

**20 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The lower edge of the loop box should not align exactly with the lower edge of the activation bar because the diagram shows a prototype being returned only after the loop has fully completed. Aligning them could create ambiguity by suggesting the method terminates immediately as the loop ends or even within the final iteration. Since sequence diagrams are time-based, keeping the return arrow clearly outside and below the loop box provides a much more accurate representation of the sequential logic.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/293#issuecomment-3931415939" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**22 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It is indeed possible for none of the paths in an alt frame to be executed if the conditions for those paths are not met, similar to an if-else structure where an else block is missing. In an alt frame, at most one partition can be executed, which implies that zero executions is a valid outcome if no guard conditions evaluate to true. The reason the Week 6 tutorial example required one path to be taken was not due to the alt notation itself, but because of the specific external condition forward == !backward, which logically guaranteed that at least one of those two conditions must be true. Without such a mutually exclusive and exhaustive condition, the opt frame is not necessary to allow for the possibility of no paths being taken.
</panel>

**37 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @aurorazurary, I get this error message when I ran `java -jar overflow-3.0.jar` on my end. Using Windows btw for context.

<img width="2643" height="1474" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/79197596-856c-446d-be94-0da3fbcce5cb" />

### 🐛 Bug Analysis: `DateTimeParseException`
The application crashes because the `Storage` parser cannot handle the timestamp format currently saved in the data file.

#### 🔍 The Logic Gap
In the provided code snippet, the formatter is defined as:

'''java
DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd HHmm");
'''

This pattern specifically expects:
- A **space** between the date and time.
- The time to have **no separator** (e.g., `2359`).

However, the error log shows the program is trying to parse:
`2026-02-20T23:59`

Because the input string contains a `T` and a `:`, the parser fails at index 10 (the position of the `T`).

#### 🛠️ Suggested Fix
To make the parser more robust and compatible with standard ISO timestamps, update the formatter in `Storage.java` to:

'''java
// Handles '2026-02-20T23:59'
DateTimeFormatter formatter = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm");
'''

Alternatively, if you want the app to be "extra safe," you could use a `try-catch` block around the parsing logic so that a single corrupted date entry doesn't crash the entire application instance.
</panel>

Hi @limyifei1513 

OS used: Windows

I have tested your app and here are some pointers i have to let you know:

1. Based on the first screenshot, your JavaFX application is running well, but it has a CSS styling error. The program is trying to apply a background color to a scroll bar, but it's getting a "text string" when it expects a "color object." This is likely due to a syntax error or an unsupported color format in your main.css file.

2.  I don't know your chatbot introduces me the list when the list is empty and I didn't even enter the list command when I started your app. That's kinda awkward. Plus, there were no introductions by the chatbot itself.

3.  One of the bugs I encountered is that your chatbot seems to accept the use of "|" delimiter in your task creation when it should be banning the use of such character.

4.  Another bug I also noticed is that your chatbot seems to accept invalid dates e.g. 31st Feb when I do commands like deadline and event as shown in the fourth screenshot. It should be retruning an error message for invalid dates using a strict date time formatter. 

Other than these pointers, your UI looks good despite the first screenshot and also your other commands work as intended.
</panel>

Hey @petelectron ! I noticed some warnings about JavaFX versions when running the app. It looks like your iP project settings and UI files are slightly out of sync regarding the JavaFX versions used.

- The Issue: Your build.gradle file is currently set to use JavaFX 17.0.7.

- The Cause: The .fxml files (like DialogBox.fxml and MainWindow.fxml) were saved using a version of Scene Builder that tagged them as 17.0.12.

- The Fix: Update the javaFxVersion in your .fxml files to match of that inside your build.gradle


<img width="579" height="1276" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9f1bbb23-7904-46e0-8aab-3055b7a5d7a8" />


<img width="594" height="1282" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08846e89-6fe9-4df8-8648-a2332edb3041" />

Tbh, the app still runs normally tho. The UI looks very cute and interactive tbh.

All the commands run normally as per the user guide, was kinda why your commands are much longer compared to many others, actually. Took me a while to understand how your commands work.  Also, there's no event command strangely, is it by your design you wish to do this?

After following your user guide, all your commands work as expected. Kinda wish there was a help command.  But the real feedback is that your app contains a few bugs.

-  Firstly, it doesn't seem to ban the use of "|" delimiter in task creation, so when I inserted it in, it became part of the task name, which is not supposed to happen (should return an error message for this)

-  Secondly, there's also no checking of invalid dates, so when I passed in an invalid date, e.g. 31st Feb, your chatbot takes it and converts it to 28th Feb, which is technically not supposed to be in design (should return an error message for usage of invalid dates and/or timings for your deadline command)

Overall, the usage of your app is smooth, and i hope you can take the above feedback and improve the app within the last few hours before submission, hopefully
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/759#issuecomment-4364371950" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**47 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You are overthinking it a bit. UML diagrams in these types of questions often omit interfaces or concrete implementations to avoid visual clutter. 

The explicit inclusion of `-observers: List&lt;Observer>`, `add(Observer)`, and `-notify()` in the `Process` class is definitive proof that the **Observer** pattern is intended here. It is not a trick option; it's safe to select it alongside **Singleton** (the `Main` class) and **Command** (the `Task` hierarchy).
</panel>

</panel>

**1. :fas-info-circle: Name of jar file different from given format**
</div>

When testing my assigned team's application, I realized that the name of their jar file (from the downloaded zip file) does not exactly match the format we were told to save the file as.

Link mentioning expected file format for jar:
https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week13/project.html#2-submit-final-deliverables-tue-apr-14th-14-00

<img width="733" height="189" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4c060936-532b-471e-8715-f8edb361a579" />
<br/><br/>

taking the example from the image
Expected Format: `[CS2103T-T09-2][ContactsPro].jar`
Actual Format: `CS2103T-T09-2-ContactsPro.jar`

After some research, I realized that this is the case for all teams which saved their files in the expected format.

This causes an issue when I am reading the User Guide and it specifies the user to run the jar file using the command `[CS2103T-T09-2][ContactsPro].jar`, when the file name is actually `CS2103T-T09-2-ContactsPro.jar`.

Could I ask if others have experienced the same issue? I don't think this should be treated as a bug, since the mismatch in jar file names is out of the team's control.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/513" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8. :fas-info-circle: Git-Mastery T6L2 Detour Comparing Branches: .. vs ...**
</div>

I was working through the branch-compare [exercise](https://git-mastery.org/lessons/trail/branchingLocally/#exercise-branch-compare) and wanted to know the difference between `git diff` comparing branches using `branchA..branchB` and `branchA...branchB`

This was the explanation given by the DETOUR.

<img width="644" height="226" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/107a32c7-afcd-4b91-95c8-145689012e37" />

I tried it out on the main branch of the repo, but I was confused since `stream-1...stream-2` appears to describe the changes from an empty data.txt to stream-2's data.txt.

<img width="172" height="233" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fa38fa15-e484-40cd-bf84-37ba8b101889" />

Searching [online](https://www.baeldung.com/ops/git-branches-differences#finding-the-differences-from-a-common-ancestor), I found that `branchA...branchB` compares the differences between 
1. the most recent common ancestor of branchA and branchB, and
2. branchB

<img width="427" height="168" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cb600d66-6bf6-4f79-abd4-c0e8f2141010" />

This makes more sense, since `branchA...branchB` will be different from `branchB...branchA`.

I suggest editing the description of double-dot notation and triple-dot notation to the following:

- Double-dot notation git diff `branchA..branchB`: Shows the changes from branchA to branchB. This is the one used more commonly.
- Triple-dot notation git diff `branchA...branchB`: This shows changes from the merge base (most recent common ancestor of branchA and branchB) to branchB.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/512" expanded>
<div slot="header">

<div slot="header">

**16. :fas-info-circle: Git-Mastery T2L6 .gitignore File Syntax clarification**
</div>

hello everyone, I was reading through the Sidebar: .gitignore File Syntax. In the section on Directories, I have a question on the second rule "Patterns without / ...". From my understanding of the first rule, if a pattern does not have /, then it cannot match directories (folders).

Does this mean that patterns without / can only match files? If so, then the wording on the second rule could be modified by removing "/folders".

<img width="463" height="199" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/766a54cf-8525-4ced-bb72-490628ddb3cd" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/35#issuecomment-3788045259" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I was about to comment the same. I have attached a screenshot below, where SDK should be the folder containing your JDK 17. The  Language Level should be set to 17 - ... as well.

<img width="631" height="188" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a0cdfbec-bfd5-49fe-8c77-e9d880f751d7" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/40#issuecomment-3788643302" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I have done some research from https://git-scm.com/docs/gitignore.

> The slash `/` is used as the directory separator. Separators may occur at the beginning, middle or end of the .gitignore search pattern.
> 
> If there is a separator at the beginning or middle (or both) of the pattern, then the pattern is relative to the directory level of the particular .gitignore file itself. Otherwise the pattern may also match at any level below the .gitignore level.
> 
> If there is a separator at the end of the pattern then the pattern will only match directories, otherwise the pattern can match both files and directories.

My understanding from this
Case 1: Pattern has `/` at the beginning or middle
The pattern is relative to the location of .gitignore, so only that specific file path will match.

Case 2: Pattern does not have `/` at the beginning or middle
The pattern can match anywhere in the project folder, meaning the search is done recursively.

If the pattern has `/` at the end, only directories will be matched.
If the pattern does not have `/` at the end, both files and directories will be matched.
Note: files need to be named exactly the last word before `/` (no file extensions) in order to be matched

example

- `/dir1/dir2/` or `dir1/dir2/` would match the directory named `dir2` which is in the directory `/dir1`
- `/dir1/dir2` or `dir1/dir2` would match files or directories named dir2 which is in the directory `/dir1`
- `dir1/` would match any directory named `dir1`
- `dir1` would match any files or directories named `dir1`

A possible update to the tutorial could be:
- Add a trailing `/` to match directories
'''bash
logs/             # Ignores all directories named logs
'''

- Patterns without trailing `/` match both files and directories
'''bash
logs              # Ignores all files and directories named logs
'''

- Patterns without `/` match both files and directories recursively
'''bash
*.bak             # Ignores all .bak files anywhere
'''

- Patterns with non-trailing `/` are relative to the `.gitignore` location
'''bash
secret/file.txt   # Only ignores file.txt in the /secret directory
'''
</panel>

Some comments

- I decided to add a `list` command before `delete 1`, since the previous command was `sort`. As a user, I was confused on which task will be deleted, since the tasks have a different numbering under `sort` or `find`.
- A suggestion for `delete` would be to prompt the user on whether they want to delete this specific task (after retrieving the index). An expert user can bypass this check by adding an option `--force`.
- One feedback I have, is that a larger minimum width can be enforced. I tried minimizing the width, which caused the messages to appear with one character on each line. Some comments
- I like that the app mimics messaging-style apps like whatsapp with the green and white bubbles, where messages also have timestamps. Moreover, the Bit is typing... makes me feel like I am messaging someone on my phone.
- It is interesting that your chatbot stores both the list of tasks and the chat history. Advanced users can figure out how their list of tasks was modified from start to finish.
- The chat history is also populated, which is great. The timestamps of each message could be saved, so that the correct timestamp could be populated.

- A better example could be given for a datetime format which matches YYYY-MM-DD, for instance single digit months and days: e.g. 2026-03-05.
- Unintentional commands like `deadline by 2026-03-05` which do not have a description, do not receive a response from Bit and the screen does not shift down to show the user input. A default command could be shown if Bit does not generate a response on time.

- On starting the app again, Bit retrieved the marked deadline but not the unmarked event. The unmarked event is stored in bit.txt. Bit only seems to retrieve marked tasks.
- With the starting dimensions of Bit, I am unable to see my response, so I need to increase the width of the application. You could possibly increase this width (to about 1.5 times) so that user responses appear.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/480#issuecomment-3981256162" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**23 :fas-comment:**
</div>

hello @Arkanian4 , I was waiting for someone to spot that observation. I realized the flexibility of the find command while implementing. This is due to partial matching, meaning tasks are returned as long as they match the first letter of the keyword (a non-empty prefix of the keyword appears in the description)

Some ideas I have to resolve this:
- Decide on a minimum prefix length to match before displaying the result (e.g. at least 3 characters match)

- Sort the matching list based on how many characters match. Full matches will appear first, followed by long partial matches, then short partial matches.

- Have a boolean switch in find command (--pmatch) to differentiate between full keyword match and partial keyword match

e.g. `find task` will list only full matches but `find task --pmatch` returns partial matches too

Others are welcome to pitch their ideas too!
</panel>

I agree with Elijah's point above, on how Java assertions can be used to validate that user input validation has been done before the method is called. this is a secondary check, so the code should execute correctly even if assertions are disabled. if you get an assertion error, it probably means you haven't ensured that user input validation is done in all places which calls the method containing the assertion.

adding on, I used assertions in a similar place of my iP code, in the TaskList class. when the user enters a string corresponding to a mark command e.g. `mark 2`, the `MarkCommandParser` class calls the `checkIntegerInRange` method to ensure that the integer is a valid task number. otherwise, an exception will be thrown.

<img height="300" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c6cc93e7-84bf-47d6-a4bd-32369d97f119" />

<img height="300" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/26184662-8c20-4955-b85f-a4acd749157f" />

to ensure that user input validation has been done correctly by `checkIntegerInRange`, I used an assertion in the `markTask` method in the `TaskList` class.

<img width="497" height="197" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/172c40c6-58cb-482d-b4c6-a79cf206793e" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/622" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to use third-party libraries for JavaFX UI testing (TestFX, Monocle)**
</div>

## Library

- [testfx-core](https://github.com/TestFX/TestFX)                                                                                                                             
- [testfx-junit5](https://github.com/TestFX/TestFX)                                                                                                                           
- [openjfx-monocle](https://github.com/TestFX/Monocle) 

## Purpose

Used for automated testing of JavaFX UI components. JUnit alone cannot interact with JavaFX nodes, so `testfx-core` and `testfx-junit5` are needed to simulate user interactions and assert on UI component state for components such as MainWindow and Command Box. 

 ## License

  - `testfx-core` and `testfx-junit5` are released under the [EUPL (European Union Public Licence) v1.1](https://github.com/TestFX/TestFX/blob/master/LICENSE.txt)
  - `openjfx-monocle` is released under the [GPL v2 with Classpath Exception (https://github.com/TestFX/Monocle/blob/main/LICENSE).

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3790355950" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yeap i agree with the understanding of the 3 types of resets here!

From what I understand:
1. Soft Reset leaves staging and working directory unchanged and moves HEAD back a few commits by what you specified (can be used to squash commits by re-committing them as one
2. Mixed reset will leave your work in the working directory but unstaged (could be used in some cases, like if you change your mind to commit it in small chunks as patches)
3. Hard reset does overwrite the working directory to match the target commit.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815274558" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with the point above on communicating intent. The choice sort of acts as implicit documentation for anyone reading the code too!

Using a static method usually signals that the operation is self-contained and stateless, whereas instantiating an object usually signals that there is meaningful state being tracked (like the example with Storage or TaskList).

If we make stateful classes static, the state persists in the class itself rather than a specific instance, which may not be what we want when it comes to testing of the different classes such as the storage class!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/135#issuecomment-3841064109" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/174#issuecomment-3867101788" expanded>

**7 :fas-comment:**

I would agree that option 1 would probably be the way to go when it comes down to adhering to the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle where you have a single class that acts as your source of truth for validation, and you don't need to have repeated validation code in different classes
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177#issuecomment-3869771851" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I would think that for Java `assert` it will prob not be appropriate for unit testing as it only checks for correctness of an assumption made at a certain point of code. For verifying that the logic of the code you have written is correct, i would turn to other alternatives such as the JUnit tests for questions like 'Does this method return the right result for a certain set of inputs?' 

How i would narrow it down is asking whether i am checking for an assumption or checking for a result, but this is just my 2 cents on this! 

</panel>

I think you can refer to [Java 17 Installation Guide for Mac Users](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/javaInstallationMac.html), specifically the Java 17 JDK+Azul distribution on MacOS using SDKMAN (version manager for SDKs).

You can install the Java 17 JDK FX version (codename Zulu) by running:
'''sdk install java 17.0.14.fx-zulu'''

---

You can follow up by running '''java -version'''
Expected output format:
openjdk version "17.x.x" ...
OpenJDK Runtime Environment Zulu17... (build 17.x.x...)

---

Confirm in your IDE with "Java: Configure java Runtime" to confirm that it is pointing to the correct installation!

---
Not sure if this helps but just my 2 cents on this!




</panel>

where in this case your `modules` are javafx.controls and javafx.fxml

Also replace that `path/to/javafx/lib` with your actual installation path for JavaFX if appropriate!

But tried downloading and running the jar file and it works!

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/536#issuecomment-4059910425" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**33 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Not sure if this helps, but i would think for such diagrams that design intent comes into the picture too (context is also provided that contestants are parent-child pairs, so If Person A is the parent of Person B, then Person B is the child of Person A).

I would think that it's hinting at a mutuality and there's that asymmetry involved (unlike the Person-twin example where you can use one variable to show the bidirectional relationship like `alice.twin` points to `bob`, and `bob.twin` points to `alice`)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/598#issuecomment-4159421379" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**34 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hello! This is an interesting question and personally the n-tier architecture may be a good starting point due to separation of concerns but as mentioned in the website, some of the applications may use a mix of architectural styles (e.g may use event-driven for things like "order placed" triggering notifications and emitting events and certain consumers/services like logging analytics etc. listening to these changes). For me i would ask myself about what concern i am trying to solve first!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/752#issuecomment-4352874145" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**35 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hello! Just my 2 cents on this, not sure if this helps!

I would think that because the module's guidelines strictly require _exactly one guard condition to be true_ at any given branch node, the logic branching out is meant to be exhaustive. So, if we explicitly define one path (like `[x &lt; 5]`), the diagram's underlying rules would mean that the remaining, blank path (without the label) should automatically covers the rest of the logical space.

Hope this helps!
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 12. TENG..THUR `@archonward` (33 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/598" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Choosing an architectural style**
</div>

I was reading the architectural styles section, and personally I feel like n-tier (layered) architecture is the safest default because it keeps responsibilities separated (UI → logic → data), which seems easier to maintain and test. 

If you were building a real student project, would you also default to n-tier, or would you pick something else like client-server, event-driven? Has anyone actually tried the other alternative styles and had success with them before? Some of the other styles do seem quite abstract to me on a first glance.

<img width="1606" height="268" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/72e8437c-1965-4bfa-8d08-80ebb7ea50e4" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/575" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Defensive Programming**
</div>

Hi everyone, I was reading the section on defensive programming, especially the examples of returning a defensive copy and enforcing compulsory associations (preventing Guarantor from being null). It also mentions that we don’t need to be 100% defensive because extra defensiveness can make code more complex and slower.

In your opinion, when is it worth writing “more defensive” code (e.g., null checks, defensive copies, enforcing constraints) instead of relying on tests and documentation to prevent misuse?

<img width="1494" height="372" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3da3c4ec-0f41-4b9a-8d98-4c36d4a34483" />

<img width="1542" height="518" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e2ae803d-2639-47ef-adc1-fcf31c2c6078" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/551" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: How strict should we adhere to Law of Demeter**
</div>

The LoD says “don’t talk to strangers” and avoids **_b.getGoo().doSomething()_**-style chains. But sometimes method chaining feels convenient and readable.

Where would you personally draw the line between “clean” and “too strict” for LoD? Is any “friend-of-a-friend” call always bad, or are there cases where it’s fine?

I know when I was doing up the TP, there are some cases that violate this LoD, and it took additional work if I wanted to alter the implementation. Is there a sort of rule of thumb regarding the adherence threshold for this principle?

<img width="1653" height="698" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/60756c6d-31af-43e3-afa0-b6cf60288dc4" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/546" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Iterative Development vs Frozen Sprint Scope: Helpful or Harmful?**
</div>

I was reading the part on iterative models and Scrum, and I noticed a tension: iterative development encourages learning and adjusting each iteration, but Scrum also says the sprint backlog is frozen once the sprint starts.

In your opinion, does freezing requirements during a sprint help or hurt an iterative/agile team overall?
For this mod, we are utilising the breadth-first iteration, to deliver a working but basic product early, but what kind of scenario would a depth-first iteration achieve better results? Personally when I develop my own personal projects I tend to use the latter approach, curious to hear which approach you guys usually prefer as well.

<img width="1642" height="1102" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/371dd63f-2f0c-4b30-ac12-802750c3d4f3" />

<img width="1504" height="456" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3714c59a-710f-4a6a-916c-89d6d9f5caca" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/514" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Why system pass isnt equal to acceptance pass?**
</div>

Hi guys, I was reading the above section that says passing system tests doesn’t necessarily mean you’ll pass acceptance testing (like the design solving the spec, but still not the real user problem).

Since system testing is mainly about verifying the system against the written system specification under controlled test conditions, it doesn’t always guarantee the system will succeed in the real user context.

In your opinion, what’s the most common reason a system might pass system testing but still fail acceptance testing? Is there any way to then ensure that our TP has the highest chance of success in the real user context?

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/487" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6. :fas-info-circle: Coupling and cohesion real life application**
</div>

I was reading the notes on coupling and cohesion. 
[https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14000762/what-does-low-in-coupling-and-high-in-cohesion-mean](url) 
The top answer in this link sums up basically what coupling and cohesion actually means nicely. 

The guide pushes for low coupling and high cohesion, and I also saw people online say they often go together, because if a module is focused, it usually depends less on others. But in some designs, for example microservices (large application is built as a suite of small, independent, and loosely coupled services),  you can still end up with lots of dependencies.

In real projects, would it not be sometimes worth accepting higher coupling to keep things simpler or ship faster, as long as each module stays cohesive?

</panel>

**7. :fas-info-circle: Apache Commons Logging (JCL)**
</div>


<img width="2613" height="468" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2c6d6de6-b5ea-4fa8-aba7-d68a37d6ac5d" />

Hi everyone, I was reading the Quick Start section (W6.4 Logging) where it says JCL is designed to silently ignore any logging library it can’t initialise, so the application still runs instead of failing.

Do you agree with that design choice, or would you rather it fail fast and show an error immediately? Why?

Personally, I feel failing fast is better because it tells the developer right away that the logging library can’t be accessed, instead of hiding the problem until later. After all, isnt logging supposed to make debugging easier later on? If the library cant be accessed, and nothing is logged during a application crash down the road, troubleshooting becomes harder. Thoughts?
</panel>

Interesting. lambda expressions should still follow the same “Egyptian brackets” (K&R) and wrapping rules, because they are just another kind of block/statement in Java. Not sure why your example 1 fails the style checker, but maybe it could be due to the lambda block? 

A clean, commonly accepted approach that usually satisfies CheckStyle is to avoid a block lambda when you don’t need it, personally i always try to avoid these. You can replace your code with the below, see if the error goes away.
@Test
public void deserialiseTask_invalidTodo_exceptionThrown() ``{``
    assertThrows(ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.class,
            () -&gt; Storage.deserialiseTask("T|0"));
``}``

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177#issuecomment-3871262163" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi very interesting question, did some searching online and I found an example where assertion is usually used. This weeks material says that if it “should be impossible” unless the programmer made a mistake, use a Java assert.

For public methods, argument checks should usually throw the specified exception (so it works whether assertions are on or off). For non-public methods where you fully control the callers, assertions are appropriate. For example,

'public void setRefreshRate(int rate) ``{``
    if (rate &lt;= 0 || rate > MAX) throw new IllegalArgumentException();
    setRefreshInterval(1000 / rate);
``}``

private void setRefreshInterval(int interval) ``{``
    assert interval > 0 && interval &lt;= 1000 / MAX; // should always hold if public method is correct
``}``'

This is just one example where I saw online that assertions can come in handy, I'm sure there should be other examples too, I am interested to see if anyone has other use cases for them. 

As for the performance cost, you are correct that there is a tiny additional cost, which is why the best practice is to keep assertions short and meaningful, not expensive computations. Oracle’s guidance is that frequent short assertions about important assumptions are appropriate. 
The link for oracle: [https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19683-01/806-7930/assert-11/index.html](url)

Edit: Not sure why clicking the link brings us to a page in github, you have to manually copy out the link and paste it in browser
</panel>

I have used AI, especially to help with making sure my naming conventions and code style is adhering to the same set of guidelines that the course has provided us with. It helps a ton with saving painstaking and manual work of needing to go through each of the violations especially for the checkstyle portion that we just did last week, and its really useful for me.

For the designing of the GUI, I also used AI to help with the debugging of the 2 fxml file, as mentioned by @icyyolo. I am still quite unfamiliar with the syntax for the majority of the FXML files, so AI is a big help in saving you the trouble to type everything out by hand.

Outside the context of the IP, AI assisted coding has also helped me with some of my own projects, and the key thing is really to have a detailed prompt in order to let the AI understand what it is that you want. Generally, I use ChatGPT and Qwen as my 2 favourite models, and my prompt usually involves outlining the high level idea that i want first, followed by a point-form format of the different lower level implementations or features that I want to have as well. 

In addition, I highly recommend doing some sort of cross referencing between the 2 models, as I have noticed that AI sometimes have "hallucinations" and does give wrong code, especially when the chat history starts to get too long.
</panel>

**17 :fas-comment:**
