<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/520#issuecomment-4031951229" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The notes gave a pretty good analogy that I found useful to remember the difference. Aggregation is a container-contained relationship; in the case of this example, a shelf is like a container for books, and containers can be empty. On the other hand, composition, as the name suggest is a _composed-of_ r/s. In this context, a book is composed of chapters; if a book has no chapters, its hardly a book at all. hope this analogy helps :)

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/520#issuecomment-4037396396" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> [@desnen](https://github.com/desnen) Thanks for citing the book-chapter analogy but be careful how you interpret it. Composition doesn't enforce a 'at least one' multiplicity (i.e., it doesn't say "cannot be empty"). A book can exist without chapters (if it is not divided into chapters) but a chapter cannot exist without belonging to a book. The more important thing that determines composition is the existence of the 'part of' relationship: a chapter is a 'part of ' a book but not 'part of' a shelf.

@damithc oh, I've always thought of the relationship(wrongly) being the other way. Thank you for the clarification prof!🙏
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/534#issuecomment-4055180381" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I find this an interesting question, as I have similar queries as well. I think whether it is a composition relationship boils down to context, like the notes mentioned. For the scenario that you mentioned, I would think a better implementation for a similar "updating software" would be to have two different tasks objects for each of them. This makes each tasks specifc to each different project; I would assume that software updates for system upgrade vs security patch would be quite different, and hence why two different objects required. Hence, I believe this is why they chose to model this as a composition instead, to capture this particular relationship.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/541#issuecomment-4062568736" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yea, I think if i rmbr correctly from the CS2030S it all boils down to the specifications provided. I think argument 1 is wrong in the wrong direction because if the subclass is more permissive, you can't substitute objects of the superclass with a more permissive child class object, breaking LSP. LSP is broken both ways because of specifications provided that are not compatible with each other; rectangle needs to be able to take in any width and height, square must take in same width, same height. One of them mandates a r/s between both lengths, one of them mandates NO r/s between both lengths. Hence, there's just no way for either to inherit each other without breaking LSP.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/547#issuecomment-4071851815" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/173#issuecomment-3865984601" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @Leomarbel, when you attempted to restart IDEA, did you also make sure to end any remaining instances of `conhost.exe` or `OpenConsole.exe` via Task Manager after closing the software itself? As there is a possibility that such processes might still persist in the background even after closing the main program, and that relaunching IDEA without first closing said background processes might result in the IDE attempting to reconnect to a 'zombie' process that is already frozen or unresponsive.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/154#issuecomment-3865990145" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @Frankwotfurters, to add to what others have mentioned, one major "potential issue" with a static-heavy design is Testing (Unit Testing & Mocking).

If `Storage` or `Ui` methods are static, it becomes much harder to isolate your logic during testing. For example, if you want to test a method that calls Storage.storeTasks(), you are forced to actually write to the hard drive every time you run that test.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/133#issuecomment-3865997266" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<img width="494" height="785" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1fe29f44-0a9f-4d09-8c30-3091e76b8f0c" />

Here is my GUI, I followed the tutorials and adapted it with some CSS styling. Looking to further enhance the overall theme and polish soon!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/157#issuecomment-3866012868" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The behavior you observed with `bind()` happens because it creates a permanent, one-way link. Whenever you try to scroll manually, the binding immediately forces the `vvalue` back to the container's height, effectively 'locking' the view at the bottom.

In contrast, `Platform.runLater()` avoids this lock by triggering a one-time jump only after the UI has finished its layout pass. This ensures the chat auto-scrolls for new messages while leaving the scroll bar free for manual use afterward.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/174#issuecomment-3874960545" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I would go with Option 1 to follow **DRY** and the **Single Responsibility Principle** because centralizing validation creates a single source of truth and prevents inconsistent error handling, though I would still use Option 2 as **defense in depth** for high risk areas like security.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/92#issuecomment-3874989699" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think skipping the corrupted lines is a better approach since it preserves user data and keeps the chatbot functional instead of just crashing. That said, reinitializing the file is also feasible if you want a simpler implementation without the extra parsing logic.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/187#issuecomment-3894526011" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @Haofeng125, I believe that the `isExit` boolean does not violate SLAP as the principle focuses on the conceptual level of tasks within a method, not the data types used. Since the variable only manages high-level control flow, it remains consistent with other high-level calls like greeting or executing commands. Moreover, a violation would only occur if you mixed this logic with low-level implementation details, such as manual string parsing.
</panel>

Hi @Mehxa ,

1.

Not sure if it's intentional, but typing in 'find x' shows a new list but marking that list marks the original numbers (if the user typed 'list' beforehand). Maybe it's a better idea to have it correlate to the original list instead? So as to avoid potential user confusion. 

2.

<img width="506" height="170" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/81d5dbbd-2c84-441d-bcaf-8229ecc4112d" />

<img width="1223" height="471" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/62cc5fed-d3ab-4ccd-be1e-56c4d512900f" />

File corruption doesn't seem to be handled correctly, resulting in a crash.

Other than the above, the commands all work as intended! The FNAF-themed GUI looks AWESOME as well so kudos to that!
</panel>

OS Used: Windows

Hi @KeithCheng22 ,

Just conducted a smoke test, here are my findings:

1. Not sure if it's intentional but `data/keef.txt` file does not seem to be created on startup and is only created after a task is added.

2. 

<img width="498" height="786" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6445c01c-ac06-4f8a-9693-96af93dc88e2" />

Swapped fields (start time after end time) as well as the scenario where the start time is later than the end time are falsely added. Perhaps you could add checks for those scenarios?

3.

<img width="498" height="782" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/544f491b-0850-4aca-84d5-6cd9f824d1fa" />

<img width="497" height="782" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5bfc1bb1-c557-4da6-bc81-3c79a28f5787" />

Tasks added with the inclusion of the file storage delimiting character `'|'` are overlooked and are listed inappropriately after restarting the `jar` file. Perhaps consider banning the use of that character in task creation commands?

Other than the above the rest of the commands work fine, and I also appreciate how there is a cap to how small the chatbot window can be resized to, which enhances the user's experience by preventing the UI from becoming unreadable or breaking the layout. Good job overall!


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/386#issuecomment-3933798863" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**19 :fas-comment:**
</div>

OS Used: Windows

Hi @eunicegwee ,

Just attempted to run a smoke-test on `kira.jar` and I ran into this issue upon running `java -jar kira.jar`:

<img width="1427" height="604" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/22f755ce-a59a-434c-b8a5-d260cf3ef637" />

From what I understand, this is usually an environment-specific issue where JavaFX can't find the correct graphics pipeline for Windows. After taking a look at your `build.gradle`, I noticed that the native dependencies for different platforms (Windows, Mac, Linux) aren't explicitly defined in the `dependencies` block meaning that the JAR only includes the native libraries for the OS it was built on (which in this case is Mac).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/390#issuecomment-3934662262" expanded>

<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I was thinking that if no concrete implementation of bar() is given in A, then we need not show bar() as it can be interpreted that that operation should be a part of it since it was inherited but it has not been implemented.

For point 2, it makes more sense to add it in to explicitly show that bar() has been implemented in A. 

For point 3, I also agree with @kenghow that we should probably only show it when there has been a change like A overrides the implementation in the parent class. 

This is just my opinion, let me know if it is wrong :)
</panel>

**2. :fas-info-circle: Question about workflow for incremental PRs for tP**
</div>


Suppose I am implementing a relatively big feature F. I understand that instead of one big PR, I should break it down to smaller issues and PRs. 

Let's say my increments (separate branches branching off from master) are: 1-add-X-command-skeleton, 2-add-X-command-logic, 3-add-X-command-testing.

Does that mean after each increment, I need to wait until that corresponding PR is merged to master before I can start my next increment? Subsequent increments would require code from the previous increments so there is no way to work on them simultaneously right?

Is there any workaround to this?

Thank you!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3881829847" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with @Omgeta. After writing a few JavaDocs properly according to the coding standard, letting AI generate the rest for similar methods save a lot of mundane and repetitive effort.

I've also found that this works best when the methods follow SLAP and have clear logic repsonsibilities. If the method is doing multiple things or is too long, the generated comments may become vague or misleading, which is usually a sign that the code needs refactoring. So in a way, AI-generated JavaDoc can serve as a quick "design-check" for method clarity.


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/187#issuecomment-3888055958" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<img width="1756" height="910" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e70744d9-b7b3-4705-ab32-f36bb495f96b" />

This may side-track a little but it is okay to have different levels of abstraction sometimes, especially if the code readability is not affected (via clear comments & separation of logic). 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/202#issuecomment-3902985626" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<img width="522" height="729" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/875882db-64ce-4651-9a29-4999f197b723" />

Overall, I really like the UI and the use of Yoshikage Kira's monologue as the chatbot introduction. It really gives the bot a strong personality from the start, which makes it interesting.

As a Windows user, I don't see any issues with the functionality. However, some improvements I can think of: 
- Dealing with duplicate tasks
- Since clear is highly destructive (removes all tasks), it might be safer to include a confirmation prompt before exeuction. (e.g., The bot can prompt "Are you sure you want to clear all the tasks? (y/n)". An alternative is to include an undo command.

Other than that, good job on meeting the requirements and implementing a functional chatbot with an aesthetic GUI.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/604#issuecomment-4197339903" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Agree with the comments above. One additional perspective is that equivalence partitions should generally be defined based on observable behaviour rather than just input ranges. If inputs within the same parittion lead to different outputs or execution paths, they should be treated as separate partitions. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/615#issuecomment-4197359763" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think multiplicity should reflect how many values an object can actually hold, not how many values exist in the enum. The enum size can be a useful upper bound but the multiplicity is ultimately determined by the data structure (e.g., Set&lt;B>) and business rules
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/619#issuecomment-4197625932" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**19 :fas-comment:**
</div>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/721#issuecomment-4289593213" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**20 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on my understanding, extentions are meant to describe meaningful variations of the MSS, not every single possible low-level validation detail. So I would say that it depends on the context and how "useful" is the explicit extension of this use case.

For instance, if multiple validation failures lead to the same system behaviour (e.g., show error -&gt; prompts user to re-enter), perhaps it can be grouped into one extension to avoid repeition. 
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815239912" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> A clearer example for what I mentioned, let's take Storage vs Parser.
> 
> Storage mainStorage = new Storage("duke.txt");
> Storage backupStorage = new Storage("backup.txt"); // notice this would not be possible if Storage were static
> 
> mainStorage.save(tasks);       // writes to duke.txt
> backupStorage.save(tasks);     // writes to backup.txt
> And for Parser we have something like this.
> 
> Parser.parse("todo read book");
> Parser.parse("deadline submit report /by 2026-01-30");
> Since Parser holds no state, it's suitable to be written as a static class.

I agree with this. I believe if you aren’t planning to store any form of data in your class, you should just use static methods in that class, where the data passed into the method is used only for that method. However, if you see yourself passing in the same data into multiple methods, it’s better if you use a class with non-static methods because you creating a class with multiple static methods is pretty much equivalent to functional programming and defeats the purpose of OOP
</panel>

> OS: Windows Here are my findings during the smoke test:
> 
> 1. Inputs that do not directly correlate to another command is treated as a new todo task. I wonder if this behaviour is intentional, as this feature was not stated in the README.
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="493" height="780" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/73937826/551936224-deb23f36-5593-4f36-8c92-75495f7bade5.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..vEAopdAqHlX5mj_uUQDbsz-acat6hg35TuUevXRHyAg">
> 2. The find command is case sensitive, which can make it a hassle for the user to find tasks.
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="495" height="782" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/73937826/551937010-dcfcbb60-df30-4842-b042-2cbd181eab9f.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..AoqGEMR2O8Pmskur2FNIkOpoNzfE1WvoNV-on4TtHNE">
> 3. When inputting an incorrect date & time for a deadline task, the error informs the user to input in d/M/yyyy HH:mm format, which is not actually accepted by the program.
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="492" height="783" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/73937826/551938584-f01e9207-b9c0-49d6-b4fd-ebd05e3c292f.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..0YWM2-avqQYbGT16JUdRrR_iWd2WS8V0-KaufsmtG44">
> 4. When using the find function, the tasks are not listed with their actual index, which can make it difficult to delete/mark/unmark tasks accurately when using such commands in conjunction with the find command.
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="496" height="783" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/73937826/551939378-cababf65-8015-40d5-8e26-b4ce5ab9b0fb.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..lXq-l4B33w7TveJwkqd5igoMSm7h5F2FFlWnGKqkIdQ"> <img alt="Image" width="493" height="780" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/73937826/551939433-2d627889-b018-4c1f-8823-822003363a4a.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..FgP4b1jCqSpIkz0Tu2hGOwH-WCQKQ_iCnDUCQD2Kpjk">

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/287#issuecomment-3931331644" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> OS: macOS Tahoe 26.3
> 
> ### Smoke Test Checklist
> 
> * [x]  **Commands**: All commands specified in the User Guide work as intended.[x]  **Crashes/Weird Behaviour**: None occured during the duration of the smoke test.* [ ]  **Error handling**:
>     
>     * [ ]  Missing error handling for empty `todo` and `deadline` commands. When entering `todo` or `deadline` command in isolation, the command cannot be executed, indicating an unhandled error on the programmer's part.* [ ]  Error handling could be more specific to let the user know what information is missing. When creating a deadline with no description, the error message prompts that the due date and time is missing instead of the description. (Figure 1)* [ ]  When creating a `todo`, `deadline` or `event`, if there is no whitespace after the command word, the program executes the command and simply removes the first character following the command word and sets it as the description. (Figure 2)* [ ]  Missing error handling for `delete` command when trying to delete a task at a non-existent index. A similar error handling already exists for the `mark` and `unmark` commands but not for `delete`. (Figure 3)* [ ]  When using the `edit` command and the task type is not specified, the task will be overwritten and will no longer possess any type. (Figure 4)[x]  **Data Persistency**: Works well. Upon exiting the program, tasks are saved to a `sleeper.txt` file. Tasks are loaded correctly when launching the app again.
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="387" height="236" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/52093793/552131885-f97fa3ee-7f81-434e-9d42-5fef28ba3984.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..PUR0yQeGqSiByqwmHxXUfqCUwP_eXAvDrX2OBiFZ7go">
> Figure 1
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="387" height="501" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/52093793/552135391-afa2bbea-d7a6-48b1-9d66-b277ea365a11.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..B5K5AnqWNdvcbtZ5QD1BGRgbUTq7_tXGAz1IyQzMz-8">
> Figure 2
> 
> <img alt="Image" width="402" height="382" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/52093793/552133699-64b0f899-fe8c-4673-b66b-e9fae7c8f007.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..yIyvCBqcLqnALH1geG6gpGqfFzIQBVcOQO9TYpsnxuo">
> Figure 3: invalid `delete` command cannot be executed (missing error handling)
>

<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: CI is failing due to Codecov but the changes are trivial**
</div>

I have a small PR to refactor some of my lines for readability and consistency, but now the PR is failing CI due to a drop in code coverage mainly in my `SampleDataUtil` class, but the changes only consist of line swaps. A partial screenshot is attached below:

<img width="1077" height="167" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/de5713e0-7510-4858-b020-f4406dcb891d" />

Before it was:
'''java
getTagSet("friends"),
getBoxSet("box-1"),
'''

Question is how do I write test cases for this? eg. am I supposed to have a `SampleDataUtilTest.java` class? Because the original AB3 did not have it and it was passing CI so it doesn't make sense for me to create one right? Otherwise what are some other approaches? Thanks in advance!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/476" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: How often to update tP README?**
</div>

For our tP READMEs, if we are including a section with a list of our features but we are not sure what features our final product will have (which extensions we will end up implementing), are we required to update the README throughout the weeks to reflect the changes?

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/473" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Do we need to show usage of buffers in our tP workflow?**
</div>



For buffers between milestones, do we have to explicitly set our milestones deadline to include the buffers we decide on? 

For example, if the due date for milestone v1.1 is 5 March (Thursday) and we decided on a 2 day buffer, do we set our milestone deadline to be 3 March to indicate that we followed the buffer we decided on? Or is this on an own-self check basis where we just track it within our group?
</panel>

Hi, I tested on Windows 11.

<img width="395" height="630" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d93e10bf-1a4e-4d15-82d4-8f83150aca9f" />

<img width="396" height="634" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d30077e7-ab8e-40fd-86af-6804aa6bf64a" />

1. Your exit command is not working properly, the gui doesn't close.
2. For deadlines and events, whitespace descriptions are not being handled properly: input does not return a response upon entering, but task list is apparently still being updated. See attachments 2 and 3. There are exceptions being thrown as seen in the terminal so do try to replicate this and fix it.
3. The above point breaks some commands. `list` and `find` stops working entirely, `unmark` ,`mark`, and `delete` only works on some indexes.
4. Data persistency works but I think point 2 corrupts the task list.

As discussed by others earlier, I think the difference in ordering between both when you include `--all` is due to the fact that Git would have to go through all the refs and then show it all in topological order. Perhaps using `--graph` as mentioned earlier may provide a better visualisation of this.

### 32. JUST.. WEN `@nitsujeel` (18 posts)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/245" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/nitsujeel/ip/releases/tag/A-Release
* Which OS'es to test on: Windows | Linux | Mac (tested a little on my own macbook but more help is always welcome)
* Things to check specifically:
    * Local file creation, reading, and updating: Check if the save file is created on first run of the application, check if it loads properly with saved tasks from second run onwards.
    * Exception handling: Check if any edge cases are not properly handled, e.g. improper index with `mark`/`unmark`/`delete`, etc.
    * Bye: `bye` should trigger a farewell message and cause the app to close by itself in a few seconds.

Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/4" expanded>

Week 1 →  Admin → 2. Learn about the course → Admin: Using this website [essential info] → A panel that can be opened as another tab 

Clicking the button that opens this panel in a new tab results in a new tab that displays a 404 error as shown below. The phrasing of the text above the panel,

"You can use the  button in a panel to open it as a new tab (it can be expanded in-place too). This feature is available only for panels containing another full page of the website (i.e., not available if the panel contains an extract of a page). e.g.,"

Hi @SpaceMongoose 

I'm using MacOS to test your product.

## 1. Commands

Commands all function as expected, and edge cases are all addressed well.

todo
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7a818de8-4979-4853-8850-0b9fb8ed2fa2" />

deadline
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/79e79413-b8f0-449a-b122-f1fe216102ff" />

event
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/069cd6b6-e02c-4c47-8f09-a73a6fa8548f" />

list (and default loading of locally saved tasks)
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7b221e9a-73b5-425d-9acd-e8bb881a911f" />

mark/unmark
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/363d9dff-a53f-42f3-b596-746a6270f51c" />

find
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/646ffb63-ec6c-4804-a8ff-25cfd1e40835" />

delete
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e07a7494-f83e-42c0-88f0-4bc8357d67f2" />

undefined commands
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/64715d9d-07ae-4082-91b3-eb683a365770" />

One suggestion I have is to perhaps change the message printed for `list` when there are zero tasks in the list, so the user is certain there is nothing in the list and it's not a bug:
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/38d07013-be97-432d-8060-1994607c66a9" />

## 2. GUI

The GUI is smooth and intuitive, and great overall.

I like how success and error messages are coloured differently.

As mentioned above, I think Esquie would benefit from a minimum window size.

## 3. Import Functionality

Works as expected.

Contrary to what was said above, selecting the exact txt file worked fine for me, as specified in your user guide.
<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cb9ef67a-b3dd-4c2a-be67-80891097dac9" />

## 4. Other

I was not able to send my commands to Esquie from the typing textbox using my "Enter" key ("return" on mac).

As a result, I had to click the textbox to type, then click send, then click the textbox to type my next command, which is suboptimal for users who prefer typing (as mentioned in your user guide).

</panel>

Hi @cyledge 

I'm using MacOS.

## 1. Functionality

Commands (except `bye`, see below) all seem to work fine:

<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/90d33307-1bc9-4b8f-b4ca-de6089a21781" />

<img width="1470" height="923" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/11b53e26-faea-4387-95ae-e24744300320" />

<img width="512" height="740" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/da791a8b-e50d-482e-84a8-0fbd26ec678c" />

## 2. GUI

GUI is smooth and can be resized, but can be shrunken until the text wraps to an unintelligible extent, or even until the window is quite literally showing nothing:

<img width="243" height="354" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e13b9668-0f84-4c31-9d48-1ae36bc5a85c" />

<img width="185" height="141" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2b38a0d7-29e0-4c46-8f91-216a2e8d0621" />

## 3. `bye` causes the program to crash

Telling CYbot `bye` seems to trigger a crash. Here are some screenshots of the error messages given to me by my terminal and my OS:

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/546#issuecomment-4074144327" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi! To me, I think that for whether freezing the sprint helps or hurts more, it depends heavily on the duration of the sprints that the team agrees on. If a sprint is too short, the whole purpose of having sprints with frozen requirements is defeated. If a sprint is too long, adaptability is compromised. But if a sprint manages to find the sweet spot, it allows the team to have a focus, which helps achieve progress, while also allowing the team to take what is learnt during each sprint and use it to improve the next one.

For breadth first vs depth first, I think depth first might work better when one feature is especially important or risky, like for example what @jolenechong mentioned, if the product depends heavily on one complex core function, I also think it could make more sense to develop that deeply first before building around it.
</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 33. TSE ..HENG `@Iota113` (17 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/583" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Design Pattern for ClearCommand Confirmation**
</div>

Hi! My team is thinking of adding a confirmation feature for the clear command in AB3 as it is so destructive.

We thought of two approaches:

1. Creating a new ConfirmCommand that (for now) only supports clearing of the address book. When the user types `clear`, they will be prompted by a message that asks them to type `confirm clear` if they want to delete the whole book.

2. Add states to the ClearCommand: PROMPT, CONFIRMED and ABORTED, and returning ClearCommands of varying states. The use case then goes something like: user types `clear`, they get prompted by a very standarrd `This will delete all contacts. Are you sure? [y/N]`, to which they can type y or n (case-insensitive) to proceed.

We identified some concerns for both approaches:
In 1, the ClearCommand functionally does nothing now, only giving the prompt. The confirmation stage can also be skipped by typing `confirm clear` directly.

In 2, the changes of ClearCommand is quite a big departure as no previous commands has states. Also, the fact that the ClearCommand now has to handle both confirmation and clearing may break single responsbility. Design-wise, the AddressBookParser also has a flag called awaitingConfirmation specifically for the ClearCommand. 

Would love to hear what others feel is an elegant way to solve this problem!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/222" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/Iota113/ip/releases/tag/A-UserGuide
* Which OS'es to test on: Mac | Linux
* Things to check specifically:
1. Ability to run the jar file on Mac or Linux OS
2. Ability to save and load data
3. Shows appropriate (red) error messages with invalid commands

* User guide: https://iota113.github.io/ip/


Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/188" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Code Quality: Naming local variables in parser**
</div>

Here is a snippet of a method that parses adding an Event Task e.g. "event CS2103T Meeting /from 2026-01-30 /to 2026-01-30". How it works is it removes "event" from the user input, and divides the remaining String into several parts by "/from", "/to" so the parser can get the necessary parts for each field.

I am having trouble making this more readable per the suggestions from this week's topic on code quality. I think one of the issues is I can't think of better names than "parts" and "timeParts" for the String arrays.

Would love to hear what others think!

```
    private static AddCommand parseAddEvent(String userInput) throws SandroneException ``{``
        // Removes "event" from userInput
        String remainingCommand = userInput.substring(5).trim();

        checkEventCommand(userInput);

        String[] parts = remainingCommand.split("/from");
        
        String desc = parts[0].trim();
        if (desc.isEmpty()) ``{``
            throw new SandroneException("The description of a task cannot be empty!");
        ``}``
        
        String[] timeParts = parts[1].split("/to");
        checkFromToFields(timeParts);

        String fromString = timeParts[0].trim();
        String toString = timeParts[1].trim();
        LocalDate from = parseDate(fromString);
        LocalDate to = parseDate(toString);

        Task newEvent = new Event(desc, from, to);
        return new AddCommand(newEvent);
    ``}``
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177#issuecomment-3885567615" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**

1. Check size of task list is updated correctly after adding or deleting a task
2. Check a task is truly marked or unmarked after the method to do so has completed
3. "default" case of "else" in my conditonals for parsing commands in my Parser: assert false since it will never reach that case
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/583#issuecomment-4127516694" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @DenseLance-alt 

Thanks for the sharing! 

For your question, basically for approach 1 right now because typing `clear` creates a ClearCommand that does nothing besides prompting the user to type `confirm clear` to actually clear the addressbook. The user could have just typed `confirm clear` from the start without having to go through: type `clear` -&gt; be prompted -&gt; type `confirm clear`, making the confirmation process moot.

Regarding the multi-step command, it seems to be less of a constraint than recommendation? It is also stated around the same place:

> That being said, multistep commands can complement the one-shot-command approach in specific cases e.g.,
> 
> as a crutch for new users to learn the command format
> to be used for rarely-used tasks or tasks requiring multiple steps (e.g., importing data from a file)

But yeah I think the ideal case is one-shot command. I am personally leaning more towards the undo/redo approach as it seems the more responsible solution.
</panel>

</panel>

<img width="1384" height="745" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f90d544e-fdde-4a48-913b-edec10f06a23" />

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Confusion on how to write meaningful Assertions**
</div>


Hi everyone, I have gone through this week's material on Assertions, but I am still confused as to when to use these assertions apart from testing (i.e the JUnit cases). 😖 

While I understand that assertions are meant to indicate a mistake made by the programmer, I am having difficulties thinking of when and where to use these assertions. Would it take the form of testing every/most variable types & values during runtime? 😬 
If that is the case, then is it really a best practice since each assertion in code will incur an additional processing cost? 🤔 

I would really appreciate some guidance in this, and possibly some examples to set me in the right direction.
Thank you. 😃 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/179#issuecomment-3870630304" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

After taking a look at your repo code, it seems that perhaps your getResponse() method is trying to achieve too much? 
Personally, for my iP, I passed the responsibility of executing by command to my Parser class. This may help to reduce your LoC for getResponse().

However, to truly abstract everything out, you may consider creating the optional "Command" class to further extract each command's execution functionality, as mentioned by @jolenechong and @DenseLance-alt.

@CH144 does this well for his code, so it may be worthwhile to check out his repo. 😉 
</panel>

@chirlasai & @Jasmitha-05 The time format issues were due to the fact that the Event class was taking in Strings while the Deadline class took in the Datetime object. However, I noticed that almost everyone whose code I smoke tested used Datetime for the Events as well, and so I have modified my code to fit this expectation.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/557#issuecomment-4090942074" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I had a similar issue with **VSCode**. I'm using the "Test Runner for Java" extension and presume that you are too (you can verify this at the Extensions tab)

Here's how I resolved it:
<br/>
1. Ensure that your `build.gradle` file includes `jacoco` in its plugins

<img width="791" height="210" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2f9be062-1ed8-4f20-9303-f71158910224" />
<br/><br/>
2. Include these 2 dependencies in your list of dependencies in the `build.gradle` file.


<img width="1282" height="161" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/642511e6-d61c-484c-9c68-655d9e963256" />
<br/><br/>
3. Open the Command Palette `Ctrl+Shift+P`, type "Java: Clean Java Language Server Workspace", and select Reload and delete.


<img width="438" height="249" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/20ff89ac-21b6-489e-be58-6254e88c7d85" />
<br/><br/>
4. Try running the "Run Test with Coverage" now
<br/><br/>

This issue happens because the Test Runner for Java extension requires the JUnit5 BOM and JUnit Platform Launcher to run the test cases. On the very first run of testing code with coverage, the Test Runner would have crashed and given an error in the Debug console on how it can't find the files it requires. This is then cached, and on subsequent runs, the Test Runner skips the test cases to avoid crashing, which is why it shows 0% code coverage, as it does not actually run the test for the code and ends in less than a few seconds. Step 3 helps to reset the test environment to be rid of the cached error.

I hope this helps 😄 

⚠️ Note: This only works for the "Test Runner for Java" extension. You may require a different solution if you're using another extension for running the tests.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/559#issuecomment-4096530943" expanded>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/38#issuecomment-3787777215" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You can view and restore previous versions of a file by right-clicking the file and selecting **_Local History → Show History_**. This will display the file’s edit history. However, I have not used this feature extensively for long-term history, so I am not certain how far back the changes can be traced.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3790140408" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Let's consider a file empty.txt that is initially committed empty and pushed to the remote repository. After adding the line "hi" and committing locally, performing a revert on the second commit creates a new commit that removes "hi", so the file appears empty locally, while the remote repository remains unchanged until the revert is pushed. A soft reset moves HEAD back to the first commit but keeps "hi" staged, whereas a mixed reset also moves HEAD back but leaves "hi" unstaged while still present in the file. Finally, a hard reset moves HEAD back and discards all changes, restoring the file to its original empty state. These observations are consistent with my experiments during T4L5: Rewriting History to Start Over, which helped clarify the effects of different reset and revert operations.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/52#issuecomment-3793876747" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

According to the details provided, the Git Standard task can be successfully completed by ensuring that the repository contains a minimum of 5 commits and that the most recent 5 commit messages fully comply with the Git conventions outlined in the notes. The commit messages should be clear, sufficiently descriptive, and free from any easily identifiable standard violations. 

Please refer to the guidelines outlined [here](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/git.html) as you may have overlooked some of aspects (such as the use of imperative mood in subject line, limiting the character count to 50 characters, etc).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/81#issuecomment-3804692856" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi,

regarding your questions:

a) According to the Level-7 task requirements, the task list can be stored in any file format (e.g. .txt, .csv), as long as it does not require users to install additional software.

b) The file should be stored locally on the user’s machine, since it represents the user’s personal task list rather than shared project data.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/92#issuecomment-3814643974" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Just to chip in, I feel that it may not be very user-friendly for the application to terminate or completely discard all data when the file is corrupted. Doing so could result in users losing all their data and being left confused about what went wrong or what steps to take next. Instead, the application could attempt to recover any valid entries first, and then reinitialise a new Duke.txt file using the recovered data. This approach would improve robustness, as it allows the application to handle partial corruption gracefully rather than failing abruptly on startup.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/126#issuecomment-3838166532" expanded>

**9 :fas-comment:**

Hi,

Personally I think `parts` and `timeParts` are still reasonably clear since their meaning becomes obvious from how they’re used. 

One small readability improvement could be removing the magic literal in substring(5). You could either:
- replace it with `substring("event".length())`
- define "event" as a static constant (e.g. `EVENT_KEYWORD`) and use `substring(EVENT_KEYWORD.length())`

This makes the intent clearer and improves maintainability if the command keyword changes later. You could also consider abstracting this logic into a helper method to better follow SLAP, so the method operates at a consistent level of abstraction. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/540#issuecomment-4061968980" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi,

From what I understand, it’s fine to have multiple triangles pointing to the same superclass or interface in a UML diagram. Each subclass (or implementing class) can have its own generalization/realization arrow pointing to it, and merging the lines into a single triangle is mainly just for visual clarity.

That said, the actual implementation may depend on the programming language. For example, in Java a class cannot extend multiple classes (though it can implement multiple interfaces), while languages like Python support multiple inheritance.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/541#issuecomment-4062375956" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with @MrMarshall12 and would like to add on to the point. Based on my understanding of LSP, the key idea is that a subclass should preserve the behavioural expectations or contract of its superclass, rather than simply being more or less restrictive.

Even if `Rectangle` extends `Square` and allows different height and width values, this could still violate LSP because code written for `Square` may rely on the invariant that height must equal width. For example, if a method calls `Square.resize(2, 4)` expecting an exception, substituting it with a `Rectangle` object that accepts those values could lead to behaviour that the code did not anticipate.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/572" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Differentiate three kinds of exceptions**
</div>

Hi Prof, during revision I wanted to check whether my understanding is correct: a checked exception is something a program is generally expected to anticipate and recover from, usually caused by normal external conditions such as invalid user input or a missing file; an Error is a more serious problem coming from the runtime environment or system itself, such as hardware failure or the JVM running out of memory, so the application usually cannot meaningfully recover from it; and a RuntimeException usually indicates a bug in the program logic itself, such as passing null where a valid object is expected, so instead of treating it as a normal recoverable situation, the main solution is usually to fix the code. Thanks!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/456#issuecomment-3948586724" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/568#issuecomment-4105920407" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Please correct me if I’m wrong. My understanding is that when the video says white-box testing cannot reveal errors due to “missing paths,” it is referring to paths that should exist from the specification point of view but are not present in the implementation at all. Since white-box testing derives tests mainly from the code that already exists, it is good at exercising existing branches, control flow, and logic, but it may not naturally reveal a behavior that ought to be there but was never implemented. For example, if the spec says an account should be locked after 5 failed login attempts, but the code only handles “correct password” and “wrong password” without any lockout logic, a white-box tester may thoroughly cover all existing code paths and still miss the fact that a required path is absent. In contrast, a spec-based/black-box test would be more likely to catch this, because it starts from what the system is supposed to do rather than what the code currently does.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/571#issuecomment-4109047775" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Thanks! That helps alot!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/575#issuecomment-4116028863" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think more defensive code is worth it when misuse is likely and the cost of failure is high.
For example, at public APIs, module boundaries, or places where important invariants must always hold, checks like null validation, defensive copies, and enforced constraints can prevent invalid states from entering the system. But I would not use heavy defensive programming everywhere. For private methods or well-controlled internal code, tests and documentation are often enough, especially if extra checks make the code more complex or slower.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/572#issuecomment-4116032541" expanded>
<div slot="header">

Hi, I’d also lean toward n-tier/layered architecture as the default for a student project, mainly because it keeps responsibilities clear and makes the code easier to test and maintain. At the same time, I don’t think client-server is really an alternative to n-tier since they describe different things and can work together. Based on this week’s topics, it seems more useful to treat MVC and Observer as patterns you can apply on top of that base, since both are meant to reduce coupling and keep the UI and data interactions cleaner.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/717#issuecomment-4286882511" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

my answer: separation of Concerns is the broader principle: split the system into distinct parts so each part addresses a separate concern, such as persistence, security, or a feature. Single responsibility Principle is narrower: each class should have only one reason to change. SoC is about how concerns are separated across the system or architecture, while SRP applies that same thinking at the level of an individual class/module. In that sense, SRP is a more specific way of achieving SoC.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3793807421" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hey, heres my analogy of whats going on if it helps 

git revert
git revert undoes a commit by creating a new commit that reverses the changes introduced by the original one. The original commit remains in the history, but its effects are cancelled out by the new revert commit. This means the working directory reflects the undone changes, while the commit history remains intact. Because no history is rewritten, revert is safe to use on commits that have already been pushed to a shared remote repository.
Analogy:
Think of it like publishing an errata for a lecture/teaching session you had. You cannot just scrap the whole slides because of a small error because it would be wasteful and people depend on the slides so you add in an errata to let everyone know.

git reset --soft
A soft reset moves the HEAD pointer back to the specified commit but leaves both the staging area and the working directory unchanged. The changes from the undone commit remain staged and ready to be committed again. This is useful when you want to modify or squash recent commits without losing any work or changing file contents.
Analogy:
This is like deciding not to officially release a set of slides yet, but keeping all your edits neatly prepared and ready to publish again. Nothing is lost; you are just rewinding the record of what was officially released.

git reset --mixed (default)
A mixed reset moves the HEAD pointer back and also resets the staging area to match the target commit, but it leaves the working directory unchanged. The changes from the undone commit remain in the files but are unstaged. This is commonly used when you want to redo how changes are staged or split a commit into smaller pieces.
Analogy:
I would say its more like you publish a set of slides but theres quite a decent chunk of erratas that makes it easier to just unpublish the slides and change it and haven’t slated it for republishing yet? Sorry if this doesn’t make a lot of sense haha. But yeah its too big a task for a separate errata slides and would make the readers life annoying if they had to keep swapping between the two

git reset --hard
A hard reset moves the HEAD pointer back and resets both the staging area and the working directory to exactly match the target commit. All changes made after that commit are permanently discarded from both staging and files. This operation is destructive and should be used with caution, especially if the changes are not recoverable.
Analogy:
This is like deleting all edits you made after a certain lecture and restoring the slides exactly to how they were at before that point. Perhaps because you made the slides in a hurry and theres too many errors to be able to fix it reasonably and its just easier to start from scratch


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3878449380" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with most points @AK-matrix made,
I’ve found that AI tools are a massive time-saver for the "boring" stuff like repetitive refactoring, satisfying Checkstyle, and generating formulaic JavaDocs, but I’ve also learned the hard way that treating it as a black box is dangerous. While it’s great for quick tweaks, AI can easily "corrupt" your system by suggesting logic that looks right but fundamentally clashes with your existing architecture or library versions. I actually had a situation where an AI-suggested change broke my entire project, and I had to spend hours manually resetting everything and finding a different way to implement the feature from scratch. It’s a great co-pilot for boilerplate, but you have to be ready to vet every line it writes; otherwise, a "five-minute fix" can quickly turn into a night of debugging a codebase meltdown.
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I have used AI, specifically Claude to help improve the GUI by generating some FXML files, and most of the CSS. 

It is important to have some understanding of FXML files before prompting AI, as they might generate other types of solution that might be a completely new concept, and not modular to the code. If that happens, you need to prompt the AI in the right direction.

For the CSS portion, generally the AI helps a lot, as it keeps to a specific color scheme. Hence, the overall product looks presentable.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/562#issuecomment-4105038583" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi,

One of the examples I can think of where exceptions are more useful than assertions is at API boundaries, such as a frontend calling a  backend with an invalid request.

For assertions, the backend will silently skip this assertion (assuming assertions are disabled in production), and return a generic error message. 

However, in the case for exception, the check for this error, and return a detailed error message.

This makes exceptions the better choice at API boundaries, as it will result in more detailed error messages.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/37" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6. :fas-info-circle: Git-Mastery T4L5 hp-force-push**
</div>

Step 4 of `HANDS-ON: Force-push commits` failed for me:
```
$ git push -f origin main
fatal: unable to access 'https://github.com/.../gitmastery-samplerepo-things/': Could not resolve host: github.com
```

The provided remote link seem to be missing .git:
```
$ git remote -v
origin	https://github.com/.../gitmastery-samplerepo-things (fetch)
origin	https://github.com/.../gitmastery-samplerepo-things (push)
```

I amended `origin` to https://github.com/.../gitmastery-samplerepo-things.git and the force push works as expected.
</panel>

1. for operations I included the parameter name eg. +drive(speed: int)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/157#issuecomment-3857926098" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

For this:
'''java
scrollPane.vvalueProperty().bind(dialogContainer.heightProperty());
'''
vvalue is turned into a bound property. When the user tries to change vvalue by scrolling, the binding forces it back to a certain value. Hence, scrolling doesn't appear to do anything.

The second approach:
'''java
Platform.runLater(() -&gt; scrollPane.setVvalue(1.0));
'''
does not bind vvalue. After each user input, the VBox height doesn’t update immediately. `Platform.runLater(...)` makes it update at the next layout pass, once `scrollPane` knows the new scroll range. So both user scroll and auto-scroll are preserved.

source: my understanding + AI
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/155#issuecomment-3858094826" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

for enhanced/lambda switch specifically:

> Lambda-style switch statements/expressions can have indented case blocks (as shown below):
> '''
> switch (condition) ``{``
>     case ABC -&gt; method("1");
>     case DEF -&gt; method("2");
>     case XYZ -&gt; method("3");
>     default -&gt; method("0");
> ``}``
> '''
source: [coding standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#layout)

so I believe it's optional
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/174#issuecomment-3869369872" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think it also depends on the context. Option 1 allows common validations to be abstracted, while 2 is suitable for checking more specific rules. We can use 1 for exposed methods (eg. taking inputs), and use 2 in critical internal areas (eg. calling DB).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/437#issuecomment-3940239164" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Ah ok, thank you
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I suspect a file type mismatch. You can check if your image is a png file -- iPhone images are often HEIC, and renaming it to githubusername.png may not have actually converted it.

Also double-check that the image is in the folder `docs/images/` and you reference the path like so: `<img src="images/githubusername.png">`.

</panel>

Hii! I've tested your chatbot on **Win** and here are my thoughts:

1. Overall good job! The gif profile picture looks cute, and I like the part when you start a conversation, your name should be firstly typed in, which makes me feel that I am really talking a chatbot (though for this one we can only use it to handle simple task management)
2. I noticed that when I propmted "todo" but without a task description, I cannot really send it out, which confused me a little bit as I thought it was because my laptop was frozen. Therefore you may consider to just allow users to send a task even without necessary attributes and then let the bot to respond to it (e.g. "Missing task description").

3. I also noticed that when I entered an invalid prompt, aside from error messages, a full list of tasks is also shown, which personally I do not think is really necessary, as it looks a bit to long and too much (because in the case when user input is invalid, you want the programme to show the error messages and thus guide the user to fix that problem, and showing the whole list of tasks should be done by "list" instead.

<img width="490" height="400" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2c9dcbd6-bed6-4faa-a133-86403dd8a339" />

4. The last suggestion I could make is that when I prompt "bye" the program does not exit automatically: I have to click on "X" at the top right corner to "truly" exit the program. Perhaps this is where you can consider to fix.

<img width="503" height="388" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5d9e0b5d-8dd3-424c-a4f5-da8297d72477" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3943768963" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

From my perspective, different AI tools have different usages in different contexts, and it is better to try on various AI instead of using only one (e.g. ChatGPT). For example, personally I use GPT5.2 to debug, Opus to handle complicate coding tasks, Gemini to handle text-related tasks or to handle some more light-weighted tasks (e.g. render a static website). 

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/162" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Java Coding Standard camelCase for Methods**
</div>

Under the "Names representing methods must be verbs and written in camelCase." section of the [Java Coding Standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html), I think an exception should be made for constructor methods, since class names must be PascalCase and nouns, meaning constructor method names must also be PascalCase and nouns, instead of camelCase and verbs.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/103" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: 💡IntelliJ Reformat Text Wrapping Settings**
</div>

Aside from the following [recommended IntelliJ code style tweaks](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/intellijCodeStyle.html), I believe that under Editor > Code Style > Java > Wrapping and Braces, one should also replace the default "Do not wrap" with "Wrap if long" for each of the various subsections, e.g.
<img width="542" height="394" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a66561a5-2917-4e42-9bc7-27109e5abee3" />

Without doing this, it seems that performing reformat on IntelliJ (via Ctrl+Alt+L) may not properly wrap lines even if they exceed the hard limit of 120 chars, leading to lines that exceed 120 chars even after reformatting is triggered.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/85" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6. :fas-info-circle: For Each Loop Guidelines**
</div>


I took a look at the [Java coding standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html), but saw that there was no mention of for-each loops (e.g. `for (int element : intArray) ``{`` ... ``}```).

As such, I just wanted to check whether there any guidelines specific to the use of for-each loops? I presume that we're allowed to use them, but I'm unsure if there's any specific format/usage requirements.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/84" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Java Conventions for Whitespace**
</div>


Under the [Java coding standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html) section on "White space within a statement", it's stated that:
> Colons should be surrounded by white space when
> used as a binary/ternary operator.
> Does not apply to `switch x:`. Semicolons in `for`
> statements should be followed by a space character.

I'm a bit confused, because `switch x:` seems to be invalid Java syntax, given that the syntax for `switch` is `switch (condition) ``{`` ... ``}```? Is this statement intended to be `case x:` instead?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/66" expanded>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/116#issuecomment-3828293658" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In my opinion, it might be better to throw an exception to indicate failure (or success in the absence of an exception) rather than returning a boolean to indicate success. Doing so would force the caller to explicitly handle the failure (since the returned boolean can be easily ignored), and would also allow for richer failure information to be passed to the caller.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3903733994" expanded>

Personally, I had a great experience using Codex to automatically generate JUnit test cases. It managed to generate 33 test methods one-shot, which covered all happy paths and edge cases for parsing all commands, and I only had to make minor adjustments. I would say that AI could be used to effectively automate some of the testing code generation, since much of it can be repetitive and boilerplate in nature.

**8. :fas-info-circle: Confusion over revert and reset**
</div>



Hi, I'm a bit confused about the git commands of revert and reset, so I'm asking this to confirm my understanding:
Let's say I have a local file empty.txt, I stage and commit it in my local repo, push it to a remote repo, add one line "hi" to it, then stage and commit it in my local repo again.
Am I right to say that:

1. Reverting the commit will basically make a new commit that erases the line in my local repo, but not in the remote repo until it (the new commit) is pushed. When I open the txt locally now, it will be empty.
2. A soft reset will be as if I staged the changes, but haven't committed it yet. Opening the txt after a soft reset will show "hi".
3. A mixed reset will be as if I added "hi", but haven't staged or committed it. Opening the txt will show "hi".
4. A hard reset will erase changes everywhere, and opening the txt will show an empty file.

Thank you!
</panel>

### 45. KAMA..TOSH `@AK-matrix` (15 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/224" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

• **Link to JAR file:** https://github.com/AK-matrix/ip/releases/tag/A-Release

• **Which OS'es to test on:** Mac | Linux

• **Things to check specifically:**

1. Whether the application runs correctly on Mac/Linux OS
2. Whether commands work and invalid inputs produce the correct error messages
3. Whether contact commands (especially `edit`) persist across sessions, i.e., data is saved and correctly loaded after restarting the app

• **User guide:** https://ak-matrix.github.io/ip


**Guide for testers:**

• Post screenshots from your smoke tests
• Mention which OS you used for testing

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96#issuecomment-3818554513" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Building on the points above, I think the key question is how we view `TaskList`. If it’s just a thin wrapper over a collection, then returning either a generic list or a `TaskList` doesn’t really change much.

If we instead treat `TaskList` as a more meaningful object, I’d still prefer having `Storage` return a generic collection. That keeps `Storage` focused only on reading data, and makes it easier to change or evolve `TaskList` later without needing to touch the storage code. It also makes testing `Storage` simpler, since it doesn’t depend on higher-level behaviour.

The domain logic and checks can still live fully inside `TaskList` once it’s constructed, so the separation stays clear.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/97#issuecomment-3818642124" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Did this start after the package increment? If so, it’s likely related.

When a class is moved into a package, its fully qualified name changes. If the run configuration wasn’t updated, IntelliJ will still try to run the class using the old name and fail.

You can try closing the run configuration and running the class directly from the file. IntelliJ should regenerate the configuration with the correct class name.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/133#issuecomment-3842948789" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

My UI incorporates ImageView handling, hover effects on buttons, and external CSS linked to FXML for styling.

<img width="400" height="600" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b30bc863-49ce-4477-acf7-3a5999c56ee0" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/139#issuecomment-3843090425" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As mentioned above, I agree this approach makes sense here given the number and variety of commands involved. In cases where commands remain very small and uniform, though, introducing many separate command classes can add structural overhead, making the codebase harder to navigate without much practical gain.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/170#issuecomment-3869157120" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As said above, this case is already fully covered (and obvious), so an extra error branch isn’t needed.
But in conditions where it isn’t immediately obvious that every case has been handled, keeping an error branch can be useful as a safety net (even if theoretically unreachable) because future edits or misunderstood logic might accidentally break the exhaustiveness.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3876083832" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I’ve learnt that AI tools can be a huge time-saver when refactoring code, especially for repetitive tasks or small tweaks across many files. They’re great for exploring new libraries, features, and tools I might not have discovered on my own. I’ve realized it’s really important not to treat AI as a black box, understanding its suggestions and debugging yourself is key, otherwise small issues can snowball into bigger problems. AI can be a great teacher, showing coding patterns and best practices, but you still need to question and verify its suggestions to truly learn.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/219#issuecomment-3909764744" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi! I’m using Windows.

All your commands work correctly for me, including 'event' and 'deadline' (I tested both date formats). I think the issue mentioned in the previous post happens because the date must follow the format `1 Feb 2026, 5:00PM` exactly, the time is required and the comma is mandatory.

The command examples in the README don’t seem to follow this exact format, so if someone uses them, the 'event' and 'deadline' commands fail. 

You may want to update the README examples to match the required format to avoid confusion. Otherwise, everything works fine!

<img width="370" height="560" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ee04c469-6a13-4fde-88c2-a03a1e30359d" />

<img width="370" height="560" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/56ac7e0c-9159-42fe-9473-96f237a08111" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/234#issuecomment-3914582759" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @keiran0 

The OS I'm using for testing is Linux (Ubuntu 24.04) to provide additional cross-platform coverage beyond the usual Windows/macOS environments.

All core commands execute correctly, and the program responds appropriately to invalid inputs. In particular, invalid indices and date inputs are handled with proper error messages.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/562#issuecomment-4109513453" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree with the points about API boundaries and failing fast. I think a cleaner way to see it is about where the error comes from.

Assertions are for internal assumptions within the same codebase. If they fail, it means there is a bug that should have been caught during development.

Exceptions make more sense when you are enforcing a contract across a boundary. Even if the caller is another developer, it is still external to your module, so misuse can happen in production. Since assertions can be disabled, relying on them there would be unsafe.

So yes, I think exceptions are appropriate for developer errors when they come from outside the class or module, especially when continuing execution could lead to a bad state.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/587#issuecomment-4136252424" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think if relaxing validation significantly degrades duplicate detection, it might suggest that the current duplicate logic is quite dependent on exact matches. Instead of framing it purely as a tradeoff between strict validation and usability, I wonder if we could keep validation reasonably strict while exploring ways to make duplicate detection less rigid, such as using near-match warnings. This might shift the focus from “validation vs duplicates” to “whether to block or warn on potential duplicates”, which seems consistent with the guideline’s emphasis on weighing different types of user pain.

I also feel that a warning-based approach could make things more convenient for most users, since valid inputs that differ slightly in format are less likely to be rejected. At the same time, the risk of incorrect duplicates might still be manageable, as users can confirm or override warnings when needed, so any issues would likely be occasional rather than frequent.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/647#issuecomment-4231185398" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree that having clearly specified syntax and examples in the UG is important, and helps show that the behaviour is intentional.

However, based on the PE rubric, I think that alone may not be sufficient. A behaviour can still be considered a bug if it does not handle legitimate user behaviour or differs from normal expectations, even if documented.

For example, inputs like "+65xxxxxxxx" or positions with spaces seem like fairly standard real-world formats, so rejecting them might still be considered a bug under “legitimate user behaviour is not handled”.

So I think it ultimately depends on both how well it is documented and whether the constraint aligns with reasonable user expectations, as per the rubric.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/75" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: 💡Sometimes JDT.LS may mess up the package namespace, heres how to fix it.**
</div>

This is intended for those who use jdt.ls as the java language server in their setup.

Sometimes, jdt.ls may show errors that your package definition does not match your file location. This seems to be related to jdt.ls wrongly constructed the classpath in its data folder. Here is how to fix it:

1. First, navigate to the jdt.ls data folder. [On my windows machine, it turns out to be `%APPDATA%\jdtls\jdtls-xxxxxxxxxxx`.]
1. Then navigate to your project dir. [Would be something like `ip_4c02b380`].
1. There is a `bin` folder. In this folder, arrange the `.class` files into the same directory structure as your `.java` files in your project.
1. Then open the `.classpath` file, delete all other entries with `&lt;classpathentry kind="src" path=.../>` except for `&lt;classpathentry kind="src" path="_/src/main/java"/>`.
1. Then it should work.

Hopefully this can help those encountering the same issue.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/68" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: 💡For the exercise mix-messy-docs in T6L5.**
</div>

If you have accidentally merged `feature-search` WITH fast forwarding, then just reset. Because the verifier checks the first & second REFLOG for whether you have done the mergings correctly.

I have been confused by why it always errors out with `You need to merge 'feature-delete' second!`, MULTIPLE TIMES, so I checked the verifier code.

Source: https://github.com/git-mastery/exercises/blob/main/mix_messy_docs/verify.py

Suggestion: maybe make the verifier more flexible?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/35#issuecomment-3797643054" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

So it turns out that maybe we just only need to set a few environment variables.
'''batch
@set PATH=C:\Program Files\ojdkbuild\java-17-openjdk-17.0.3.0.6-1\bin;%PATH%
@set JAVA_HOME=C:\Program Files\ojdkbuild\java-17-openjdk-17.0.3.0.6-1
@set PROMPT=(cs2103t) %PROMPT%
'''
And for configuring jdt language server on Neovim: (latest jdtls requires Java 21 to run, but we are working with Java 17 in this course, so we need to do this setting)
'''lua
vim.lsp.config(
    'jdtls',
    ``{``
        rootmarkers = ``{`` '.git' ``}``, -- if you have installed nvim-lspconfig then maybe you do not need this line
        cmd = ``{``
            'jdtls.bat',
            '--java-executable',
            'C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\jdk-21.0.9.10-hotspot\\bin\\java.exe',
        ``}``,
        filetypes = ``{`` 'java' ``}``,
        settings = ``{``
            java = ``{``
                configuration = ``{``
                    runtimes = ``{``
                        ``{``
                            name = 'JavaSE-21',
                            path = 'C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\jdk-21.0.9.10-hotspot'
                        ``}``,
                        ``{``
                            name = 'JavaSE-17',
                            path = 'C:\\Program Files\\ojdkbuild\\java-17-openjdk-17.0.3.0.6-1',
                            default = true
                        ``}``,
                    ``}``,
                ``}``,
            ``}``,
        ``}``,
    ``}``
)
'''
</panel>
