### 48. JAIN..ANYA `@ananyaj1515` (15 posts) <span class=text-warning>:octicon-eye:</span>
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/708" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Penalty Calculation for Documentation Bugs**
</div>

Hi, I understand that penalties for functional bugs are based on bug density rather than absolute bug count.

Is a similar approach used for documentation bugs as well, relative to each member’s contribution to the documentation? For example, are documentation bugs evaluated based on bug density with respect to how much a member contributed?

Thanks for the clarification!
<img width="1024" height="449" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c6e3cc82-cb2a-4595-94de-74ec8e2e9293" />


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/706" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Assignment for Duplicate Bugs and Sub-Issues**
</div>

Hi, I’d like to clarify the expected workflow for handling duplicate bugs.

If a duplicate bug is assigned to a specific person, do all related sub-issues also need to be assigned to the same person? Or can the sub-issues remain unassigned since the parent issue is already assigned?

Thanks!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/674" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Finding two bugs in the same UML diagram**
</div>



I have found two bugs associated with two different entities in the same UML diagram. Should I report them as two separate bugs or one?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/609" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Javadoc comments for classes**
</div>

Hi all, I had a quick question about Javadoc conventions for classes.

Is it acceptable for class-level Javadoc comments to use verbs like “adds” or “changes”? These feel more action-oriented (like method descriptions), whereas I thought class descriptions should typically be noun-like (describing what the class represents rather than what it does).

I noticed that the remark tutorial uses phrasing like this, so I’m unsure what the preferred convention is.

Should class Javadocs:

* Describe the class in a noun-like way (e.g., “Represents…”), or
* Is it okay to describe behavior using verbs (e.g., “Adds a remark to…”)?

Thanks!

<img width="983" height="351" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd27e2b9-503b-46dc-876a-40a31044179b" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/646#issuecomment-4230893338" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The [PE-D](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/tp-ped.html#tp-practical-exam-dry-run-pe-d) admin page mentions that “even bugs inherited from AB3 need to be fixed.” In this context, restricting valid name inputs by disallowing commonly used special characters such as `-` or `/` may be considered a bug. These characters frequently appear in real-world names (e.g., `Anne-Marie` or `s/o`), and rejecting them limits the application’s practicality and accuracy in handling legitimate user data.
</panel>

</panel>

While trying to enable plantUml on my computer, i was faced with this error

<img width="1241" height="631" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a635685e-45d4-46d5-99f4-eb7ef4e7af04" /><br> 
Following this [stackoverflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54413962/cant-render-component-diagram-with-plantuml-in-intellij), just need to do an install of graphviz on your system. Hope this helps people who have the same issue!

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/40#issuecomment-3789193836" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hey Ram, having `logs` and `logs/` in `.gitignore` will refer to the same thing. 
There can only be 1 instance of `log`, whether it is a directory or a file. 
```
jedeon@JDFedora:~/temp$ mkdir asdf
jedeon@JDFedora:~/temp$ touch asdf
jedeon@JDFedora:~/temp$ ls
asdf
jedeon@JDFedora:~/temp$ ll
total 0
drwxr-xr-x. 1 jedeon jedeon 0 Jan 23 17:08 asdf
```

Additionally, 
```
logs              # Ignores all files and directories named logs
*.bak             # Ignores all .bak files anywhere
```
would be the same thing execution, just that `.bak` would be more of Regex usecase

The only use case i could foresee would be when the user does not know whether logs is a directory or a file (which i think will be quite rare haha)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/50#issuecomment-3791159078" expanded>

83463e0 HEAD@``{``1``}``: merge daughter: Merge made by the 'ort' strategy.
a87658a HEAD@``{``2``}``: commit: Mention Morty is grandson
b38b5c0 HEAD@``{``3``}``: checkout: moving from son-in-law to main
95d09df (son-in-law) HEAD@``{``4``}``: commit: Add Jerry
b38b5c0 HEAD@``{``5``}``: checkout: moving from main to son-in-law
b38b5c0 HEAD@``{``6``}``: checkout: moving from daughter to main
e9f91d9 (daughter) HEAD@``{``7``}``: commit: Add Beth
b38b5c0 HEAD@``{``8``}``: checkout: moving from main to daughter
b38b5c0 HEAD@``{``9``}``: commit: Add morty
8a24370 HEAD@``{``10``}``: commit: Add Rick
3cb5915 HEAD@``{``11``}``: commit (initial): Set initial state
```

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/739#issuecomment-4334329767" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<img width="2667" height="1440" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f761f5b4-1168-4a08-adf9-d616c462636b" /><br>
I got this, for bidirectional association, the main one for this question would be Lid and Box, where they are both pointing to each other. 
Multiplicity for the array of parts would be limited to 10, since it is already declared in the parts variable, so i would put 0..10, since its an array and does not need to be specifically 10 at all times.
Hope it helps!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/766#issuecomment-4365504306" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The multiplicity at Door for d is `1`, whereas multiplicity at Door for b is `*`. By having `*`, it can imply that there is no door tagged to a table, which is true as shown in the object diagram. However, for d, since it is explicitly 1, there must be a door tagged to the table, which is not shown in the object diagram. 
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/141" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Unable to import JavaFX into iP**
</div>

After completing the JavaFX tutorial, I decided to transfer the .java .fxml and .css files over to my iP files along with updating my `build.gradle` file to include the dependencies as instructed in the tutorial.

However after copying everything over and reloading VSCode to ensure by `build.gradle` changes were in effect, none of the JavaFX imports were recognised in any of the files with Visual Studio Code stating that "The import javafx cannot be resolved."

This was in the output panel of VSCode:
```
* What went wrong:
Could not open cp_init generic class cache for initialization script 'C:\Users\YEOJIA~1\AppData\Local\Temp\97b18158b79a5198b3377caf4b300f06852313eed7a636a3434469cd2df34745.gradle' (C:\Users\Yeo Jian Ming\.gradle\caches\7.6.2\scripts\a3xcgb5qmbsfyaeakcq21zhtm).
> BUG! exception in phase 'semantic analysis' in source unit '_BuildScript_' Unsupported class file major version 65
```

I researched online as well as found a post of someone experiencing a similar issue on the forum and thought it might be a issue with my gradle and jdk versions? Although prior to transferring the files from the javafx tutorial over I had never encountered this issue. I am running Gradle version 7.6.2 and Java version 17.0.17.

My current `build.gradle`:
```
plugins ``{``
    id 'java'
    id 'application'
    id 'checkstyle'
    id 'com.github.johnrengelman.shadow' version '7.1.2'
``}``

repositories ``{``
    mavenCentral()
``}``

dependencies ``{``
    testImplementation group: 'org.junit.jupiter', name: 'junit-jupiter-api', version: '5.10.0'
    testRuntimeOnly group: 'org.junit.jupiter', name: 'junit-jupiter-engine', version: '5.10.0'
    implementation group: 'com.joestelmach', name: 'natty', version: '0.13'

    String javaFxVersion = '17.0.7'

    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-base', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'win'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-base', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'mac'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-base', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'linux'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-controls', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'win'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-controls', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'mac'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-controls', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'linux'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-fxml', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'win'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-fxml', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'mac'
    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-fxml', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'linux'

    implementation group: 'org.openjfx', name: 'javafx-graphics', version: javaFxVersion, classifier: 'linux'
``}``

test ``{``
    useJUnitPlatform()

    testLogging ``{``
        events "passed", "skipped", "failed"

        showExceptions true
        exceptionFormat "full"
        showCauses true
        showStackTraces true
        showStandardStreams = false
    ``}``
``}``

application ``{``
    mainClass.set("epsilon.Launcher")
``}``

checkstyle ``{``

``}``

shadowJar ``{``
    archiveBaseName = "epsilon"
    archiveClassifier = null
``}``

run ``{``
    standardInput = System.in
``}``
```

Please help.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/141#issuecomment-3851359572" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @breezuu, thank you for your advice. I swtiched to using version 8.5 for gradle. The import errors have disappeared though they will occasionally show up again without reason before disappearing after a few seconds without me doing anything. Other than that there seems to be no other issues.

After this change, while implementing my JavaFX code into my existing iP, I ran into a similar error as before where it was stating that my MainWindow controller could not be found. Initially I had thought this to be an extension to my prior problem of mismatched Gradle and JDK versions. However I realised that it was because my MainWindow.java file was now in a package and that needed to be reflected in the FXML file.

Thank you @itsapaulblem @Rahulvira @gwhrg @damithc for your responses to my queries. I will be closing this issue.
</panel>

**2. :fas-info-circle: Week 3 Progress Status for Admin Task (branch-A-CodingStandard & Git-Standard) not turning green**
</div>

I've done all the different increment and branch out accordingly for branch-A-CodingStandard. I've the relevant tags and branch in my local Git and did a push to remote as well. However my Git Progress is not turning green even though I've done it? 

<img width="773" height="133" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c85faa81-5176-4146-8e23-522ca9dd4773" />

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3796003618" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on what you've mentioned i think your understanding is right, as it is similar to what I've understand as followed:

A git revert doesn't remove the original commit but instead creates a new commit that undoes the changes made by the previous one. So this means the history actually stays intact, and the remote repo will only reflect this change after the new revert commit is pushed.

As for git reset, the behavior depends on the option used. A --soft reset moves the commit pointer back but keeps the changes staged, so the file will still contain 'hi'. 
A --mixed reset also moves the commit pointer back, but unstages the changes while keeping them in the working directory. Lastly, a --hard reset discards the commit and all local changes, returning the file to its previous empty state.

Hope this helps to clarify your doubts.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/102#issuecomment-3822513120" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> According to the IP dashboard troubleshooting, your branch is ahead by the master by 1? Below is the suggested remedy, I do not know if it will solve your issue.
> 
> "Note the numbers n|m shown for each branch. If the m is not zero, that means the branch is ahead of the master branch by that number of commits, and hence, not considered fully merged (only merged branches are detected by the dashboard). Reasons: You did not push the branch after merging it. Remedy: After merging a branch to the master branch, you need to push both branches to the fork. You pushed commits to the branch after merging it to the master branch. Remedy: Either delete those extra commits from the branch (both locally and in the fork) or merge the branch to the master branch again."

@prickloll right , okay i think that should be the issue i didnt realize it was ahead by 1 commit , i've git merge and and resolve the conflict and git push to master and the remote branch now. It is no longer ahead of master. Will wait for the next Git Progress Update and check on it. Thank you @prickloll @Annazx 
</panel>

OS : Mac

1. I like the UI design. The app adjusts smoothly even when I change its window size.

2. I have tested all the commands and shortcuts provided, and almost everything works perfectly.

<img width="300" height="300" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/93734c13-7f84-4d2c-accd-245dbbdc87c1" />

<img width="300" height="300" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ee521841-6d08-4390-b8ef-cf9bb44c19fd" />

3. When I type event/deadline <task name>, instead of showing the command format, it displays “event cannot be empty,” which I think might confuse users who don’t know the correct command format.

<img width="408" height="254" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/db37a8c6-6794-4cfd-a344-a5f7941d05b1" />

4. When I type b, it shows the “bye” message, but it doesn’t exit automatically. Also, b is not listed as a shortcut in the user guide.

<img width="388" height="499" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4a368f50-fcca-452e-a0ef-2482ff7d3b1a" />

5. Currently, the system is case-sensitive. Uppercase and lowercase letters are treated as different inputs. When I enter the same data, it says I’ve already entered it before. Overall, it is cool and good!

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/574#issuecomment-4116155650" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Interesting question! I had the same doubt. Correct me if I’m wrong.

I drew part of AddCommandParser and ArgumentTokenizer sequence diagrams.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ec252177-e15a-486a-8222-ea5fddeef27f)

For static methods, we don’t call them on an object. We call them on the class itself. So in the diagram, we label the class with &lt;&lt;class>> if it contains static methods.

In my diagram, tokenize and findAllPrefixPositions are static methods. However, we can’t tell from the sequence diagram alone that they are static right? We can only tell from the class diagram.
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 54. ANNA..N XI `@Annazx` (13 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/578" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Auto-resizing JavaFX window**
</div>

Hi,

I’m a bit confused about the warning regarding auto-resizing the app window:

> “If you set the app to auto-size to a specific size at launch, it can go out of screen in smaller screens or at lower resolution, with no way to close or resize it, rendering it unusable.”

Does this mean we should avoid auto-resizing the window to fit its content, and instead use fixed width and height values for JavaFX windows?

In my case, I converted the AB3 help window into an in-app help guide. I set a fixed initial width and height, but made the window resizable so users can adjust it if needed. Would this approach be acceptable, or is there a better practice I should follow?

Thanks!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/252" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>


Link to JAR file: https://github.com/Annazx/ip/releases/tag/v0.2
Which OS'es to test on: Windows | Mac | Linux
Things to check specifically: Features in the [User Guide](https://github.com/Annazx/ip/tree/master/docs), graceful handling of erroneous input, resizing, and data persistence in between app loads.
Guide for testers:
Please post screenshots from your smoke tests and mention which OS you used for testing! 

Thank you very much!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815166652" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hey! I had the same thought. While static is definitely faster to write, I decided to use non-static (instance) classes for components like Ui and Storage for flexibility. I realized that if I kept them non-static, I could swap them out later more easily. Whereas if everything was static, the code would be 'hard-wired' to those specific classes, making it harder to test.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/102#issuecomment-3822357461" expanded>

### 55. LEE .. JON `@eejon` (13 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/529" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Are we allowed to add custom configuration for codecov?**
</div>

Since getting started on milestone v1.2, with actual code changes, my team and I have been seeing 0% patch coverage flags on files that are difficult or impractical to test. 

While looking into the warnings and reading up on CodeCov, I read that we could add a `codecov.yml` configuration which would help us ignore specific directories or file paths that can be safely excluded in the coverage reporting. 

Is it acceptable to add our custom `codecov.yml` configuration for the tP? And is it acceptable to exclude the `ui` package (and similar files) from coverage, given that JavaFX GUI automated testing requires additional tooling that isn't set up in the project yet?



</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/210" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: [link](https://github.com/eejon/ip/releases/tag/A-Release)
* Which OS'es to test on: Windows | ~~Mac~~ | Linux
* Things to check specifically:

| # | Test | Command to Run | Expected Result |
| - | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | List | `list` | Empty task list |
| 2 | Create Todo | `todo read book` | Successfully create todo |
| 3 | Create Deadline | `deadline return book /by 2026-02-20` | Successfully create deadline |
| 4 | Create Event Failure | `event project meeting /from 2027-02-18 /to 2026-02-18` | Unsuccessful because `from` is later than `to` |
| 5 | Create Event Success | `event project meeting /from 2026-02-18 /to 2026-02-18` | Successfully create event |
| 6 | Mark Todo | `mark 1` | Successfully mark Todo: read book. There should be an X indicator |
| 7 | Find task | `find book` | Returns Todo and Deadline task since both include book |
| 8 | View sorted list | `sort` | Returns a chronologically sorted list of tasks in this order: E &gt; D &gt; T |
| 9 | Delete task from the back | `delete 1` and `list` | New list should not include Todo; numbering of tasks should be updated |
| 10 | Exit and Re-open | `bye` and rerun the application with `list` | The program should exit after 3s and previously created data should persist (deadline and event). |


Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing

Appreciate any help and feedback!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/209" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on whether code quality guidelines apply to test code**
</div>

Hi all,

I wanted to clarify whether **code quality guidelines from the textbook apply to test code (user testing)**.

In my user testing code, I currently have a lot of `assertEquals(expected, actual)` calls with magic literals. For example:
'''java
assertEquals(1, taskManager.size());
assertEquals(1, storage.loadTasks().size());
assertEquals("read book", taskManager.get(0).getLabel());
'''

Based on the code quality guideline, I understand that the rationale behind avoiding magic literals is to give meaning to the literals so that it is easier to read and understand the code. But does this apply to unit tests? 

Personally, I would argue that it is **OK** to use magic literals here because we are just comparing output, the `assertEquals` implies that the literal is the expected output of the method. Also, there are too many different expected literal values, extracting all of them into named variables would make the tests **harder to read rather than easier**. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3793773064" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yes, I believe your understanding is correct! Here's what I understand:

### Git Reset
For `git reset <commit>`, it basically moves your current branch and `HEAD` pointer to the commit you specify. Like the others mentioned, there are 3 flags that we are taught:
- `--soft` : Moves `HEAD` but leaves your changes staged.
- `--mixed`: Default flag if no flag specified. Moves `HEAD` but leaves your changes unstaged.
- `--hard` : Moves `HEAD` and discards all changes you made. 

For example, 
```
git status // A. no changes at all. current commit: 7b1e4d9
echo "Hi" >> existingFile.txt // B. unstaged
git add existingFile.txt // C. staged
git commit -m "Add text to existingFile.txt" // creates a new commit a3f5c9e
```
```
// what each command would do, current commit: a3f5c9e
git reset --soft HEAD~1 // back to state like C (changes staged); current commit: 7b1e4d9
git reset HEAD~1 // back to state like B (changes unstaged); current commit: 7b1e4d9
git reset --hard HEAD~1 // back to state like A (clean); current commit: 7b1e4d9
```

### Git Revert
For `git revert <commit>`, you correctly pointed out that it creates a new commit that undoes the specified commit. 
For example,
```
echo "Hi" >> existingFile.txt // B. unstaged
git add existingFile.txt // C. staged
git commit -m "Add text to existingFile.txt" // creates a new commit a3f5c9e
// You make a few more commits
git revert a3f5c9e // This creates a new commit 7b1e4d9 that removes "Hi" from existingFile.txt
```

*Note:* Both of these commands apply to your local repository. But they differ in their behaviour when you push. Changes from `git revert` is safe to push to your remote repository because it does not rewrite history but just creates a new commit. But `git reset`rewrites history. So if you want to push changes from `git reset`, you'd need to use `git push --force`. But it's not safe if you have other collaborating on the repository because if they `git pull` (fetch + merge) before you force pushed the changes, then others would have a different history locally and it just snowballs. So if you're working in teams, it is better to do `git revert`. 

References: 
- https://git-scm.com/docs/git-reset
- https://git-scm.com/docs/git-revert
- https://www.linkedin.com/posts/curiouslearner_git-reset-vs-revert-activity-7344044512647774209-1rOO

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/133#issuecomment-3848022162" expanded>

**6 :fas-comment:**

Yes, I think it was meant to convey that for Java switch statements, there should not be a trailing whitespace after the colon for the `case` keyword. 
'''java
switch (condition) ``{``
    case x:// &lt;- no white space 
        statement
        break;
``}``
'''
To be safe, just follow the coding standard for switch statements [here](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#:~:text=The%20switch%20statement%20should%20have%20the%20following%20form).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/144#issuecomment-3848306677" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on my understanding, a class can be represented just by its class name. You can refer to this [exercise question](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/se-book-adapted/chapters/modeling.html#:~:text=Which%20classes%20are%20correct?) as I found it helpful!

To answer your second question, I agree with your conclusion.

On a separate note, I think you brought up a good point:
> If compartments may be omitted, and the attributes/operations themselves can also be omitted if deemed irrelevant to the purpose of the diagram,

In the end, I think it boils down to how you use the UML diagram. So while a certain design choice is valid, the key question is how the diagram's level of detail helps us. Personally, I would think it is best to be more detailed to reduce ambiguity.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/188#issuecomment-3888509820" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi,

I think you made a good attempt at abstraction and using method names with intent. Some points I did observe from your code:
1. I am not quite sure what your `checkEventCommand()` method does from how it is being called. I.e. You call `checkEventCommand` after you start processing (removing the event from the string) and proceed to call a different method to validate the date format. 
2. You are doing parsing, validation, date conversion, and object creation all at once. This is a little too much and also makes it difficult for testing.
3. Use of magic literals like `userInput.substring(5)` which I think can be improved.

Here are some suggestions I have:
1. Split the input into parts and process them separately. This will shorten your code and also naturally divide responsibility for each part of the user input. Perhaps your code could be structured something like this:
'''java
private static AddCommand parseAddEvent(String userInput) throws SandroneException ``{``
    validateEventFormat(userInput);

    String[] components = extractEventComponents(userInput);

    String desc = components[0];
    LocalDate from = parseDate(components[1]);
    LocalDate to = parseDate(components[2]);

    Task newEvent = new Event(desc, from, to);
    return new AddCommand(newEvent);
``}``
'''
2. Replace magic literals. For extracting commands, I personally split by a single white space to distinguish commands and arguments. Something like `String[] inputs = input.trim().split(" ", 2); // [command, arguments]`. This way I can parse each command separately and manage arguments separately. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/527#issuecomment-4040264008" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @TacoTako, I’ve been running into the same issue as well 😢

From what I understand, CodeCov tracks coverage automatically using its default configuration, so files like GUI components can sometimes get flagged as “not covered” even though they aren’t usually tested with automated tests.

Based on the FAQ in this week's project section [here](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week8/project.html#3-add-the-first-functionality-increment-thu-mar-12th-23-59),
> Ignore those warnings, and merge the PR (a member with admin permissions can merge a CI-failing PR). Suitable for cases such as,
> - the coverage drop is in code that is not normally covered by automated tests and/or 'not worth the effort to' test automatically (e.g., GUI).
> - you deem that automated testing of that part of the code can be done at a later time (i.e., not a priority at the current time).

So if the coverage drop is mainly due to GUI-related code, it should be fine to proceed with the merge.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/529#issuecomment-4040385605" expanded>
<div slot="header">

</div>

> “You may lower the test coverage targets set for Codecov as well.”

and 
> “Codecov is there to help you manage code coverage — it is not graded.”

Thank you! @damithc 
</panel>

</panel>

https://github.com/s6r6k/ip/releases/tag/v1.0

**Link to the User Guide:**
https://s6r6k.github.io/ip/

**Which OS to test on:** Windows | Mac | Linux

---

### Things to check

* The application launches successfully using:

  ```
  java -jar duke.jar
  ```
* The GUI loads properly without errors
* All commands work as described in the User Guide
* Core features (add, delete, list, mark/unmark, etc.) function correctly
* Invalid inputs show appropriate error messages
* Data is saved correctly and loads properly after restarting the app

---

### Guide for testers

1. Download the JAR from the release link
2. Copy it into an **empty folder**
3. Open a terminal in that folder and run:

   ```
   java -jar duke.jar
   ```
4. Try a few commands from the User Guide

If possible, please:

* Share a screenshot of the app running
* Mention which OS you tested on
* Report any bugs, unexpected behavior, or suggestions

Thank you so much for your help! I really appreciate your time 🙂


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/198#issuecomment-3901106063" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

While you can remove the .gitignore line, which excludes the jar file. It is not recommended, as the jar file can already be generated by users who have access to the repository. The purpose of the task is to ensure that the jar file is working with the new integrations that were added. If you do not wish to create a release within GitHub, you can follow the screenshot below from steps 1-3, excluding the portion on creating a new release. 

<img width="960" height="917" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cc9cb013-5839-4db4-8c7f-05e1a7dcd782" />

&hat; Screenshot from @Cxyu629 comment
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3903355551" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

During this project, I maintained a strictly supportive role for AI, prioritizing it for complex debugging rather than general code generation. Initially, I authored all test cases manually to ensure I grasped edge case logic, though this proved time-consuming as a first-time experience. AI became invaluable during major refactors following the addition of new features and packages. 

However, utilizing tools like Copilot for the GUI implementation required significant oversight. While it resolved specific bugs, it frequently removed necessary properties inadvertently. This experience highlighted the need for rigorous vetting to prevent a cycle of "feature regression" where the AI adds one fix but breaks existing functionality.

> A particularly interesting use of it is to let it run `.\gradlew checkstyleTest` and `.\gradlew checkstyleMain` and tell it to correct all the errors seen in the terminal output and run the command until there are no errors left. Now my CI passes.

Similar to @keiran0 response, I also found that every change Copilot did while helping to troubleshoot the code for the GUI, they prompted to test out the code and if a check fail, they analyse and modify the code till it passes the checks and is able to successfully build the project using gradle

</panel>

# Smoke Testing Outcome
Overall, all functionalities except for the update task work as intended.

Edge Cases found to have failed ❌ 
1. Editing tasks that have a description with a task type name
> update 3 event todo event /from 2026-03-01 /to 2026-03-12
3. Usage of the test case stated in the documentation
> update 3 event Team meeting /from 2026-02-22 /to 2026-02-22

</panel>

<div slot="header">

### 58. DAVI..NING `@deltaMinor` (13 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/290" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

Hi, thank you for taking some time to help smoke test my task tracking chatbot HERM35. I've put a lot of features into this project, thus any help in testing in is greatly appreciated!
- Link to JAR file: https://github.com/deltaMinor/ip/releases/download/A-Release/HERM35.jar
- Link to the User Guide: https://deltaminor.github.io/ip/

Things I would like feedback on:
- Whether HERM35 can be run on OS other than Windows.
- Flexible time inputs(You may even try inputting 'today' or 'tomorrow' for time)
- Combined parameters for the find command
- Convenience of the tag system
- Readability and accessibility of the custom theme and fonts
- Proper error handling

Guide for testers:
- Post screenshots from your smoke tests
- Mention which OS you used for testing

</panel>

OS: Windows
Here are my findings during the smoke test:

1. Inputs that do not directly correlate to another command is treated as a new todo task. I wonder if this behaviour is intentional, as this feature was not stated in the README.

<img width="493" height="780" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/deb23f36-5593-4f36-8c92-75495f7bade5" />

2. The find command is case sensitive, which can make it a hassle for the user to find tasks. 

<img width="495" height="782" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dcfcbb60-df30-4842-b042-2cbd181eab9f" />

3. When inputting an incorrect date & time for a deadline task, the error informs the user to input in d/M/yyyy HH:mm format, which is not actually accepted by the program.

<img width="492" height="783" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f01e9207-b9c0-49d6-b4fd-ebd05e3c292f" />

4. When using the find function, the tasks are not listed with their actual index, which can make it difficult to delete/mark/unmark tasks accurately when using such commands in conjunction with the find command.

<img width="496" height="783" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cababf65-8015-40d5-8e26-b4ce5ab9b0fb" />

<img width="493" height="780" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2d627889-b018-4c1f-8823-822003363a4a" />

Aside from these issues, the rest of the program runs fine for me! 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/324#issuecomment-3927553694" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the main issue is that GitHub Pages(the system that hosts https://cyledge.github.io/ip/) doesn't exactly use GitHub Markdown like the GitHub preview does to format its contents, which results in differences and even certain elements breaking. This was especially an issue I had when trying to use the details and summary tags to create collapsible dropdowns.

With regards to your issue, I have 2 suggestions:

1. If you simply want the example command to not be at the same indentation, add an additional blank line in between it and the previous line(or two extra spaces after the previous line) hopefully this should make it clear to the GitHub Pages formatting that these are separate elements.
2. You can add a _config.yml file to the docs folder with the line
`markdown: CommonMarkGhPages`
inside, then commit the change. This helped to partially fix my issue with collapsible dropdowns.

I hope at least one of the above suggestions work out for you.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/323#issuecomment-3927608167" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**

OS: Windows 11
Here are my findings:
1. Odd error message with task index is invalid.

<img width="497" height="783" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/18e94ec2-024b-4667-adfe-2bc36a6cf266" />

Granted, it can be inferred what it means, but perhaps it should have a consistent style with the other error messages.
2. `find` command does not index tasks by their actual position.

<img width="500" height="783" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6966b2bd-4347-43fd-adb5-6e6f6dbfbc67" />

This is not a high priority issue, however if the tasks are indexed by their actual position it would make it easier for the user to mark/unmark/delete tasks they have located using the `find` command.

Aside from above, the commands I have tested run correctly, and persistent data storage works as well. Well done.
(Also, this issue is labeled type-HelpRequest, I think it would be better if it were labeled type-SmokeTestingRequest)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/290#issuecomment-3932295618" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

>     An error that was missed out previously is in how the `event` command parses and checks the dates. In the screenshot below, the chatbot allows the user to input a start date that is **AFTER** the end date, which should not be the case.
Hi @breezuu , thanks for pointing this out. After some consideration, I've decided that I do want to give the user the capability of creating event tasks with a "to Date" that is before the "from Date", despite how illogical it may initially seem. (Just in case of (the unlikely) Parser error, and to provide user freedom). Thus rather than an error, I've implemented a warning instead in such a case:

<img width="1347" height="785" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5a3e6112-b6df-4ce2-aaac-21d480657c82" />

And as @darentanrw suggested, I have increase the default window size(reorganising the basic help section is too difficult without further complicating the help commands).

>     However, why would you cap the number of tasks at 100, especially when your chatbot is built for scalability?  
Thanks for pointing this out @Hack-Zac, that was a message due to legacy code, it has been removed in the updated release.
</panel>

OS Used: Mac

### Smoke Test Summary

✅ Commands work as specified in the user guide.
✅ Save data functionality works as expected.

### Feedback / Findings

❌ Window resizing works properly.
❌ no error message shown if file fails to load
❌ application still closes even if file saving fails
- video: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8868ce7b-ad72-4eba-92d0-b52fe95da70b 

The user guide is easy to follow and clear enough but there is room for expansion, for example, creating a section for each command so more details are available. But overall no major issues in the user guide

Overall good job creating the application, no major bug that causes crashes. Relevant error messages are displayed as well when incorrect user input is given. I like the thoughtful addition of the command shortcuts 👍 

### Screenshots

<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 61. BENE..TJIA `@BenedTj` (12 posts) <span class=text-warning>:octicon-eye:</span>
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/659" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Bug on tP Feature Freeze Period Code Dashboard**
</div>

I had previously only made changes to the content of string constants that hold error messages in my group's tP [in the files shown here](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/tp-feature-freeze-dashboard/?search=&sort=groupTitle&sortWithin=title&timeframe=commit&mergegroup=&groupSelect=groupByRepos&breakdown=true&checkedFileTypes=functional-code&since=2026-04-10T10%3A00%3A00&filteredFileName=&tabOpen=true&tabType=authorship&tabAuthor=BenedTj&tabRepo=AY2526S2-CS2103T-W14-2%2Ftp%5Bmaster%5D&authorshipIsMergeGroup=false&authorshipFileTypes=functional-code&authorshipIsBinaryFileTypeChecked=false&authorshipIsIgnoredFilesChecked=false). However, RepoSense has attributed changes to a large number of LoC in `ParserUtil` and `DeliveryTime`, which I did not do, to me, causing LoC attributed to me to exceed the allowed LoC changes during the feature freeze period. Is there any possibility for this to be rectified?

The following are examples of pieces of code that I did not change but whose change was falsely attributed to me:
<img width="1920" height="1032" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/554c4dbf-4d22-4741-9f13-fbb887b7f022" />
<img width="1920" height="1032" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fb452c22-856d-4d30-a222-e139faaa2a97" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/14#issuecomment-3765126796" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think on the bottom it says that `[We have not started updating this dashboard yet]`.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/41#issuecomment-3789045989" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

If I am not wrong, the progress for your Git Mastery tasks can be detected by enabling remote progress tracking using `gitmastery progress sync on` (but I could be wrong on this) and yes, you would need to download the specific Git Mastery task on your local directory to complete the task.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3789432515" expanded>

**4 :fas-comment:**

If you mean `git revert HEAD` and `git reset [the flag here] HEAD~1`, then yes, my understanding would be the same as yours.
</panel>

**5 :fas-comment:**

OS: Windows

Overall, good job on implementing the app! All commands work as intended and corrupted data storage is handled gracefully!
I would also like to commend how detailed your user guide is!

Perhaps, the only suggestion I would make is to possibly remove the line with the invalid task format in your data storage after the app has detected it.

<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

This one is for Windows.

Overall, user-friendly, clear in terms of command instructions given through `help` and handles unexpected command formats and exceptions well! The app runs smoothly on Windows.

If I had to be extremely nit-picky, it feels odd to me having the user side of the conversation on the left side of the app rather than the right side, which most apps do but the app looks good as is!

<img width="604" height="947" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/216c7277-d396-40f7-8538-9e8411623b7e" />

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/475#issuecomment-3977497012" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

OS: Windows

Overall, good job on implementing the commands and considering all the exceptions that could arise from the incorrect command formats!

The major bug that I have found is that the constructor throws a Java exception in the command line when the format of the file storage is invalid.

<img width="1154" height="617" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7c7ff405-6a30-4885-b5d0-10168e094c59" />

# Additional points
- I would suggest making the error messages more descriptive for handling `DateTime` values, such as in the case when the parameter value is expected to be a `DateTime` value is not and when the "time" part is omitted.
- I would suggest creating a different error message for when the parameter value for a parameter (e.g. /from) is empty/missing (when '/from' is directly followed by '/to').
- I would suggest updating the error message for index out of bounds errors to reflect the 1-based indexing that users are more familiar with when using the app.

# Screenshots from Smoke Testing

<img width="604" height="947" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd7551a8-5c7f-44d6-a837-567a4b5d2b12" />

Hello, thank you for releasing the new JAR file! I tried running the JAR file that you have just uploaded but it still gives the same error:

<img width="1920" height="1032" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/54a73965-bd99-4fb8-8728-f972b651dad7" />

Perhaps you did not create a fat JAR (a JAR file that includes dependencies such as JavaFX in it) because personally the sizes of JAR files for the full project that I have seen, including mine, are more than 10 MB so I suspect that based on the size of the JAR file, it is not a fat JAR. To do so, you can run the `shadowJar` task included in Gradle.

For more about fat JAR files you can find them [here](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/jar.html).
</panel>

> > Perhaps you did not create a fat JAR (a JAR file that includes dependencies such as JavaFX in it) because personally the sizes of JAR files for the full project that I have seen, including mine, are more than 10 MB so I suspect that based on the size of the JAR file, it is not a fat JAR. To do so, you can run the `shadowJar` task included in Gradle.
> > For more about fat JAR files you can find them [here](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/jar.html).
> 
> Hi, thank you again for testing and for pointing this out earlier.
> 
> I have rebuilt the application and updated the release with a new bit.jar that bundles the JavaFX runtime properly using the shadowJar task. The latest JAR file is now around 10 MB, which should include the necessary JavaFX dependencies.
> 
> Would you mind downloading the latest bit.jar from the release page and giving it another try?
> 
> Please let me know if the issue still persists. Thank you again for helping test this.

Hello, thank you for releasing the fat JAR file! I tried your app and good job in implementing the commands! Particularly, I have to commend you on the good UI and user experience.

Some bugs/suggestions I can give:
* Event tasks with end dates stated after the start dates are allowed, which should not be.
* When the storage file is corrupted, the app handles it gracefully but when a line in the file is corrupted, the rest of the lines are not read and the app could tell the user that some lines of the file have been corrupted.

### 62. SEAN..OUNG `@seanchiew` (12 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/769" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Activity Diagram Clarification (can an Action have multiple incoming flow arrows?)**
</div>

Given this activity diagram:
<img width="993" height="508" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3b4872f5-de59-4f39-a642-32e55b438d42" />

Would this following activity diagram still be considered correct?
<img width="1280" height="618" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/218a24ba-7dcb-4758-9132-5c88d5f53cab" />
In particular, i would like to ask, for the Action "Read n", can it have two incoming arrows (similar to how if we omit a merge node, we can connect multiple flow arrows to an Action, as shown by the 3 arrows merging into Action 5).

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/767" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Tutorial 9 CCD clarification**
</div>


<img width="1748" height="1233" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fa37a90c-0a97-46a5-8345-d88bd9bb9c31" />
Tutorial 9 recording shows that the above example CCD is wrong in representing the highlighted text "and taught by one or more instructors one of whom is the coordinator", because using this CCD could allow tom to be two separate objects (as illustrated in the left object diagram image), or have the reader not be able to tell which course tom is the coordinator for (as illustrated in the right object diagram image). 


However, arent both object diagrams provided to attempt to disproof the correctness of the CCD flawed to begin with? 

For the left object diagram, if tom is a coordinator for one course, and an instructor for another course, shldnt tom only be represented as a single coordinator object (because the CCD clearly states that a coordinator IS An instructor), where he has an association to c1 with the association role being instructor, and he has another association to c2, with the assocation role being "coordinator" (similar to how its represented in the right object diagram, just now including the respective association labels)?  i.e: 
<img width="1280" height="563" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a65b1cbb-fc5e-4633-903b-6ad14350ccdb" />


Further, the only reason why the object diagram on the right makes you not know which course Tom is the coordinator of, is because the object diagram omits the assocation label of each association. Simply adding the assocaition label would resolve this, e.g:
<img width="1280" height="563" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5c45eb2a-0d54-48c7-9197-7ae23a428aab" />

As such, my point of confusion is that, it seems like its not that the CCD is wrong, but its instead that the object diagram given to attempt to disprove the correctness of the CCD is instead flawed. 
Further, I still dont see why this CCD would be erroneous, and thus might be missing something, and would like to seek clarifications on whether this CCD is truly wrong or actually correct in representing the highlighted info "and taught by one or more instructors one of whom is the coordinator"

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/743" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: UML Activity Diagram Inquiry on Fork/Join**
</div>

Based on this exercise provided in the textbook:
<img width="951" height="945" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/16845a1e-c56e-4974-9212-62e737da0790" />
It states that paths forked from the same fork node must also be joined by the same join node.

As such, if I would like to model the following activity in an activity diagram:
- P1, P2 and P3 are ran concurrently
- Only P1 and P2 are required to be complete (i.e joined) before executing Action B and Action A
- P3 is required to complete before the end

Then given these two options:
<img width="872" height="655" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/81ab8439-011d-476f-9f67-942c97be0ced" />

Can i confirm that Diagram A would still be wrong, since the parallel path that starts with P3 is forked from the same fork node as the parallel paths that starts with P1 and P2, yet P3 is joined at a different join node from the other two parallel paths, and instead Diagram B is the correct way to represent the activity.


Thank you!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/508" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Clarification regarding Sequence Diagram arrowhead**
</div>

The textbook notes that "Arrows showing synchronous (i.e., the caller method is blocked from doing anything else until the called method returns) should use filled arrowheads", where as "Asynchronous method calls (i.e., the caller method does not have to wait till the called method returns) are shown using lined arrowheads", Further, "As the latter is out of scope for this textbook, all sequence diagram arrow heads you encounter in this textbook will be of the first type."

<img width="1065" height="341" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5301f4a6-a749-40dc-a590-243a2cb6e0b2" />


However, this week's (week 8) UML sequence diagram drawing exercise utilised lined arrowheads to represent  synchronous calls. Is this considered as an error as part of the video, or is it that since asynchronous calls are out of scoped, therefore we are free to utilise either arrowheads since there would not be any ambiguity.
<img width="506" height="427" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/57a46e41-551d-4111-8fb8-947a1e5b0211" />

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/148" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Question for UML Diagram (Association)**
</div>

<img width="1016" height="59" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4986dd8c-7d9b-45f1-aa0a-429c3076261a" />

<img width="1077" height="685" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5e77688f-92ff-4a62-a4cd-24e9ce4ebf4f" />

If association is to be expressed as "either an attribute or a line, but not both, where a line is preferred", then for the exams, if we are asked to "Draw a class diagram", showing "**the attributes**, methods, associations, navigabilities, and multiplicities" (similar to that stated in the second image), does this imply that we should now be expressing the association as an attribute, so that we can explicitly state the attributes of the class? 

On a hypothetical note, if the question also specifies "access modifiers" to be included in the class diagram, would this also force us to express association as an attribute rather than a line?

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/144" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6. :fas-info-circle: Question for UML Class Diagrams**
</div>

<img width="1278" height="383" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6d793eaf-1171-46fd-b191-1dd903046d84" />

If compartments may be omitted, and the attributes/operations themselves can also be omitted if deemed irrelevant to the purpose of the diagram, then how will we represent a class with no attributes (and/or operations)? 

By the same token, when looking at a UML class diagram, is it correct to say that even if the class diagram has an empty attribute compartment, we cannot conclude that the class has no attributes, right? 

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/219#issuecomment-3908893607" expanded>

**10 :fas-comment:**

@QR-Phua thanks for your input!

i hear your point

> Think about the edge case where we have two different people who are coordinators for two different courses. In the system, both must be instantiated as Coordinator objects. If both of them are then assigned to co-teach a completely separate third course as regular instructors, the system breaks down. We'd have a course taught by two Coordinator objects, and no way to inherently know who holds the authority for that specific course without relying entirely on the association links.

For this, wouldnt this exactly be the point of therefore including the association links and association roles, since they are only omitted if they dont add value, or is not important for the current context, but if without the association roles then there is " no way to inherently know who holds the authority for that specific course", then this surely warrants an important enough need to then include the association labels, solving this "edge case" all together.

> And if we have to rely on the association links anyway (like your fix suggested), then the Coordinator subclass isn't actually doing any useful work and shouldn't exist.

I think its abit harsh to say the "coordinator subclass isn't actually doing any useful work and shouldn't exist.", because at the end of the day, since those two instructors that you mentioned in ur "edge case" are still coordinators for a course each, therefore the coordinator subclass would be used to effectively represent those two instructors who are coordinators.


> Hence, wouldn't it be better to remove the subclass entirely. Instead, we just use a single Instructor class and draw two separate association lines to Course

Yup i absolutely agree, but my question wasnt about asking about what is a "better" way. The actual correct tutorial solution provided (and as describe by you) is definitely the most appropriate. But my question was more about asking whether the aforementioned CCD (that was deemed incorrect by tutorial 9), should actually be regarded as "correct", though admittedly less ideal.

Still, appreciate your input
</panel>

**1. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Activity Diagram Arrows**
</div>

In the first quiz of week 9:
<img width="792" height="238" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/623da217-5215-4706-8990-3f3aa43d1657" />
One of the questions asks whether this has valid notation, but I noticed that the arrows for the `[input < 5]` and `[else]` paths are connected at the end.

In the [course website]() under `Activity Diagrams → Basic Notation → Alternate Paths`:
> Some acceptable simplifications (by convention):
> - Omitting the merge node if it doesn't cause any ambiguities.
> - Multiple arrows can start from the same corner of a branch node.
> - Omitting the [Else] condition.

Multiple arrows can start from the same corner of a branch node, but can I confirm the arrows also need not point to the merge node separately? Thanks in advance.

**7 :fas-comment:**

According to the [website](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/ip-w4.html#1-use-gfmd-in-the-pr-description):
>Update the description of the iP PR (how?) you created earlier (do not add a new comment) so that it contains the following GFMD elements:
a heading
a bullet list
a numbered list
a fenced code block (with syntax highlighting)
a task list
an emoji
a blockquote
a hyperlink
inline code
some text formatting: bold, italic, strikethrough etc.

Just took a look at your PR and I believe the unchecked ones are what you are missing:
- [X] Heading
- [X] Bullet list
- [ ] Numbered list
- [X] Fenced code block (with syntax highlighting)
- [ ] Task list
- [ ] Emoji
- [ ] Blockquote
- [ ] Hyperlink
- [X] Inline code
- [ ] some text formatting: bold, italic, strikethrough etc

You can also refer to the [website](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/ip-w4.html#dukepro) or [my PR](https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/346) for some examples!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/534#issuecomment-4054249894" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on the diagram, the intention seems to be that a task should be explicitly tied to 1 project, although I think the code would technically allow tasks to be linked to more than 1 project. I guess this shows the difference between diagrams and actual code, as the diagram represents the intent, while the code is more of some implementation of the intent.

I would thus believe that in the example you gave:
> If one task belongs to multiple projects (e.g. in real life the task "Updating Software" can be part of the "Security Patch" project and the "System Upgrade"), then if I delete the "Security Patch" project, I shouldn't delete the "Updating Software" task.

That this would not fit the intention given in the diagram above. In my opinion, a "Security Patch" would instead be a task under "System Upgrade" and not a whole separate project, thus making "Security Patch" a subtask of "Updating Software".

At the end of the day, I think the main point of the composition relationship in this case is simply that a task cannot exist without a project, which makes sense whether or not a task can be related to multiple projects.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/550#issuecomment-4072902971" expanded>

### 64. LEE ..HENG `@iamqz` (11 posts) <span class=text-warning>:octicon-eye:</span>
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/624" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on what counts as reused code for tP**
</div>


wanted to clarify what level of similarity counts as “reused code” that needs to be declared.

For example, if code is written independently but follows a similar structure/format:
- Defining a constant like:
`public static final String MESSAGE_DUPLICATE_&lt;new_attribute> = "&lt;message>";`
- Writing a test case with a similar pattern/structure (e.g. setting up objects, building a descriptor, calling the command, and asserting failure): `assertCommandFailure(editCommand, model, MESSAGE_DUPLICATE_...);`
- new additional **assertion codes** (e.g., more edge cases, enhancement of given codes)
- newly created attribute as a **Class** (not sure if `toString`, `equal`, `isValid&lt;Attribute Name>` methods are considered as reused as well)
</panel>

Hi @athrv2,

I've finished smoke testing your JAR file. Here are my findings:

## Environment

**OS**: macOS (Apple Silicon)

**Java Version**: 17

## Feedback

<img width="595" height="319" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fa602508-cb36-4ca2-8a13-d3f1251ef0bf" />

**General**: The application is stable and the core features work as expected!

**Empty List Handling**: When the list is empty, running the list command still displays the header "Here's your list:". It might be more user-friendly to show a specific message like "Your list is currently empty."

**Date Validation Logic**: I noticed that entering an invalid date like 2026/02/30 is automatically corrected to 2026/02/28. While it prevents a crash, it might be better to alert the user that the input was invalid so they can provide the correct intended date. If you're using LocalDate, you might want to check out ResolverStyle.STRICT to "solve" this minor problem.

Great job on the UI otherwise!
</panel>

**6 :fas-comment:**

Hi @danielyap4103,

I've finished smoke testing your JAR file. Here are my findings:

## Environment

**OS**: macOS (Apple Silicon)

**Java Version**: 17

## Feedback

**General**: The application is stable and the core features work as expected (with proper commands)

**Critical Bug: Application Crash**

- Issue: The app throws a RuntimeException and stops responding when entering only the command deadline without any description or /by tag.

- Stack Trace Snippet: 'Caused by: java.lang.StringIndexOutOfBoundsException: begin 9, end 8, length 8
at atlas.parser.Parser.parseDeadline(Parser.java:140)'
- Observation: It seems like the substring() logic in your Parser class expects a minimum string length that isn't met, causing the index to go out of bounds. Perhaps have a checker to ensure the /by keyword is present before the substring() logic


</panel>

### 65. TAN .. RON `@jianrontan` (11 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/577" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Storing nested objects in JSON for AB3 - best approach?**
</div>

We are implementing a workout session log feature where each `Person` 
can have multiple `WorkoutLog` entries. We are unsure of the best way 
to handle this in AB3's storage.

The main challenge is that `WorkoutLog` is not a simple field — it is 
its own object that needs to be persisted, and each log needs to be 
linked back to the correct `Person`.

We considered:
1. Adding a `List&lt;WorkoutLog>` field to `JsonAdaptedPerson` and 
   serialising each log as a nested JSON object
2. Storing logs in a separate JSON file outside of `addressbook.json`

Approach 1 feels most consistent with AB3's existing design, but we 
are not sure how to handle the `JsonAdaptedWorkoutLog` class and how 
it fits into the existing `JsonSerializableAddressBook`.

Has anyone implemented something similar? How did you structure your 
`JsonAdapted` classes for nested objects?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/501" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Use-GFMD tag not turning green despite using GFMD in PR**
</div>

Hi everyone,

I've been trying to get the `Use-GFMD` tag to turn green in the iP dashboard but it hasn't turned green despite what I believe is correct usage of GitHub Flavored Markdown in my PR.

**My PR:** https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/292

### What I have in my PR

My PR comment uses the following GFMD elements:
- Headings (`#`, `##`, `###`)
- Bullet point lists (`-`)
- Inline code (e.g. `data/tasks.txt`, `help`)
- Fenced code blocks (` ''' `)
- **Bold text**

### What I've tried

I've looked at neighbouring PRs (#290, #291) and their content also appears as a comment in the same format as mine. I'm not sure what I'm missing.

### Question

What exactly does the grading script check for to satisfy the `Use-GFMD` requirement? Is it the location of the content (PR description vs comment), specific GFMD elements that must be present, or something else?

Thanks in advance!
</panel>

**6 :fas-comment:**

Thanks for the detailed responses! We will likely go with with Approach 1 as suggested.

Just one more thing, when deserialising, if a `WorkoutLog` entry is malformed in the JSON, should we fail the entire person load or just skip the invalid log and load the rest? If I'm not wrong, I think AB3 throws an `IllegalValueException` for the whole person if any field is invalid, curious if others handled this differently for nested list fields.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/577#issuecomment-4134675808" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Hi, to add to your second follow-up question, I believe it's better to fail the entire person load. This is because as a user, skipping bad logs means the user may never realise some workout history disappeared. It makes it difficult to identify which user details is invalid, and increases the difficulty of debugging.

Hi, thanks for the suggestion, we have also come to agree with that actually. Thanks to everyone for the feedback!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/589#issuecomment-4134734843" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

According to https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/admin/tp-pe.html expandable panel titled Admin tP → Practical Exam (PE) → Guidelines for bug triaging (extract 2):

"Specificity of error message: Error messages can be correct but not specific enough (e.g., it says the input is 'invalid' without giving the reason, or gives too many possible reasons without pointing out the specific reason). These cases can be considered type.FeatureFlaw."

So I think that more specific is better. So tell the user what went wrong, for example: "Invalid phone number. Phone number must be an 8-digit number starting with 6, 8, or 9." and give them the correct format.
</panel>

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yeah you're right about the navigability arrows, I actually have them for the Handout-Brochure associations (same line) but I missed it for the Brochure self-association, good catch.
For the * thing, I think using * is fine because it just means unconstrained, for the cover relationship, the question only says each Brochure has exactly one Handout as cover, so we put 1 on the Handout side. But it doesn't say anything about how many Brochures can share the same Handout as cover, so * on the Brochure side just means we're not adding any restriction that wasn't stated. It's not really assuming it's many, more like saying there's nothing stopping it from being many. Leaving it blank is also fine though I think.

These are my updated diagrams:
<img width="1280" height="752" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c64c0a80-f67d-4400-9b16-759f99cc191f" />
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/614" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Questions regarding Codecov**
</div>

Hi I just want to clarify something. From the CS2103 website, it was stated that CodeCov is not tested but it helps in identifying the code coverage. 

1. For our team, the Codecov was initially set up properly and was able to track the first initial commits so we assumed that it was running fine. 
2. But recently we went to check and realised that it stopped at tracking the first initial commits which explain the unchanged code coverage percentage and we had to re-configure the token again for it to work. However it only tracked the latest commit and now has the `"Missing base commit"` error.  
3. So our history on CodeCov has missing pieces essentially. 

So is there any remedy for this situation besides rerunning the workflows (Since it is only limited to the past 30days)? Or should we just do an empty commit at the master level to give the commits a base commit to compare to?

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/102#issuecomment-3822388806" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

According to the IP dashboard troubleshooting, your branch is ahead by the master by 1? Below is the suggested remedy, I do not know if it will solve your issue. 

"Note the numbers n|m shown for each branch. If the m is not zero, that means the branch is ahead of the master branch by that number of commits, and hence, not considered fully merged (only merged branches are detected by the dashboard). Reasons:
You did not push the branch after merging it.
Remedy: After merging a branch to the master branch, you need to push both branches to the fork.
You pushed commits to the branch after merging it to the master branch.
Remedy: Either delete those extra commits from the branch (both locally and in the fork) or merge the branch to the master branch again."


</panel>

<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Code quality: Avoiding magic literals**
</div>

Hi, I was wondering if instead of using a bunch of constants for literals as the textbook suggests, e.g.:

'''java
private static final String VALID_REGEX_FOR_EXIT = "&hat;bye$";
private static final String VALID_REGEX_FOR_LIST = "&hat;list$";
...
'''

would using enums be a valid way to avoid magic literals in this course? e.g.

'''java
private enum Command ``{``
    EXIT ("&hat;bye$"),
    LIST ("&hat;list$"),
    ...;

    private String validRegex;
    ...
``}``
'''
</panel>

### 68. CHOO.. ZHE `@cedarglass` (11 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/733" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on Gray-Box Testing**
</div>


Previously, #568 provided a great discussion on the difference between white-box and black-box testing. From my understanding of the post, white-box testing (primarily) aims to check the internal structure of the code, while black-box testing aims to check the behaviour of the code according to specifications. 

These two categories seem to cover most possible test cases to me, which leaves gray-box testing rather ambiguous. Is it right for me to say that gray-box testing is primarily specification-based, but incorporates some (minimal?) information about the implementation in its test case design, as opposed to being implementation-based, but incorporating specification details (which would be white-box)?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/111" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on coding standard question in Week 3 debrief**
</div>


For ease of discussion, here is the code block from the debrief: 
'''java
boolean send (int expected) ``{``
    String XmlFormat = null;
    if (XmlFormat.length() &lt; expected) ``{``
        return true;
    ``}`` else ``{``
        return false;
    ``}``
``}``
'''
The coding standard violations mentioned in the debrief were: 
1. boolean variable name should be binary sounding (e.g. `isSend`)
2. `XmlFormat` should be in camelCase 

However, since method names should be verbs, would naming a method `length()` also be a coding standard violation? A name that follows coding standards could be `getLength()`.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Utilising OOP principles in class method design**
</div>


After doing the OOP commit in the iP, I now have a `Storage` class which implements a `loadTasks()` method, that can read from disk. At first, I thought this method should return a generic data structure (e.g. `ArrayList&lt;Task>`). However, I realised I could also package it in my `TaskList` class, and return that instead.  

My instinct is that I should hide the generic data structure under `TaskList`, because that's the object class I've designated as a task collection. Nonetheless, I'm happy to hear everyone's thoughts on which option better utilises OOP principles. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96#issuecomment-3827841134" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Thanks to everyone for their comments! It has been an engaging discussion with support for both sides. To summarise: 

### In favour of returning an `ArrayList`:
* Decouples `Storage` and `TaskList`, so that method changes to `TaskList` may not incur changes in `Storage`, and testing `Storage` is easier (e.g. no need to create stubs). 

### In favour of returning a `TaskList`:
* `TaskList` functions as an API which keeps implementation details hidden, good use of abstraction. 
* Avoids the need to propagate change: if we return an `ArrayList`, all callers of `loadTask()` have to know about the `ArrayList`, which is messy.
* One can also take a `TaskList` as input to `loadTasks()`, or use an instance of `Storage` in `TaskList`. 

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/197#issuecomment-3900890118" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the greater significance of the object deletion of `:Parser` occuring before the `createParser()` method's activation bar ends is that it was created in the limited scope of the `opt` frame, and thus would no longer be referenced once we exit that frame. On the other hand, the `createParser()` method can continue to run after that frame has ended. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3900985959" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think AI is good for getting a basic overview of codebases, e.g. for the tp. It's also good for planning: I used AI to help me brainstorm potential ways to implement some of the more open-ended Levels (e.g. the Extension). When AI helps me to break down feature implementation into a step-by-step process, I find the task less daunting. I'm also less likely to create bugs from updating the codebase incompletely after introducing a new change. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/236#issuecomment-3917950212" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/714#issuecomment-4285727938" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It seems that the most valid response to these sorts of bugs would be to label it as `NotInScope`. However, there are several additional criteria for `NotInScope` that was not mentioned above: 

> In addition, the following (at least one) need to be satisfied:
> The UG specifies it as not supported or coming in a future version.
> The user cannot attempt to use the missing feature or when the user does so, the software fails gracefully, possibly with a suitable error message i.e., the software should not crash.

In other words, an issue can only be considered as `NotInScope` if the dev team "saw it coming", and either: 
1) explicitly mentioned it in the UG, or
2) barred the feature's usage --> note that this cannot apply to issues that are taking an existing feature and using it in unintended ways.

Speaking from personal experience, my team has encountered several bugs which are, in fact, taking an existing feature (e.g. the UI) and using it in unintended ways (e.g. extreme minimising). And indeed I would say that fixing those issues are secondary to the work already done: ensuring that the feature itself (e.g. the UI) exists. However, without fulfilling the conditions above, the only valid response seems to be to `Accept` these issues as bugs.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/735#issuecomment-4311191357" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

If I understand it correctly, the spirit of the LoD is to reduce coupling. When we obtain another object (i.e. `a` above) through a method call on an object that's a parameter (i.e. `b` above), we introduce a dependency on `a` that isn't obvious to the code author, which might cause additional work during future updates of `a`. 

So the code above does seem to violate the spirit of the LoD. To me it's not clear how calling `b.initA()` is much different from calling `b.getA()`. 

One interesting perspective (from [here](https://www.tedinski.com/2018/12/18/the-law-of-demeter.html)) is that some problems the LoD seeks to address can be avoided simply by being more careful/selective about making such getter/initialisation methods public, which tempts programmers into chaining. 
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As mentioned by @desnen and @Joulenergy above, I think it really is quite dependent on context and the system design. LoD seems like it serves as a general guideline to prevent highly coupled behaviour and to maintain the abstraction barrier, which could also be helpful in code extensibility. Personally, I wouldn't want to have to know everything about the internal structure of a class written by someone else just to call its methods.
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yes, as mentioned by @DenseLance-alt above, I think equivalence partitions are context dependent. Black-box testing uses EPs since the tester generally does not need to know about a program's internals, so EPs provide a good estimate for correctness. However given more information, it is likely that we can break them down into "sub equivalence partitions" as you suggested.
</panel>

Untick "Indent 'case' branches" under switch statement/expression
