<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/wincura/ip/releases/tag/A-Release
* Which OS'es to test on: Windows | Mac | Linux (tested on Windows 11, but help for other OSes as well as Windows is welcome!)
* Things to check specifically:

- [ ] Exception Handling: Whether edge cases are handled properly including invalid indices, invalid datetime formats, incorrect logic like end times being after start times.
- [ ] File IO: Whether an empty file gets created during first run, and after replacing it with a file with existing data ([tasklist.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25371834/tasklist.txt)), it loads properly.
- [ ] GUI: Any glaring issues with GUI, just as an addition.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/109" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Usage of wildcard import at base app code**
</div>

Acknowledging that we generally refrain from using wildcards, in the scenario where we are at the .java file containing the base app, and where we expect to use a lot of the packages, would it be okay to use import &lt;package>.*, especially if we are using all of the classes in that package?

Appreciate the advice!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/84#issuecomment-3805681857" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think it was meant to be `case x:` because the first statement is referring to the use of colon in this kind of Java statement:
`cond ? if_true : if_false;`

The rule about surrounding the colons by whitespace also applies in this kind of code:

```
for (type item : collection) ``{``
    ...
``}``
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/183#issuecomment-3881845785" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You may checkout to main branch in remote, go to the previous pre-merge commit in your main, and click 'Reset Current Branch to here'. Then, checkout to branch-Level-10 to make sure the commits are still there. Do the same for local, and then merge again without fast forward.

If anything goes wrong, what helped me was IntelliJ's own version history. File > Local History > Show Project History, and you can revert to when you had your changes. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/259#issuecomment-3921638218" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

OS: Windows 11

<img width="666" height="413" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ef55d547-23cb-4ea8-8b53-d7f4897e92f5" />

The app works as intended, with expected behavior for the commands and the app being able to run with or without the data folder and save file. 
 
<img width="664" height="635" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8e04a44e-3eb5-43f1-a278-b3aa509d2de0" />

I like that it checks for invalid input for a command, as well as invalid input for event time, with start date needing to be before end date logically. 

I have some feedback on the word choice for the tag commands. Could 'tag' be 'tag-add' to align with 'tag-delete'? 

A visual nitpick is that the color for the scroll bar is in contrast to the rest of the app for the 'normal' parts (i.e. the background, the send button and the normal messages) and is more similar to the error messages. Could it fit better as a darker shade of blue/green instead of pink? 

The icons could also look sharper/high-resolution, as its blur edges looks slightly out of place.

Generally, a well-validated functional app with a nice minimalist design making use of gradients and contrasting color schemes to differentiate normal/error messages.
</panel>

I managed to fix the issue by doing `./gradlew clean build` then `./gradlew clean run`, instead of `./gradlew clean build` then `./gradlew run`. Not sure why this was the case, but the issue has been resolved.

Are tasks 3 and 5 marked as complete if we release v1.5 jar file, pdf file, and close v1.5 milestone before Thu 2359? Then after that, we are allowed to modify our code and UG/DG after Thu 2359, before Fri 10am? Will the latest version be used during PE-D testing?

<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Typo in Textbook: UML: Sequence Diagrams → Alternative Paths**
</div>

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e51ec1c7-eef2-4bb1-ac7f-3593b230097e)

"Minefield calls the Cell#setMine method if the cell is supposed to be a mined cell, and calls the `Cell:setMineCount(...)` method otherwise."

Should it be `Cell:setCount(...)` according to the diagram instead?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/101" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5. :fas-info-circle: Topics W3.7f: JUnit Intermediate: Outdated Hyperlinks**
</div>

The list of 13 different noteworthy JUnit concepts each have a unique hyperlink [more...], but all the pages seem to be outdated and they reroute back to the homepage of the JUnit User Guide. I believe they were each meant to direct us to the specific page covering the specific concept, which now each have a different URL.

I believe this is because the User Guide was updated - the link directs us to the JUnit 6.0.2 User Guide (instead of JUnit 5 User Guide as stated in W3.7f).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/381#issuecomment-3933210214" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Thanks so much guys! It turns out that my changes when I swapped the files didn't save in git because I deleted and added the files externally in file explorer. Does this mean I should never do this and use git bash for all file changes?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/381#issuecomment-3933441690" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

@breezuu Yup, after deleting and adding 2 new files, the `images` folder doesn't show up as a change in `git status` at all. When I `cd` into the `images` folder in git bash, the changes get reverted back.

The new files appear in Intellij only after I close and reopen the project, but after the files in git bash are reverted, they also revert back again in Intellij.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/381#issuecomment-3939951299" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yup I figured it out, it turns out I was incorrectly updating the images in the `build\resources\main\images` folder instead of `src\main\resources\images`... thanks all for the help!
</panel>

</panel>

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/JamieLoh/ip/releases/tag/v0.2
* Link to the User Guide: https://github.com/JamieLoh/ip/blob/master/docs/README.md
* Which OS'es to test on: Windows | Mac | Linux
* Things to check specifically:

1.  UI loads correctly with no visual glitches or crashes
2. Resizing of application.
3. All chatbot commands (add, list, done, delete, help) work as stated in README
4. Handling of errors and edge case bugs.
5. Data persistence in between app loads.

Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing

Thank you for helping :) Happy CNY!
</panel>

**3 :fas-comment:**

Thanks for all your help! 

Events from and to times can be the same is expected to behave like this so user can just indicate there is an event at that date and no need to care about the time. So I just forbid end time before the start time and throw an error when such case happens. But thanks for pointing out this :)

Background image stretches with the width of the window is expected and designed to be like this. But thanks for pointing out :)

And thanks for spotting the typo and date formatting error. These are really helpful!👍 Will fix these problem soon.

All in all, thanks for yall.
</panel>

### 111. ALEX..RALD `@alexgeraldhandoko` (8 posts)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/534" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: UML Class Diagram in This Weeks Video**
</div>

<img width="1258" height="947" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a6076cc-c593-4d46-a6f5-7ab0832289ce" />

I don't really understand the composition relationship. If one task belongs to multiple projects (e.g. in real life the task "Updating Software" can be part of the "Security Patch" project and the "System Upgrade"), then if I delete the "Security Patch" project, I shouldn't delete the "Updating Software" task. 

Would that still count as a composition relationship then?

Thank you for your time 🙏
</panel>

### 112. JAME.. JUN `@Somebody-somewher` (8 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/676" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: PE Bug Reporting: Do possible cybersecurity vulnerabilities count as extreme user behaviour**
</div>

Hi, would like to clarify if a feature is "too permissive" such that it can be easily manipulated to result in a possible cybersecurity vulnerability, would it be a viable bug to report? I am asking because I'm not sure if this is considered a "extreme user behavior", since this is technically "deliberate sabotage".

From the cs2103t website:
```
Problems caused by extreme user behaviors:
- If the problem happens only in case of a deliberate sabotage (e.g., user entered a 30-digit telephone number), it will not be considered a bug (in our context).
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/177#issuecomment-3872734535" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Maybe not the best usage of assert, but for my iP, I had this issue where I needed to get my userInput String to my Parser class while avoiding the GUI class from holding a direct reference to my Parser. This involved initializing the GUI with a Consumer from Parser to handle userInput. 

```
private Consumer<String> commandParser;

/** Injects the Parser for Input */
public void setParser(Consumer<String> commandParser) ``{``
    this.commandParser = commandParser;
``}``

@FXML
private void handleUserInput() ``{``
    String userInput = userInputField.getText();

    // Display User Input
    dialogContainer.getChildren().add(DialogBox.getUserDialog(userInput, userImage));
   
    // Give Parser the userInput for it to process
    commandParser.accept(userInput);
    userInputField.clear();
``}``
```
Parser then had to, at some point, pass the Consumer to GUI. However, I was afraid that I would forget to do this if ever I had to change the Parser class or had to change the logic of classes that use the Parser class. Furthermore, since the GUI class will only complain about the Null Consumer when handleUserInput() is invoked, it may also be harder to debug and pinpoint when something went wrong (because the time frame between the "error" and the "error" being reported is so large). 

My solution was to create an isInitialized() function in GUI, that only returns true if GUI was passed a Consumer, and use it for assertions in my Main Class. This ensures that by the time I finish initializing my Chatbot Logic, my GUI should already have its Consumer, and I can be rest assured that the rest of my program will work correctly. If ever I forget to initialize GUI with the Parser consumer (or any other dependency), the assertion will fail early. Hence, I will know early on roughly when and where to look for the error, which will make debugging much less painful.

```
GraphicalUI ui = new GraphicalUI();
ChatBotBob bob = new ChatBotBob(ui);
assert(ui.isInitialized());
```
In summary, I think (correct me if I'm wrong), in practical terms, assertions are good sanity checks to help you debug and pinpoint "fail-points" as you code, identifying **when** and **where** your core logic stops working correctly, while the JUnit Tests are better for ensuring overall functionality and checking edge case scenarios, especially when user input is involved. 

===================================================================================
On a side note, one thing I was debating on using assertions on was validating the "save file" for Tasks, to check for improper Task encoding. However, I think for these scenarios they should just be handled by Exceptions, since (a) technically the files can be modified by users, so it should be considered as user input instead of a programming mistake and (b) I think, for an application such as the iP, the program should still be "okay" to continue without loading in the "save file" by simply ignoring the wrongly coded Task (i.e its not a big enough issue to stop the entire program). 

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/252#issuecomment-3919622312" expanded>

OS: Windows 11

## Smoke Test Checklist

* [x] Launch: The application launches successfully via `java -jar`.
* [x] GUI: The window opens, layout appears correct with no visual glitches. UI overall looks nice! The window resizes (though it can be a bit awkward if its "squeezed" all the way, see Screenshot 4)
* [x] Features:
    * [x] Todo (Todo Tasks can be created and interact correctly with the rest of the commands)
    * [x] Deadline (Deadline Tasks can be created and interact correctly with the rest of the commands)
    * [x] Event (Event Tasks can be created and interact correctly with the rest of the commands)
    * [x] Tagging tasks (Tags can be correctly created with the `#` symbol appended)
    * [x] mark (Marks tasks correctly)
    * [x] unmark (Unmarks tasks correctly)
    * [x] find (Find can correctly look for tasks based on the tag or the task name)
    * [x] delete (Tasks get deleted correctly)
    * [x] list (Tasks get listed correctly)
    * [x] bye (Application saves data and closes successfully)
* [x] Data Persistence: Closing the app via `bye` or closing the window results in tasks being saved into a `janeeyre.txt` file inside a newly created `data` folder. The tasks are loaded back into the app when the app is launched again.
* [ ] Error Handling: 
    * [ ] Adding tasks named/tagged with "`,`" or "`#`" characters will result in the task being saved and loaded incorrectly from file (See Screenshot 2 & 3)
    * [ ] Tagging tasks with ` ` or `,` punctuation, something like `todo test #TAG 1` will result in the task being created as `test 1 #TAG` (See Screenshot 1)
    * [ ] Multiple Tasks of the same name can be added (See Screenshot 1)
    * [ ] Events can be added with start dates that come after the end date (See Screenshot 2)
    * [x] Mark, Unmark, Delete correctly disallow non-numeric input or invalid indexes
    * [x] Event and Deadline correctly disallow multiple / out-of-order instances of `/from`, `/to`  or `/by` and invalid date formats

## Error Handling
* Error messages are insightful -&gt; Minor point but for the commands that involve a task index, the error message its a bit generic when the user enters a String. Potentially could make the error message more specific, asking the user for a number instead? (See Screenshot 1)
* Also adding a Task with a **name** a `' | '`  causes the program to **delimit it as a note upon saving and re-opening the app** (See Screenshot 2 & 3)
* Any characters after a **note** with a `' | '` **disappear upon saving and re-opening the app as well** (See Screenshot 2 & 3)
* Multiple tasks with the same name can be added (See Screenshot 4)

## Other comments 
* I think should specify in the user docs that there can only be one `note` at a time for a task? At least that how that works from my understanding
* Overall I like the personality :p

I'm not quite clear on how merging works for branches of branches, and when would it even be useful to do something like 'a branch on a branch'

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3879291356" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I actually really agree with what @AK-matrix has mentioned. I feel like the projects and levels so far has allowed me to explore the usage of AI coding tools and helped me to realise that my workflow can really be enhanced. I realised that there were many repetitive tasks that are time consuming. With the AI tools, I am able to spend more time on actually understanding the concepts, while the AI is able to help with completing the repetitive tasks. This has also allowed me to be able to articulate my understanding and planning better, as I had to prompt AI for certain tasks. This links to the point on issues snowballing. It is exactly why it is important that we understand what exactly is the AI implementing, to prevent further code generation which leads to "spaghetti code", resulting in long hours of debugging, more than what was saved through AI code generation. It is thus important to use AI wisely.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/331#issuecomment-3931459913" expanded>

### 116. TENG..SHIN `@YeeShin504` (8 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/730" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: What is the difference between Low and Medium severity?**
</div>

The guidelines provided by the website gives the following definition:
- `severity.Low`: A flaw that is unlikely to affect normal operations of the product. Appears only in very rare situations and causes a minor inconvenience only.
- `severity.Medium`: A flaw that causes occasional inconvenience to some users, but they can continue to use the product.

I found some bugs that could arguably be caused by typos in the commands and gave them `Medium` severity because I think its reasonable to assume that this happens occasionally and without any error messages presented, it should not be a minor inconvenience, since it is harder to detect and correct the error. The developer team downgraded the severity to `Low` because they believe that such typos rarely happen and it is minor because it is recoverable.

How should I interpret the rarity and amount of inconvenience to the user?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/680#issuecomment-4276168179" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

i think this can still be a bug candidate

putting it in the dg alone doesn’t really make it fine, because duplicate checking is something that affects normal user behaviour, so if it matters during normal usage, the ug should make it clear too

from a user point of view, it is quite reasonable to feel confused if two people with the same name cannot be added, especially since same-name people are very normal in real life. so if the ug never makes this limitation clear, i think there is a fair angle for a ug/documentation bug

for feature flaw, a bit more risky though. if their dg planned enhancement already says they want to improve duplicate detection, then reporting “their duplicate detection is too strict / not good enough” may get pushed back as already planned

so overall i’d say: not automatically fine just because it is written in the dg. the safer angle is probably that the ug does not explain this current limitation clearly enough

but this is just my personal perspective. take it as a grain of salt. enginerring decision is part of learning also!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/685#issuecomment-4276188936" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

i don’t think “not in ug = automatically not a bug” is the right way to read it

my understanding is more like: for user-facing behaviour, the ug is a very important reference point, but undocumented behaviour can still be a bug depending on what kind of behaviour it is

for example:

* if it is a simple and very intuitive behaviour, then not documenting it may be fine
* if it is a hidden / non-obvious / important limitation or behaviour that affects how users use the app, then not documenting it can definitely be a documentation bug
* if the actual behaviour itself is bad for normal usage, then it can even be a feature flaw / functionality bug, not just a ug bug

i think the course notes also mention something along the lines that undocumented features are sometimes okay if they are easily discoverable and intuitive, but otherwise it can point to an omission in the ug or a not-properly-released feature
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/743#issuecomment-4337187046" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

i think yes, diagram a should still be wrong and diagram b should be the correct one.

reason being p1, p2 and p3 in diagram a are all forked from the same fork node, but p1/p2 are joined earlier while p3 is only joined later at a different join node. that seems to contradict the textbook example that says all parallel paths starting from the same fork should end at the same join.

diagram b avoids that by using nested concurrency: first (p1+p2 branch) in parallel with p3, and then inside the left branch, p1 and p2 are forked and joined together before continuing to b and a.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/754#issuecomment-4358528176" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

yeah i agree with hongshan on this one. i wouldn’t show a self-dependency here. (but just a grain of salt)

for something like hassamename(task other), it’s true that task depends on task in a very literal sense, but putting a dependency arrow from task back to itself usually does not add much useful information to the diagram. it’s kind of too trivial / implicit from the method signature already.

i think self-links are more meaningful for associations, where the class actually stores references to objects of the same class. for short-term usage dependency like this, i would just omit it unless there is some strong reason to highlight it.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/761#issuecomment-4363686527" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

i’m not too convinced by that, mainly because the question says “without any loss of utility”, and i think that’s the tricky part.

even if cd is more expressive and can technically represent what ccd represents, ccd is still more fit-for-purpose for pure domain modelling. it forces you to stay at the conceptual level and avoid mixing in software/design details too early. so if you replace ccd with cd, you may not lose expressive power, but you do lose that modelling discipline / clarity benefit.

so i’d say “cd can subsume ccd” is true in one sense, but that alone may not be enough to justify “no loss of utility”. 

but anyway, i am not super confident for my ans, and let's see what prof can offer!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/760#issuecomment-4363699389" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

yeah i roughly agree with jolene.

for 1 and 2, i think showing bar() makes sense, because from a reader’s point of view, a does have that operation as part of its interface / contract, whether it is still abstract or already implemented there.

for 3, i probably wouldn’t show it by default if it is just inherited unchanged from the parent. otherwise the diagram can get messy quite fast. i’d only show it if there is some reason to highlight it, like overriding it or wanting to emphasize that operation in a.

so i guess my view is more like: show it if it helps us understand a, but if it is just inherited and adds nothing new, can omit.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/174" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4. :fas-info-circle: Question on catching exceptions**
</div>


Hi everyone, I wanted to ask about best practices for exception handling and validation design.
Which approach is generally considered better or more commonly used in software engineering?

### Option 1:
Have a single class responsible for checking a particular condition and throwing the corresponding exception. Other classes assume this validation has already been done and do not repeat the same checks.

### Option 2:
Allow multiple classes to independently check for the same condition and throw exceptions for the same error cases, even if the validation may be duplicated.

In practice, which approach is preferred?

Thanks!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/133#issuecomment-3870537572" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

1. The guide is detailed, concise, and easy to follow.
2. Following the user guide, I tried all the actions, i.e., todo, deadline, event, list, mark, unmark, delete, find, tag, bye. No major bugs detected.
3. The error message is clear, although there is only one date format allowed, which seems a bit too strict. Maybe consider allowing more formats?
4. The personality is fun and creative, but the long line "---" goes to the second row, which makes the replying message a little bit less neat.
5. The tag extension is implemented correctly, and the idea of allowing more flexible input (can be with or without "#") is great.
Overall good work! 

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/680#issuecomment-4312550297" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think tthis can still be a bug candidate, but more safely as a UG/documentation bug rather than a feature flaw.

Reason: the PE instructions say users test based on the product behavior and the UG PDF, not the DG, so the fact that the current duplicate rule is mentioned only in the DG does not really help the end user. If the restriction affects normal usage, the UG should make it clear. Also, the PE page says if a bug matches a Planned Enhancement, reporting it as a feature flaw is risky because such bugs can be rejected. So if their Planned Enhancement already says they want to improve duplicate detection, I would avoid the “their duplicate logic is too strict” angle.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/721#issuecomment-4312552654" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think grouping is usually better if the failures lead to the same kind of behavior.

The textbook’s use case example does exactly that: instead of listing every possible invalid input separately, it uses one extension like “system detects an error in the entered data”, then shows the recovery flow from there. That keeps the MSS clean and avoids repetitive low-level validation details.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/736#issuecomment-4312575535" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Not just the textbook, since the official exams page says this:
"In addition, you are expected to be aware of SE-related things (i.e., tools, techniques, concepts) encountered while doing the iP and the tP e.g., Was testing technique X used in the tP?.
That is, you are expected to strengthen your SE knowledge (not just hands-on skills) as you do the course projects -- in particular, to be able to connect theory to practice."
So Git should be in-scope in the broad sense, because it is one of the tools/concepts we used in the projects.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/755#issuecomment-4367418978" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**

Really meaningful discussion! Just want to add that the textbook sentence may be referring to the situation that the dash line representing dependency can be omitted if there is already a solid line (inheritance, associations, etc.).
</panel>

**3. :fas-info-circle: [iP] Intellij import code style settings**

in  [](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/intellijCodeStyle.html)

suggested code style:
<img width="985" height="600" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/45cddc88-ceb2-4004-971c-b2961e178754" />

my Intellij interface, since the suggested style does not include module imports and static imports, is it ok for me to do it in this order

<img width="940" height="316" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/afbb9034-19d1-41f3-b977-ec3892da8b0c" />

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/22#issuecomment-3773429940" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

From my understanding, index contain all the entries of all the files, so whenever u commit it tracks all the changed and unchanged files using the index(in the staging area) 

And yes, files in the index are tracked by git(even with no changes in this commit), so git will include it in the next snapshot

So when u git add u kind of update the entries then when u git commit git just look at the entries
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/72#issuecomment-3800532657" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Ahh i missed the grey line thanks for reminding

I guess if we place "import all other imports" above "import module imports", then "import module imports" will become redundant and therefore follows the suggested style

I will close this post after a few days? 
Since I see many have not reach this part yet
Hope it helps!
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> * Device used: Windows
> 
> Hello! The application works according to the User Guide, including saving and loading functionality and commands working as expected. However for some invalid inputs perhaps is it better for the error message to show in the application instead of on the terminal?
> 
> In this case I used a mark command on a task index that was out of bounds.  
> Thank you!

Hi @woshingmachine 
Thank you for your help with testing the application!

Also, thank you for pointing out the issue and for the suggestion regarding the error message. That was indeed a good catch. I have updated the application so that the error message now appears directly in the GUI instead of the terminal.

The fix will be included in the final version of the project. Thanks again for your feedback!

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/368#issuecomment-3994793543" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Hello! Testing this on Mac Sequoia!
> 
> Maybe setting a limit or constraint on minimum height and width of the window pane can prevent such scenarios?
> 
> Maybe preserving invalid user commands can be better for user experience? Users can see what they originally type and perhaps a more informative error message can be provided (e.g for this scenario user can be reminded on providing a description before the `/by &lt;time>`
> 
> Encountered this when using the mark command and inputting an index that was out of bounds on CLI

Hi @derrikzzz 
Thank you for taking the time to test the application and for the detailed feedback!

Those are very good suggestions. Setting a minimum window size and preserving invalid user commands are both great ideas to improve the user experience. I also appreciate you pointing out the issue with the `mark` command when the index is out of bounds.

I have addressed this issue and improved the error handling so that the application now responds with a proper error message instead of throwing an exception.

Thanks again for your helpful suggestions!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/368#issuecomment-3994799669" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Here's one for Linux!
> 
> OS Used: Linux
> 
> Encountered the same issue for invalid mark and delete index
>
> Tested all commands in User Guide, all commands are working, error handling on invalid tasks and dates works well.
> 
> Not sure if this is an issue, bye command doesn't cause the application to exit.
> 
> Overall, nice work I like that the UI looks clean!

Hi @jolenechong 
Thank you for testing the application on Linux and for the detailed feedback!

I have fixed the issue with the `mark` and `delete` commands when an index is out of bounds so that the application now handles the error properly instead of throwing an exception.

Regarding the `bye` command, you are right — in the current version it only returns the farewell message and the user still needs to close the application manually. That is a very good suggestion, and I have updated it so that the application exits properly when the `bye` command is used.

Thanks again for your help with testing and for the helpful suggestions. I’m also glad you liked the UI!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/368#issuecomment-3994804071" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> OS: Mac
> 
> Testing: Good job as all seems fine. I just found a very minor bug (Not sure if it is intended as such) but the error message for no task description seems to point to missing dates instead (e.g. I tried to input _"event /from 2026-12-12 /to 2026-12-12"_ but the error message is as shown in the screenshot). Maybe you could look into it or maybe have a different kind of exception possibly :)

Hi @jaris2203 
Thank you for testing the application on Mac and for pointing this out!

That’s a good catch. The error message in this case should indeed indicate that the task description is missing rather than referring to missing dates. I will update the error handling to provide a more accurate and informative message for this scenario.

Thanks again for the helpful feedback and for taking the time to test the application!
</panel>

</panel>

You can also avoid having to manually key in your credentials every time by using SSH keys. 
[https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account
](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account)

Once you have your system's SSH key set up on github you can clone using the repo's SSH address instead of the HTTPS address and you won't have to keep signing in.
</panel>

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think for the week 6 tutorial, it is guaranteed that one path will be executed due to the fact that forward == !backward. This condition guarantees that at least one of these conditions is true. Aside from this specific example, in an alt path at most 1 path can execute, which means either one path is taken, or, if none of the conditions are met, none of the paths is taken. I think this is also what distinguishes alt paths from optional paths.
</panel>

### 127. SONG..CHEN `@songqiaoyuchen` (7 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/704" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Assigning Rejected/NotInScope/CannotReproduce Bugs**
</div>

If the team is giving a response.Rejected, response.NotInScope or response.CannotReproduce, is there still a need to assign the bug to a team member?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/140#issuecomment-3852839364" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I also encountered similar issues as a Windows + VS Code user. 
Gradle gave me this error:
> Unsupported class file major version 65

This caused VS Code not being able to recognise this as a Java project and everything just failed from there. 

I found out this is because Gradle is is using the Java Runtime Environment provided by _Language Support for Java(TM) by Red Hat_
Their documentation says this:
> Now that Java extension will publish platform specific versions, it will embed a JRE for supported platforms such as win32-x64, win32-arm64, linux-x64, linux-arm64, darwin-x64, darwin-arm64. The embedded JRE is used to launch the Language Server for Java. Users are only responsible for configuring [Project JDKs](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=redhat.java#project-jdks) to compile your Java projects.

To fix, open VS Code user settings (JSON) and add in the following:
```
"java.import.gradle.java.home": "C:\\Path\\To\\Your\\JDK"
```
Updating Gradle to a newer version also fixes the issue, though I am not too sure if this is allowed. @damithc
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/244#issuecomment-3919446155" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**OS**: Windows, Linux (Ubuntu)

Nice work! I like the minimalistic style of your UI. There are no major issues found except those already mentioned. 
I can probably suggest a few minor improvements. 
1. Empty dialogboxes: Maybe disable send if the input is empty?
2. Minification aliasing: I checked that your image quality is actually much higher than what appears in the screenshot. This could be due to downsampling of the image to fit the display size. You may want to try loading the image at a smaller scale and using a better quality filtering algorithm by `new Image(IMG_STREAM, requestedWidth=2X_DISPLAY_SIZE, requestedHeight=2X_DISPLAY_SIZE, preserveRatio = true, smooth = true)` instead of simply `new Image(IMG_STREAM)`.

Hope it helps :D

<img width="379" height="253" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/60018f56-43f4-45be-b64d-1e61aab599d1" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/157#issuecomment-3940875180" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Thanks for raising this solution. I have a minor add-on:

When I tried it out, I noticed the autoscroll is not triggered consistently. Sometimes it doesn't react when new dialogboxes appear. 

This could be due to the vvalue being updated before the the new height is calculated. Calling `scrollPane.layout()` forces JavaFX to calculate the new size before `setVvalue`, which appears to solve this issue for me.
</panel>

</panel>

</panel>

### 131. ONG .. HOW `@CH144` (7 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/442" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Accidentally merged without leaving review comment**
</div>

I merged another member's PR for adding the profile picture after checking all requirements were correct, but without leaving a review comment / approval. Do I need to revert the merge and that member needs to send a new PR to be approved properly?

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/244#issuecomment-3916583484" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**OS:** Linux (Kali)

**Finding:** tester was able to create an `event` with a later "from date" than "to date"

**Severity:** _Low_

**Rationale:**
- normal user is unlikely to make such error
- there are no critical functionalities that depend on the correctness of time

**Payload:**
```
event test /from 31/12/2000 0000 /to 01/01/2000 0000
```

**Evidence:**

<img width="405" height="209" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/01ce140b-27b6-4780-85bc-6bae0d38c26f" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/285#issuecomment-3925618388" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**OS**: Linux (Kali)
#

**Finding:** `delete`, `mark` and `unmark` index references the master list instead of the found list


**Severity:**
- `delete` _High_
- `mark` and `unmark`: _Medium_

**Rationale:**
- When the task list is cluttered, user is likely to search for tasks to identify the index to use for `delete`, `mark` and `unmark`
- User may be under the assumption that the index will follow from the output of `find`, rather than the master list
- This results in the user deleting the wrong task in the case of `delete`
- Defeats the purpose of `find` as the user now needs to identify the master index to use the correct index

**Detailed walkthrough:**

Step 1: create 2 tasks that are not substrings of each other with `todo`
```
todo abc
```
```
todo xyz
```

Step 2: confirm the order of tasks with `list`
```
list
```

<img width="856" height="337" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6ad1abc4-1010-4f34-90d4-086e7507ef19" />

Step 3: use `find` on the greater task name
```
find xyz
```

<img width="859" height="324" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7414c35-8c6e-47fb-8f1b-ad7ddbee15a4" />

Step 4: perform any of `mark`, `unmark`, or `delete` using the index shown by `find`
```
mark 1
```
```
unmark 1
```
```
delete 1
```

<img width="862" height="604" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/31b14eae-fa91-455f-9dc3-128f39e1167e" />

#

**Finding:** Unexpected error message "The number is not a valid index in the task list." when finding tasks that do not exist.

**Severity:** _VeryLow_

**Rationale:** Purely cosmetic issue.

**Payload:**
```
find doesnotexists
```

**Evidence:**

<img width="856" height="252" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9be632b9-ae8e-4e14-a926-b7f18f957f4c" />

#

**Additional notes:** The ASCII art is very cute!

#
Edit: typo
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/311#issuecomment-3927746407" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**OS:** Linux (kali)

**Finding:** ` | ` in task names cause unexpected behavior after the app restarts
1. `todo`: everything to the right of the `|` is not loaded
2. `deadline`: app ***crashes***
3. `event`: task is not loaded at all

**Severity:** _Medium_

**Rationale:**
- app crash is severe
- however, chances of a normal user using ` | ` in the task name is low

**Payload 1:**
```
todo buy (chocolate | vanilla) birthday cake
```

**Evidence 1:**

<img width="407" height="179" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d6bcc2f1-e5d0-4527-afb8-d1a2bc3275ff" />

<img width="407" height="248" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/061f85bd-6daf-4564-956a-41c60c3dde8a" />

#

**Payload 2:**
```
deadline buy (chocolate | vanilla) birthday cake /by 2000-01-01
```

**Evidence 2:**

<img width="401" height="191" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3010468a-d2b5-4469-8b4a-4e6be6b62a17" />

<img width="1310" height="613" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bba0e8b1-7d2a-4592-a323-ccf839d6b304" />

#

**Payload 3:**
```
event (tennis | badminton) /from 1600 /to 1700
```

**Evidence 3:**

<img width="410" height="177" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/36409c01-a22c-48d3-8026-a3d1aa047a31" />

<img width="408" height="249" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6499ab87-7522-4788-ac99-d83bac92572b" />

#
Edit: formatting
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/342#issuecomment-3931630720" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**OS:** Linux (Kali)

#

**Finding:** Incorrect output example in user guide for `event` command in `list`

**Severity:** _VeryLow_

**Rationale:**
- command example is *correct*
- output example is *incorrect*
- may cause some confusion, but a user following the instructions would not encounter any issues
- additional help is provided in the app itself

**Evidence:**

<img width="385" height="247" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bd5ab52a-5e2b-4795-89ad-5d06462f853b" />

<img width="420" height="287" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2507603c-9f2a-42f9-b9c2-f6544319c4c9" />

#

**Finding:** Empty `find` cannot be entered, but `find ` (with whitespace) can be entered

**Severity:** _Low_

**Rationale:**
- A normal user would not be searching for nothing

**Evidence:**

<img width="415" height="296" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/65d95e66-ea0c-4f08-a422-c1f41f98bf51" />

#
**Additional notes:**
- I like the effort put into error handling within the app itself.
- It allows the user to learn and correct command mistakes entirely within the app, which I feel is important for offline apps.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/369#issuecomment-3932712261" expanded>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/203#issuecomment-3902978408" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yes, personally I think using an enum is a valid way to avoid magic literals for your case (e.g. commands like EXIT, LIST). It groups related values and gives them meaningful names. 

In general, as long as you don’t have unexplained “raw” values (numbers/strings) dangling around in the code, and they’re given meaningful names (via enum or static final), you’re good.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/251#issuecomment-3918549525" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi 

OS: macOS (MacBook Pro 14-inch, Apple M4, 16 GB RAM)

I ran the provided Stitch.jar and carried out smoke testing based on the user guide.
All core commands (todo, list, mark) worked as expected. Tasks were added, listed, and marked correctly.

<img width="394" height="625" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/47fbb935-403a-45e7-b4cd-c0ea6797e677" />

> [!WARNING]  
> After running the `bye` command, the app is still running instead of exiting.

>[!TIP]
>The `bye` command currently prints the goodbye message but does not terminate the app.
You may want to explicitly exit the main loop or call `System.exit(0) / Platform.exit() `so that the application stops running after bye.
</panel>

Overall, the application looks clean. Noiccce! 👍

From the screenshot, I don't think there are any gradle files in your repo, try downloading duke source file again and copying the gradle folder over
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/154#issuecomment-3889588859" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I would recommend changing it to non-static, as static methods are much harder to debug and follow OOP principles. Test scripts and unit tests are also harder because you cannot isolate methods as easily (e.g you can fix one unit test only to find it causes another one to break).
</panel>

Personally, I usually start with a non-static class if im not sure as I feel that it helps keep the design "open" while the requirements are still unclear. As the project iterates, if there is no meaningful behaviour, I feel that refactoring it into having static methods is easier than if I were to do the opposite.

</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815196944" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You should use static for classes that perform stateless tasks, and non-static for those that manage specific data or external resources. 

Writing static classes makes them easier to call, but non-static instances are easier to test, as they allow you to swap configurations (like file paths), and prevent 'global variable' bugs that pop up when shared a state is modified unexpectedly.

Personally, going the non-static approach is better for the Storage class. By using an instance, you can pass the file path into the constructor. For example, your main program can use ```data/tasks.txt```, while your unit tests can instantiate a separate Storage object pointing to ```data/test.txt``` to avoid wiping your user data during testing.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/133#issuecomment-3839309426" expanded>
<div slot="header">

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/755#issuecomment-4357355567" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Ultimately, I believe this depends on what the author intends to show with their model, as showing a dependency would give a better high-level overview of the interaction between classes. But with reference to this course, dependencies are explicitly shown even when captured as method parameters. An example is found in Week 8's Tutorial Solutions: `StockItem`'s constructor takes a `Rating`, and a dependency is drawn from `StockItem` to `Rating`. A similar observation can be made with the dependency between `Billable` and `Inventory`.

<img width="917" height="630" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b1ad4f86-77f9-4621-b45e-cb3dd514ce1f" />

The only time this course advises against showing a dependency is when an association line / inheritance / interfaces are already present.

<img width="1342" height="475" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b5deaeb9-8af2-4bac-b1a9-d1b9805ffe4e" />
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 144. HONG..ANYU `@legendgirlie47` (6 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/710" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Planned Enhancements bug immunity response**
</div>

What is the appropriate response to a bug that was reported during PE but was put in the DG under "Planned Enhancements" and hence granted immunity from PE bug reporting? Should it be responseNotInScope or responseRejected
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/658" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Name Validation**
</div>

In our application, we restricted the name field to alphanumeric characters and a small set of symbols (e.g. `- ' / .`) to ensure reliable command parsing and prevent unexpected behaviour from arbitrary inputs.

During PE-D, a tester flagged the lack of support for non-English characters (e.g. Chinese characters) as a FeatureFlaw bug.

While supporting a wider range of characters improves inclusivity, it also introduces additional complexity in validation and parsing, especially as this is a CLI-based application where prefixes and delimiters are involved. Additionally, our team feels that in contexts such as Singapore, names are often recorded in romanised form in official documents, which reduces the practical need for non-Latin input in some use cases.

Given this trade-off, would restricting names to just alphanumeric with certain symbols be considered a reasonable decision?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/172#issuecomment-3864325485" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi @bingmybongg,

I was running into the same issue initially, where both "run" and "jar" didn't work.

Here’s how I fixed it:

1. **Created a `Launcher` class** for the app:

2. **Rebuilt JAR using Gradle:**

'''bash
./gradlew clean shadowJar
'''

3. **Ran the JAR directly:** from the project root

'''bash
java -jar build/libs/&lt;filename>.jar
'''

After this, everything worked.
No more “JavaFX runtime components are missing” errors.

You can check my full `build.gradle` here: [[build.gradle on GitHub](https://github.com/SUN-HEQIAO/ip/blob/master/build.gradle)]

For reference, I followed the recommendations in this SE-EDU tutorial: [[Creating JAR files](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/jar.html)]


</panel>

> Perhaps you did not create a fat JAR (a JAR file that includes dependencies such as JavaFX in it) because personally the sizes of JAR files for the full project that I have seen, including mine, are more than 10 MB so I suspect that based on the size of the JAR file, it is not a fat JAR. To do so, you can run the `shadowJar` task included in Gradle.
> 
> For more about fat JAR files you can find them [here](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/jar.html).

Hi, thank you again for testing and for pointing this out earlier.

I have rebuilt the application and updated the release with a new bit.jar that bundles the JavaFX runtime properly using the shadowJar task. The latest JAR file is now around 10 MB, which should include the necessary JavaFX dependencies.

Would you mind downloading the latest bit.jar from the release page and giving it another try?

Please let me know if the issue still persists. Thank you again for helping test this.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/718#issuecomment-4363407496" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think your point is actually reasonable. The con is not wrong, but it can be phrased more precisely.

It is true that white box tests can still refer to specifications. However, in practice they are still heavily influenced by the implementation because they focus on covering code structure such as branches, paths, and conditions.

The main issue is that white box testing is good at checking whether the implemented logic works correctly, but it is weaker at catching things that were never implemented in the first place.

For example, suppose there is a requirement that the system should reject negative inputs, but the developer forgets to implement that check. A white box test can still achieve full coverage of the existing code and pass. The missing requirement would not be detected because there is no code path for it.

So the con is still valid. A clearer way to phrase it would be:

White box testing is biased toward the current implementation, so it may miss requirement level defects, especially when functionality is missing or incorrectly specified.

In short, it checks whether the code works as written, but not necessarily whether the right code was written.
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>

**1. :fas-info-circle: Question regarding using streams**
</div>

Hi, I had a question regarding the code quality guidelines for using streams. Would using excessive stream chaining (e.g., long chains of stream operations in a single statement) be considered a violation of the code quality requirements, particularly in terms of avoiding complicated expressions? Or is it acceptable as long as the code remains reasonably understandable?

For example, would something like this be considered acceptable, or would it be better to break it into multiple steps?
```
List<String> filteredNames = names.stream() 
             .filter(name -&gt; name.length() > 3) 
             .sorted()                                 
             .map(String::toUpperCase)                 
             .collect(Collectors.toList());  
```

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/585" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: 💡IntelliJ unable to find existing classes from merge fix**
</div>

Not sure if anyone else also encountered this issue.

I had an issue while using IntelliJ where a new class added from a fetch-merge was unable to be detected. Despite the class existing and correct import statements, the code could not compile properly, constantly giving errors such as **_cannot find symbol_** or **_cannot access class_**

After some digging, I discovered that it is due to **stale index**, where IntelliJ does not update it's internal index of classes when new files are added through a Git Merge. As such, the new files are essentially invisible to IntelliJ. 

### To fix this, go to _File → Invalidate Caches → Invalidate and Restart_

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

@lemonishi is right. If the child can exist independently of parent, their relationship is considered an aggregation. You can think of it as a weaker "has-a" relation where both objects are not as reliant on each other (loose coupling)
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**3. :fas-info-circle: Typo in Tour 6: Exercise mix-messy-docs**
</div>

Hi prof, 

I believe there is a typo in Exercise mix-messy-docs (2nd last exercise) under Tour 6.

Under the task description 2nd point, it wrote: "Merge the **feature-search** branch onto the development branch, without using fast-forwarding (i.e., create a merge commit). Use the default commit message (i.e., Merge branch '**feature-delete**' into development)."

The default commit message should be "Merge branch **feature-search** into development".

The typo can be found here: https://git-mastery.org/lessons/branchDelete/exercise-mix-messy-docs.html

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/30" expanded>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/574#issuecomment-4116013822" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think method calls to static methods are received by the class itself, not an instance of that class.
Since we do not instantiate using `new ArgumentTokenizer`, drawing `:ArgumentTokenizer` with a colon should not be correct as the colon represents an instance. 
To model a static method call in a sequence diagram, you should omit the colon and use `&lt;&lt;class>>` stereotype in the participant box to show that it is the class itself. 
Therefore, it should be formatted to read `&lt;&lt;class>>` over `ArgumentTokenizer`.
Hope this helps
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 151. STUA..IONG `@press-stuart` (6 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/82" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: [Gitmastery] Local progress also deleted when running `gitmastery progress sync on` or `off` while accessing `progress` folder from another terminal**
</div>

When running the command `gitmastery progress sync on` or `off` while simultaneously accessing the `progress` folder in another terminal, the local `progress.json` is unexpectedly deleted. **(EDIT: see each Steps to Reproduce section for behaviours)** ~~in addition to the remote repo (fork) being deleted (expected).~~

**(Note about OneDrive)** My file path contains "OneDrive" but my files are not actually being synced to OneDrive. This is because years ago, I deleted OneDrive from my computer, but I continued to use the underlying folder structure for all local files.

### Steps to Reproduce (`sync off`)

1. Make sure progress sync is already enabled
2. Open Command Prompt and change the cwd to the `gitmastery-exercises/progress` folder
3. Open Git Bash and change the cwd to the gitmastery root folder `gitmastery-exercises`
4. On Git Bash, run `gitmastery progress sync off`
5. Confirm when prompted

After performing the above steps, an exception occurred. ~~The progress folder was empty and the remote on GitHub was deleted.~~ **(EDIT: clarification)** The remote on GitHub was deleted, which is expected. The `progress` folder was empty, which is unexpected. The expected behaviour is that `progress.json` remains.

See the log file at the bottom of this issue, starting from `[2026-01-27 18:55:30,756]`. The output on Git Bash is shown below.

```
stuar@LAPTOP-IGM8IJ09 MINGW64 ~/OneDrive/Documents/School/NUS/Y2S2/CS2103T/GitMasteryHome/gitmastery-exercises
$ gitmastery progress sync off
 CONFIRM  Are you sure you want to turn off syncing? [y/N]: y
 INFO  Checking that you have Git installed and configured
 INFO  Git is installed
 INFO  Git v2.47.1 meets the minimum version requirement.
 INFO  You have set user.name as Stuart Lim
 INFO  You have set user.email as 61983502+press-stuart@users.noreply.github.com
 SUCCESS  Git is installed and configured
 INFO  Checking that you have Github CLI is installed and configured
 INFO  Github CLI is installed
 INFO  You have authenticated Github CLI
 INFO  You have authenticated Github CLI with the 'delete_repo' scope
 SUCCESS  Github CLI is installed and configured
 INFO  Removing fork
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "shutil.py", line 631, in _rmtree_unsafe
PermissionError: [WinError 32] The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process: 'progress'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 6, in <module>
  File "app\cli.py", line 59, in start
  File "click\core.py", line 1485, in __call__
  File "click\core.py", line 1406, in main
  File "app\cli.py", line 18, in invoke
  File "click\core.py", line 1873, in invoke
  File "click\core.py", line 1873, in invoke
  File "click\core.py", line 1873, in invoke
  File "click\core.py", line 1269, in invoke
  File "click\core.py", line 824, in invoke
  File "click\decorators.py", line 34, in new_func
  File "app\hooks\in_gitmastery_root.py", line 38, in wrapper
  File "app\commands\progress\sync\off.py", line 55, in off
  File "app\utils\cli.py", line 25, in rmtree
  File "shutil.py", line 790, in rmtree
  File "shutil.py", line 635, in _rmtree_unsafe
  File "shutil.py", line 754, in onexc
  File "app\utils\cli.py", line 23, in force_remove_readonly
PermissionError: [WinError 32] The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process: 'progress'
[PYI-12844:ERROR] Failed to execute script 'main' due to unhandled exception!
```

### Steps to Reproduce (`sync on`)

1. Make sure the `progress` folder exists and `progress.json` is inside it. (I moved a backup `progress.json` inside after running the steps for `sync off`)
2. Open Command Prompt and change the cwd to the `gitmastery-exercises/progress` folder
3. Open Git Bash and change the cwd to the gitmastery root folder `gitmastery-exercises`
4. On Git Bash, run `gitmastery progress sync on`
5. Enter your passphrase to create the fork and clone it

After performing the above steps, an exception occurred. ~~A fork was indeed created on GitHub, but the local progress folder was empty.~~ **(EDIT: clarification)** A fork was created on GitHub, which is expected. The `progress` folder was empty, which is unexpected. The expected behaviour is that `progress.json` remains.

See the log file at the bottom of this issue, starting from `[2026-01-27 19:15:12,644]`. The output on Git Bash is shown below.

```
stuar@LAPTOP-IGM8IJ09 MINGW64 ~/OneDrive/Documents/School/NUS/Y2S2/CS2103T/GitMasteryHome/gitmastery-exercises
$ gitmastery progress sync on
 INFO  Checking that you have Git installed and configured
 INFO  Git is installed
 INFO  Git v2.47.1 meets the minimum version requirement.
 INFO  You have set user.name as Stuart Lim
 INFO  You have set user.email as 61983502+press-stuart@users.noreply.github.com
 SUCCESS  Git is installed and configured
 INFO  Checking that you have Github CLI is installed and configured
 INFO  Github CLI is installed
 INFO  You have authenticated Github CLI
 INFO  You have authenticated Github CLI with the 'delete_repo' scope
 SUCCESS  Github CLI is installed and configured
 INFO  Syncing progress tracker
 INFO  Checking if you have fork of git-mastery/progress
 WARN  You don't have a fork yet, creating one
Traceback (most recent call last):

PermissionError: [WinError 32] The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process: 'progress'

### Specifications

- Windows 11
- Git Bash terminal for gitmastery
- Command Prompt (I did not test the above with other terminals)
- gitmastery v7.7.0 (latest)
- git ver 2.47.1.windows.1
- gh version 2.85.0

### Additional Notes

OneDrive: My file path contains "OneDrive" but my files are not actually being synced to OneDrive. This is because years ago, I deleted OneDrive from my computer, but I continued to use the underlying folder structure for all local files.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/600#issuecomment-4169172720" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Some disorganized points:

Maybe we can consider how each UI view is updated in the MVC pattern. There is an association where View pulls data from Model. If there is more than one UI view, here is one possible approach: Each UI part pulls data from Model and does its own data processing. Now, every UI part has the same "template" of pulling data and processing data to be displayed. We can probably extract out this template into an Interface with an update() method. That pretty much gives us the Observer half of the Observer pattern. I think this grouping of UI classes improves cohesion.

However, there are other approaches that give similar results. A "UiMain" class could pull data and transfer it to all UI parts that implement some other interface, so Observers probably aren't much better there.

On the Observable side, I think the Observer pattern frees the coder from thinking about how to send data from Model to UI, and leaves it up to UI to figure everything out. In other words, Model does not depend on UI, which reduces coupling.

AB3 implicitly uses Observers in the form of javafx collections like FilteredList, which are clearly good because we don't need to test and debug them 👍 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/720#issuecomment-4306549794" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I haven't seen any evidence in the course explicitly allowing >1 end node. If any readers have seen it, feel free to share.

Regardless of whether they actually are allowed, I think it's fine to have >1 end node, since the meaning is clear. Forcing a diagram to have only 1 end node could make it cluttered as one would need to draw arrows from all "final actions" to the end node, even if those actions are far apart.

As a sidenote, some programming purists believe that all functions should have exactly one exit point. We have already gone against their ideals by using things like guard clauses because they are easier to read. I think you can draw the remaining links on your own (pun intended).
</panel>

</panel>

If the team has explicitly stated this in the user guide as a limitation, it indicates that they are aware of the behavior and have made an effort to communicate it clearly to users, allowing them to anticipate and work around it.

In my view, when something is well-documented and intentional, it should be treated as a known limitation that can be improved in future iterations, rather than being classified as a defect or bug.

### 158. BASI..I XI `@basilkuok` (5 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/325" expanded>
<div slot="header">

</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/46#issuecomment-3790202898" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

My understanding is as follow: 

In your setup, git revert HEAD  and git reset [--flag] HEAD~1 can achieve the same thing, which is to delete/undo the extra line "hi" you have committed locally, but not pushed to repo. 

However, if you use git revert, your local repo will be **2 commits ahead** of your remote. If you use git reset, your local repo will be the **same commit** as your remote (not ahead/behind)

In the beginning, you commit empty.txt locally and also push it to remote. Then, you commit an added line "hi" locally, but didn't push it to remote. At this point, your local repo is one commit ahead of the remote repo. Then, to delete/undo your "hi" line, you do either of the following:

- use git revert HEAD. As mentioned in your point 1., you basically make a new commit to erase the "hi" line added, and you didn't push this revert commit to remote. This create one more commit, and your local repo is now 2 commits ahead of your remote repo.

- use git reset [--flag] HEAD~1. You delete (actually is de-referenced) the current commit pointed under the HEAD, which is the added "hi" line. The HEAD move back to the previous commit (HEAD~1). So you local repo is now the same commit as your remote repo (not ahead/behind).

I agree with your point 1-4

Please feel free to correct me if I made any errors in understanding. Thank you.

</panel>

### 160. NGUY..BINH `@nutabi` (5 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/685" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Scope of possible bugs**
</div>



The way that the "Possible Functionality Bugs" section is worded suggests that the UG is the source of truth of what the app needs to do.

May I ask whether not documenting a feature/behaviour is considered a bug? It is also not mentioned as a possible bug under UG/DG. Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815436309" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Rule of thumb for me: `static` for anything stateless or global.

Longer answer, static classes either hold pure functions (like formatting/parsing, encoding/decoding, etc. since Java doesn't support standalone functions) or singletons - classes with a single global instance. So it all boils down to your design considerations: Do you only need one instance of the class you're creating?

For me, I almost never use static classes unless it's pure utility, since you could sometimes need multiple instances of UI, Storage, etc. for testing and what not.

Static method is simpler. I use static methods whenever they don't interact with the object states.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/228#issuecomment-3916065884" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Tested on MacOS. Since others have already tested for what you requested, I tried other novel tests.

1. Handling of non-ASCII characters

Your chat bot properly handle non-English languages, which is great.

2. Eager loading

Your chat bot currently only reads from disk once on startup. While this is okay, it could cause problems if users accidentally open and use more than 1 window at the same time. You could weigh the pros and cons of lazy loading for each command.

3. Corrupted data handling

Your chat bot currently _silently_ discards poorly formatted data without warning users. While discarding corrupted is a totally valid approach, doing so without warning the users could be problematic as the app will overwrite the discarded tasks, potentially causing data loss.

Other than that, great app! I love the dynamic background that works on different window sizes, screen sizes, and resolutions.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/539#issuecomment-4060746746" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think @DamianNgWZ explained the first part really well already.

For the branch on branch part, there are so many different scenarios for it, but these are the 2 most common ones that I notice.

First, many repositories have more than different "base" branches for different stages (1 for master/stable, 1 for nightly/dev/unstable, 1 for release candidate, etc.)

They will then proceed to merge from feature branches -&gt; dev -&gt; rc -&gt; stable). Many go as far as blocking direct pushes to dev/rc/stable in order to enforce different CI, approvals, etc. requirements.

Second and probably more applicable to tP, you might want to split a feature into sub-features. For example, I want to add Telegram handles to contacts, then I realise that this completely messes up everything - other commands, model, UI, even tests. Therefore, it might be more prudent to split the changes to each component to a separate branch.

I think this branch-on-branch is discouraged by the course, but it is still an approach nevertheless.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/685#issuecomment-4282352236" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Thanks @kenghow!
</panel>

</panel>

### 166. JUST..EARN `@JustATin555` (5 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/155" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Make Intellij IDEA respect case indentation for enhanced switch statements**
</div>

I already have 'Indent 'case' branches' set to false for ''switch' statement/expression, but IDEA still keeps indenting the cases - causing them to fail checkstyle.

'''java
        return switch (splitArgs[0]) ``{``
            case "bye" -&gt; new ParsedCommand(CommandType.BYE, null);
            case "list" -&gt; new ParsedCommand(CommandType.LIST, null);
            case "mark" -&gt; parseIndex(input, CommandType.MARK);
            case "unmark" -&gt; parseIndex(input, CommandType.UNMARK);
            case "todo" -&gt; parseTodo(input);
            case "deadline" -&gt; parseDeadline(input);
            case "event" -&gt; parseEvent(input);
            case "delete" -&gt; parseIndex(input, CommandType.DELETE);
            case "find" -&gt; parseFind(input);
            default -&gt; new InvalidCommand("""
                    Did you forget to start with a command?
                    Don't worry, we all get forgetful sometimes.""");
        ``}``;
'''
(Yes ik about the logic - I'm in the middle of a refactor)

Anyone know how to get IDEA to put the case in line with the switch?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/117#issuecomment-3830358163" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Feels like the solution would include a rebase or some sort of sync with upstream repo.
Could try this guide: https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/working-with-forks/syncing-a-fork
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/155#issuecomment-3857874612" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi all, none of the suggestions appear to have worked. On the other hand, I am confident that the issue is not to do with the return but rather with the enhanced switch expression as the following code is also indented wrongly:

'''java
        Task task = switch (params[0]) ``{``
            case "T" -&gt; ``{``
                if (params.length != 3) ``{``
                    throw new InvalidTaskParameterException();
                ``}``

                yield new Todo(params[2]);
            ``}``
            case "D" -&gt; ``{``
                if (params.length != 4) ``{``
                    throw new InvalidTaskParameterException();
                ``}``

                yield new Deadline(
                        params[2],
                        LocalDateTime.parse(params[3]));
            ``}``
            case "E" -&gt; ``{``
                if (params.length != 5) ``{``
                    throw new InvalidTaskParameterException();
                ``}``

                yield new Event(
                        params[2],
                        LocalDateTime.parse(params[3]),
                        LocalDateTime.parse(params[4]));
            ``}``
            default -&gt; throw new InvalidTaskParameterException();
        ``}``;
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/155#issuecomment-3857997823" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Specifically to the profs (@damithc ):

Would suppressing Checkstyle for enhanced switch statements be considered a violation of code style?

Alternatively, would it be preferred to strictly follow checkstyle by deactivating the formatter for enhanced switch statements as suggested above by @QR-Phua ?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/155#issuecomment-3858839072" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> for enhanced/lambda switch specifically:
> 
> > Lambda-style switch statements/expressions can have indented case blocks (as shown below):
> > '''
> > switch (condition) ``{``
> >     case ABC -&gt; method("1");
> >     case DEF -&gt; method("2");
> >     case XYZ -&gt; method("3");
> >     default -&gt; method("0");
> > ``}``
> > '''
> 
> source: [coding standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#layout)
> 
> so I believe it's optional

Huh, I must have completely missed that. Will close this issue then. Thanks all!
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

## Smoke test report (Ello iP)

- **OS:** Windows (11) 
- **Java:** Java 17 
- **How I ran it:** `java -jar ello.jar`

### Basic functionality (followed UG)
- ✅ `help`: Shows command list (todo/deadline/event/list/mark/unmark/delete/find/bye).
- ✅ `todo read book`: Added successfully.
- ✅ `deadline submit ip /by 2026-02-20 16:00`: Added successfully and formatted date displayed.
- ✅ `event meeting /from 20/2/2026 21:30 /to 20/2/2026 22:30`: Added successfully.
- ✅ `list`: Displays tasks correctly.
- ✅ `mark 1` / `unmark 1`: Works.
- ✅ `find book`: Works.
- ✅ `delete 2`: Works and list updates correctly.

### Error handling tests (as requested)
- ✅ **Missing `/by` marker**
  - Input: `deadline homework 20/2/2026 16:00`
  - Result: Clear error message + correct syntax shown.

- ✅ **Markers out of order**
  - Input: `event meeting /to 20/2/2026 22:30 /from 20/2/2026 21:30`
  - Result: Clear error message (marker order incorrect) + correct syntax shown.

- ✅ **Missing task description**
  - Input: `todo`
  - Result: Clear error message (missing description) + correct syntax shown.

- ✅ **Wrong date/time format**
  - Input: `deadline homework /by 20-02-2026 4pm`
  - Result: Rejected with an error message (no crash).

### Exit command + data persistency
- ✅ `bye`: Closes the program properly.
- ❌ `exit`: Not recognized (shows “I don’t know what that means. Type ‘help’ for help.”)
- ✅ **Data persistency:** Tasks persist after closing and reopening (startup shows “Loaded X task(s) from storage.”)

### Screenshots

## Smoke test report (PingguPro)

- **OS:** Windows (11) 
- **Java:** Java 17 
- **How I ran it:** `java -jar pinggu.jar`

### Startup / Storage
- ✅ App launches successfully.
- ✅ App creates a save file on first run:
  - `C:\Users\Zuriel\Downloads\Jar Smoke test\.\data\pinggu.txt`

### Basic functionality (commands)
- ✅ `todo read book` → task added successfully.
- ✅ `deadline Math project /by 2026-01-20` → deadline added; date displayed correctly (e.g., `Jan 20 2026`).
- ✅ `event Science Fair /from 2026-01-20 /to 2026-01-21` → event added successfully.
- ✅ `list` → shows tasks with indices.
- ✅ `mark 1` / `unmark 1` → marking/unmarking works.
- ✅ `delete 1` → deletes the specified task and updates task count.
- ✅ `find book` → command accepted (matching output shown in UI).

### Error handling / edge cases
- ✅ Out-of-range index: `mark 999`
  - Shows a clear error message (e.g., “does not have this task number! The max is 2.”)
- ✅ Invalid date range: `event Test /from 2026-01-21 /to 2026-01-20`
  - Rejected with error message (“End date must be before start date!”)
- ✅ Unknown command: `exit`
  - Not recognized (“does not recognize this command”)

### Exit behavior + data persistency
- ✅ `bye` exits the app properly (shows exit message + closes after ~3 seconds).
- ✅ Data persistency works:
  - After reopening the app, previously added tasks are still present and shown in `list`.
