<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/760#issuecomment-4366071418" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

My understanding is that a class diagram does not need to list every method that is available to a class, especially if the method is simply inherited and not changed. It should show the methods that are relevant to the model being communicated.

For the three cases:

1. If `A` inherits abstract/interface method `bar()` but does not redeclare or implement it, I would not repeat bar() in A; it is already implied by the parent.
2. If `A` gives a concrete implementation or explicitly redeclares/overrides `bar()`, then showing `bar()` in `A` is useful.

3. If the parent already provides a concrete implementation and `A` simply inherits it unchanged, I would not show `bar()` again in `A`, unless the purpose of the diagram is specifically to highlight that inherited API.

So the deciding factor is whether `A`  itself adds, overrides, or highlights that operation, not merely whether objects of type `A` can call it.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/760#issuecomment-4366143130" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

</panel>

</panel>

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

While Student IDs provide a unique identifier without the need for manual indexing, they can be cumbersome for users to input and are prone to typographical errors. Our group opted for an index-based system to prioritize ease of use. Maybe you can consider a hybrid approach that supports both methods, allowing users to choose the identifier they find most convenient.

Hi, CS2103T students are not required to bid for a tutorial slot.

Reference: [Course Website Week 1 Announcements](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week1/index.html)

[CS2103T Students] (not applicable to CS2103):

- Of the many weekly sessions that appear under CS2103T, only two actually belong to CS2103T. Those are, the lecture slot on Friday 1600-1800, and the 1-hour tutorial slot. The other 3-hour slots belong to the CS2101 course. All CS2101 sessions start in week 1.

- **There is no need for you to bid for a CS2103T tutorial slot in the upcoming tutorial selection exercise -- the 1-hour CS2103T slot (mis-labelled as 'lecture') in your timetable is your tutorial slot.**
</panel>

```

### 172. SEBA..LEIB `@alieron` (5 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/221" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: 💡Getting Netlify working for PR preview**
</div>

For those following the [Jekyll](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/jekyll.html) docs set up who are interested in using [Netlify](https://se-education.org/guides/tutorials/netlify.html) to deploy previews of your site when making pull requests(PRs). You might encounter an error when trying to deploy the site for the first time.

This is because the tP was configured to use bundler 2.1.4, which is not compatible with the version of Ruby on Netlify.

To fix this, first ensure you are in the `docs/` directory.
Update bundler to be version 2.7.2, and update the packages accordingly with:

'''bash
bundle update --bundler=2.7.2
bundle update
'''

You should see some changes in `docs/Gemfile.lock`.

You can now run a local test, first ensure all updated packages have been installed:

'''bash
bundle install
'''

then, to host a local server:

'''bash
bundle exec jekyll serve
'''

or, to test that the build works:

'''bash
bundle exec jekyll build
'''

If you are concerned about the limited free build time provided by Netlify, two options you can consider: 
1. a custom github workflow that calls the Netlify API on pull requests, only triggering preview builds if files in the `docs/` directory are modified
2. a github action like [rossjrw/pr-preview-action](https://github.com/rossjrw/pr-preview-action) that deploys the site on a sub-directory in github pages **(although PRs from forks are not supported)**

So far, I haven't been able to find much of an advantage from using Netlify to justify the set up required. Markdown and PlantUML previews give a sufficient idea of how the site will look, and only extra elements like the header *(which you're unlikely to edit much)* will need a full build. So consider this when deciding to use Netlify.

Any alternatives or ideas are welcome, make a comment so we can discuss. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/117#issuecomment-3830386930" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

My theory is that after you created your fork, you downloaded the files via zip instead of cloning the repo.

This would explain why the history is completely divergent and why you had to force push.

To fix this:
1. backup your changes to another local branch
```
git branch -m changes
```

2. add the root ip repo as upstream
```
git remote add upstream https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip.git
```

3. pull the master branch from upstream
```
git checkout -b master upstream/master
git pull
```

4. rebase changes into master
```
git switch changes
git rebase master
```

This will create merge conflicts in 3 files that you would have to resolve.

After this your fork should have matching history with the upstream repo and you may want to rename your branch back to master.
</panel>

	at com.sun.javafx.application.LauncherImpl.startToolkit(LauncherImpl.java:659)
	at com.sun.javafx.application.LauncherImpl.launchApplication1(LauncherImpl.java:679)
	at com.sun.javafx.application.LauncherImpl.lambda$launchApplication$2(LauncherImpl.java:196)
	at java.base/java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:840)
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/321#issuecomment-3927583818" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It's possible that the version of java you used to package the jar is not 17. For some reason gradle can use a different version of the jdk if it's present on your computer, even though you asked it to use jdk 17 as per the intellij config.


Adding this into the `build.gradle`, before the dependencies section, fixed it for me:
```
java ``{``
    toolchain ``{``
        languageVersion = JavaLanguageVersion.of(17)
    ``}``
``}``
```

This forces gradle to use jdk 17 to compile your jar file.

Recompile using shadowjar after.
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>

### 178. NAVI..IANG `@nvdchw` (4 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/138#issuecomment-3841359883" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I don’t think you are required to rectify the commits for Level 9, as the ```Git Standard``` requirement only tracks your latest five iP commits and is not tied to any specific week. Therefore, you just need to ensure that your commit messages from Week 4 onwards comply with the standard so that it becomes and remains green.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/133#issuecomment-3877862163" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<img width="603" height="892" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e7ecc422-8f95-40b8-8807-a4f1bfdb9d07" />

Here is my GUI. I followed up to part 5 of the tutorial, including setting an image as the background, resizing the window and changing the dialog colour for different commands.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/187#issuecomment-3891478625" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I do not think your code violates SLAP, as SLAP is more about conceptual abstraction rather than data types. Since '''isExit''' represents the loop control state, it still supports the same level of abstraction which is to greet the user, get user input and handle commands. 

However, if you want a stronger separation of responsibilities, you could extract the command loop into a separate method:
'''java
public void run() ``{``
    ui.greet();
    runCommandLoop();
``}``

private void runCommandLoop() ``{``
    boolean isExit = false;

    while (!isExit) ``{``
        String fullCommand = readUserCommand();
        isExit = executeUserCommand(fullCommand);
    ``}``
``}``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/720#issuecomment-4365739271" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yes, it is okay to have multiple exit nodes, which we learned in Tutorial 10. See solution to [Ex 10] from T10-DG-Extracts-Answers.pdf in canvas [handouts](https://canvas.nus.edu.sg/courses/85377/files/folder/handouts). 

<img width="1015" height="711" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9bdac2f5-dc4f-405d-9988-a1feabc1e203" />
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi,
OS Used: Windows.
Java Version: Java 17

Similar to the previous tester, I also encountered the same problem when trying to run the command `java -jar guma.jar`:

```
Error: LinkageError occurred while loading main class guma.Launcher
        java.lang.UnsupportedClassVersionError: guma/Launcher has been compiled by a more recent version of the Java Runtime (class file version 65.0), this version of the Java Runtime only recognizes class file versions up to 61.0
```

It seems that you are currently using Java 21 (based on the class file version). 

Hope you are able to resolve the issue!
</panel>

### 180. SAYY..LLAH `@lamemario` (4 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/492" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: 💡Avoid PRs from your forks master branch to get contribution credits**
</div>

<img width="2561" height="552" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a9472b63-2670-4cf7-b095-13c20927e99f" />

I encountered this bot message and was wondering where I went wrong so I asked my TA about it.  Here's what I learnt:

When you do the PRs, you need to make a PR from your personal fork's branch you created for a particular feature to the master branch of our team repo. If you make a PR from your personal fork's master branch to our team repo's master, then it apparently wont count for participation and you'd get this bot message to notify you of your mistake.

Hope this helps.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/491" expanded>

**3. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**

Link to JAR file: https://github.com/lamemario/ip/releases/tag/A-Release
Link to the User Guide: https://lamemario.github.io/ip/
Which OS'es to test on: Windows | macOS | Linux

**Things to specifically check:**
1. **Date Formatting:** Run `deadline milestone /by 2026-10-10`. Ensure the output month is formatted clearly (e.g., "Oct 10 2026") without any `?` characters (This was a weird old issue I had)
2. **Chronology Guard:** Run `event time travel /from 2026-10-10 /to 2026-10-09`. It should reject it and output a custom red error message about moving backwards.
3. **Persistence:** Add a task, type `bye` to close the app, then reopen it. The task should still be there. 
4. **Persona:** Try an invalid command (like `find` with no keyword) to trigger the custom Toronto slang error UI (Quite funny :D)

Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing
</panel>

### 181. POON..SHUA `@EepyAdventure` (4 posts)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/617" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to use OpenRouteServiceAPI and Leaflet**
</div>

##Library
[OpenRouteService API](https://openrouteservice.org/)

##Purpose
Used for geocoding delivery addresses into coordinates, optimising multi-stop delivery routes using the VROOM vehicle routing engine (VRPTW), and returning road-following route geometry encoded as polylines.

##License
[MIT License](https://github.com/GIScience/openrouteservice/blob/main/LICENSE)

##Library
[Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/)

##Purpose
Used to render an interactive map inside a JavaFX WebView, displaying the optimised delivery route as road-following polylines, numbered stop markers, and a depot start marker.

##License
[BSD 2-Clause License](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet/blob/main/LICENSE)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/617#issuecomment-4193431216" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As for how to secure the API key, I am considering using a free Cloudflare worker with rate limit. Not sure if that counts as "remote server" though. Another possibility is to have it be stored as a github secret and only called when building. Not sure how good that would be if someone unzips or decompiles the Jar though. I can also make the user create their own key by directing them to a guide and then inputting it into the app. It should be noted as well that ORS have a built in rate limit and I am using the free tier. 
</panel>

> [@EepyAdventure](https://github.com/EepyAdventure) Yes, this would count as 'your own remote server' as you can shut it down anytime, rendering the feature no longer 'testable' after that. So, this option will violate a tP constraint.

>

**1. :fas-info-circle: Cannot get SourceTree to accept a Github personal access token**
</div>

I installed Sourcetree. When I try to set up either a newly generated Personal Access Token for Github, it gets rejected. Here are my steps:

1) Open SourceTree

2) Tools | Options | Authentication

3) Click Add

4) Set Hosting Service to Github.

5) Set Authentication to Personal Access Token

6) Click Refresh Personal Access Token

7) A Windows Security dialog opens including the instruction "Enter your PAT as the password for https://github.com/"

8) Put my GitHub user name in the User Name textbox. (This username works for logging into GitHub.)

9) Paste the Personal Access Token into the Password textbox.

10) Click OK.

Observed: Login failed. Authentication failed for GitHub with username &lt;my user name> - please check your account settings.

Expected: This to work.

Tried looking for people facing the same problem online, and found this user who faced the same problem: https://community.atlassian.com/forums/Sourcetree-questions/Cannot-get-SourceTree-to-accept-a-Github-personal-access-token/qaq-p/2943383

They suggested deleting the Atlassian folders they found in Users/&lt;My user name>/AppData tree and then running SourceTree again and using the Personal Access Token to log in when prompted. 

I tried deleting the Atlassian folders I found in the same location, but was not prompted to log in, and faced the same problem when I tried to add my Personal Access Token again. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/242#issuecomment-3928427401" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hello again. Just to clarify, I did run `bingbong.jar` in a new folder on the desktop when I faced that error. I re-tested it again and managed to recreate the error. To be specific, placing `bingbong.jar` anywhere on my computer, whether it be the desktop, my C drive or D drive, will encounter the same error of _"Something went wrong when initializing task storage: C:\Windows\system32\.\data. Please fix the problem and try again"_ even though I did not place it in `system32` (which is a very weird location to place the file in the first place). But just to double check, I went to ask some LLM. The basics of the problem seems to be as follows:

The `bingbong.jar` likely builds a relative path using `.\data` to store its information when the program saves. But on a Windows system, when you double-click a `JAR` file, Java sets the working directory to `C:\Windows\system32` by default, not the current directory where the `JAR` file resides. This is the default working directory for processes launched via file association on Windows. So the file can be sitting on your Desktop, but Java still thinks it is running from `system32`. Hence, when it tries to create its `data` folder for task storage, it builds the relative path and resolves to `C:\Windows\system32\.\data`. Running on the terminal is fine because the working directory is where your terminal is currently pointed to.

The suggested fix is to retrieve the `JAR` file's actual location where the user stored their `bingbong.jar` via something like `System.getProperty("user.home")`. Then have the program add the relative path `.\data` back.

That said, I am a little puzzled since others above me seem to be testing without issues. It might be worth getting another Windows user to verify as it could still simply be a problem on my side.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/242#issuecomment-3928568559" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As mentioned, this problem is specific to double-clicking the `.jar` file. That's why my suggestion was to "add a single line to guide users the start `bingbong.jar` via the terminal in the user guide."
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

### 186. LEE ..XIAN `@kip1425` (4 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/139#issuecomment-3841592231" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In my opinion, it is better to have an abstract Command class, which has subclasses that execute the command.

For example, the parser would return a DeleteCommand, which has an execute method that calls your tasklist.deleteTask method and saves the task. Execution of the command would be done in the chatbot class.

Here is an example : 

<img width="907" height="198" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1cedf647-73d4-4f08-93be-9ea229bcadc6" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/94#issuecomment-3841685796" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I had a similar issue as well and tried manually writing the gradle files myself. 

I realised the problem was I had forgotten to merge the upstream add-gradle-support branch into my master branch.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/651#issuecomment-4231510737" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think grading in the actual PE may be subjective based on the grader. I would say to try your best to clarify users' doubts in your UG and DG, but we should wait until the teaching team provides us a concrete answer.



</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/646#issuecomment-4231526990" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Yes, they should be counted as bugs. As stated by the previous comments, even "bugs" inherited from AB3 should be considered. I would suggest thinking about the scope of your product and whether certain edge cases for inherited AB3 features should be kept in your final iteration.
</panel>

</panel>

### 188. FATH..BINA `@memoflora` (4 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/254#issuecomment-3925019348" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi. I've also faced this issue before. Similar to @archonward has said, yes I think you should split the test method. Instead of making a method for each `assertEquals`, you can group them simply based on the command type. 
For example: `parse_addCommand_correctFormat`, `parse_listCommand_correctFormat`, etc.
This way, the refactoring is not excessive.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/416#issuecomment-3945649675" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Works well on Mac. Tried various commands.
LGTM.

<img width="642" height="1051" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cc6b61b5-6fb0-4b57-a5d8-942630b24816" />

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/321#issuecomment-3956878902" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

OS: Mac

Things checked:
- [x] App can start
- [x] All commands works as described in the user guide
- [ ] App resizes correctly: I can only resize the app vertically but not horizontally
- [x] Data persistence
- [x] No abnormal behaviour/errors

So, probably the only issue is the app resizing. Other than that, the app works just fine.
One thing you might want to add is the app title that usually appears on top of the window.
You can do it by writing `stage.setTitle("App Title")` in your `Main.java`

Hope this helps!

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/181#issuecomment-3881816002" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I don't think theres a "need" for this kind of refactoring (or any kind of refactoring) but it would probably depend on the context. The example given is kind of contrived since what's happening in the loop in the first example isn't really complicated to the point where it necessitates the refactoring done here.

That being said, you could encounter a loop with lots of operations and it could be better for those operations to be split up or grouped. This makes the code readable and an individual group of operations could then be modified more easily.

This also allows for those various groups to instead be [defined as methods](https://refactoring.com/catalog/extractFunction.html), at which point, we have gone from a single for-loop with lots of operations to several methods each handling a different aspect, which is simpler to comprehend and debug IMO.

When it comes to performance, its unlikely you'll notice a performance issue since you would not only need to have a large enough $n$ (roughly $10&hat;7$), but probably also have close to $n$ number of loops in your program (roughly $10&hat;7$ for-loops).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/198#issuecomment-3901016973" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Since the `.jar` is a binary file, you would generally not try to track it like source code.

Instead, head over to your Github fork and go to the `Releases` section here...

<img width="357" height="433" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ce3133de-3d22-472a-a68a-278c102dec11" />

Then, ensure you're in the releases tab and click on draft a new release.

<img width="1027" height="111" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4b8fb935-fc0a-4719-8ebd-b1927fe34c2e" />

Once you're there, give the release a suitable name and select the `.jar` binary file and drag & drop it into the area below at the bottom, and publish the release.

<img width="1003" height="947" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f9d3cf7e-26ad-4c14-821c-70a7606975cf" />

Here's a shorter TL:DR version from the CS2103 website.

<img width="915" height="277" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/33e53d27-9e65-49e4-8708-0545d88c63ee" />

Hope this helps! If I'm wrong, feel free to correct me!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/647#issuecomment-4230833779" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<img width="971" height="359" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cae08e1-d673-4d1f-8438-a7dcc2f221b9" />

<img width="915" height="679" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c271bdb3-62cf-4fce-b0ff-ef56dfbbf158" />
<br><br>

I think whether those would count as bugs would depend on the specific context. However, based on the rubrics and from what you stated, I don't believe those are bugs.


It should be sufficient to provide the specific syntax (`phone number should be digit-only (no +/-)` or `a position cannot contain spaces`) and a few valid and/or invalid examples. This should be sufficient and comprehensive for most users. Doing it this way could also help you in your testing process, since you are actively thinking and verifying whether the system is behaving according to your specifications.

For the actual PE, I believe there is some form of judgement made by the teaching team on whether the bug reports being made are valid (should be phase 4, after you contest the bug report made against you).
<br>
<img width="1017" height="631" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa15e18-8522-4eec-83f6-9e20b0a14ce6" />
<br>
I hope this helps! If I've written or understood something incorrectly, do let me know!
</panel>

</panel>

OS: macOS Tahoe 26.3

### Smoke Test Checklist
- [X] **Commands**: All commands specified in the User Guide work as intended.
- [X] **Crashes/Weird Behaviour**: None occured during the duration of the smoke test.
- [ ] **Error handling**: 
   - [ ] Missing error handling for empty `todo` and `deadline` commands. When entering `todo` or `deadline` command in isolation, the command cannot be executed, indicating an unhandled error on the programmer's part.
   - [ ] Error handling could be more specific to let the user know what information is missing. When creating a deadline with no description, the error message prompts that the due date and time is missing instead of the description. (Figure 1)
   - [ ] When creating a `todo`, `deadline` or `event`, if there is no whitespace after the command word, the program executes the command and simply removes the first character following the command word and sets it as the description. (Figure 2)
   - [ ] Missing error handling for `delete` command when trying to delete a task at a non-existent index. A similar error handling already exists for the `mark` and `unmark` commands but not for `delete`. (Figure 3)
   - [ ] When using the `edit` command and the task type is not specified, the task will be overwritten and will no longer possess any type. (Figure 4)
- [X] **Data Persistency**: Works well. Upon exiting the program, tasks are saved to a `sleeper.txt` file. Tasks are loaded correctly when launching the app again.

<img width="387" height="236" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f97fa3ee-7f81-434e-9d42-5fef28ba3984" />

Other than the error handling issues mentioned above, the program works fine :)

### 199. AU Y..CTOR `@VictorAuYeung` (4 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/100#issuecomment-3821409207" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You can essentially just "shift" the commit to the correct branch by:

git checkout correct-branch

Grab the commit: git cherry-pick &lt;commit-hash>

Go back to the wrong branch: git checkout wrong-branch

Remove it: git reset --hard HEAD~1 (This wipes the last commit on this branch)

In a way its "copying" the commit to the right branch and then "deleting" it from the wrong one, which should be easier than recreating the same commit.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/100#issuecomment-3821499449" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

If theres multiple commits above then you can still do the cherry picking, so it can be

Get the ID: Copy the hash of the commit u wanted (e.g. a1b2c3d).
Switch to correct branch: git checkout correct-branch
Cherry-pick it: git cherry-pick a1b2c3d

So now your correct branch has the right commit copied into it already, if you don't mind leaving the wrong commit in your old branch you technically don't need to do anything else. 

For this mod idk about the policy for removing wrong commits in a branch if they are already under multiple commits, but if you wanna try can look into git rebase or revert (to revert a past commit in a branch). Hopefully someone from the teaching team can comment which is recommended?




</panel>

### 202. LIAM..RMAN `@Siimen111` (3 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/116#issuecomment-3828164316" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

IMO, it does not really violate the coding standard, since it does "sound like" a boolean. However, I do believe that this form of programming can be flawed in certain scenarios, i.e. if the save transaction is critical, like a backup, where it may be important to alert the user/program that saving has failed, and thus it may be wise to throw an exception to alert them. Contrast that with the function being non-critical, like saving to cache, then I think allowing it to return a true/false would be perfectly acceptable.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/386#issuecomment-3935662471" expanded>

### 204. ZHEN..AWEN `@incursi0n` (3 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/67#issuecomment-3796930835" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, do you have multiple Java versions installed? By default, Gradle uses the same JVM that it is running on to build the project, unless configured otherwise. Since this project does not specify anything in build.gradle,[ Gradle simply falls back to the Gradle JVM](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/toolchains.html). Thats why Eclipse Temurin 17.0.12 is used. Since Gradle is in use, IntelliJ’s own Project SDK setting is not in effect for building, as IntelliJ is not the one performing the build.

<img width="1166" height="466" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/68d23c7d-e58d-4b50-847b-1bea5dab8f67" />

You can use `gradlew.bat -version` to check what JVM is Gradle running on.
```
.\gradlew.bat -version

------------------------------------------------------------
Gradle 8.14
------------------------------------------------------------

Build time:    2025-04-25 09:29:08 UTC
Revision:      34c560e3be961658a6fbcd7170ec2443a228b109

Kotlin:        2.0.21
Groovy:        3.0.24
Ant:           Apache Ant(TM) version 1.10.15 compiled on August 25 2024
Launcher JVM:  22.0.1 (Oracle Corporation 22.0.1+8-16)
Daemon JVM:    C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-22 (no JDK specified, using current Java home)
OS:            Windows 11 10.0 amd64
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815746475" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I usually just use non-static class whenever it's available since it is more flexible. 

Here is a table that I use when deciding which to use:
```
| Qn                                             | Yes        | No         |
|------------------------------------------------|------------|------------|
| Does it hold state?                            | Non-static | Next qn    |
| Might I need more than one version later?      | Non-static | Next qn    |
| Does it interact with IO / files / user input? | Non-static | Next qn    |
| Is it just a pure helper function?             | Static     | Non-static |
```
In short, just start with non-static. Make it static only if you’re 100% sure it should never change.

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/96#issuecomment-3822741355" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think returning a `TaskList` from Storage seems to be good use of OOP. Since other parts of the app do not have to know that tasks are stored in an `ArrayList`. They only need a list of tasks with a direct API(add, get, remove, size, etc.) which is what `TaskList` is. If Storage returns `ArrayList`, every caller has to depend on that type. If you are switching to another structure later on(e.g. another list implementation or a different abstraction), you have to change all those calls to that method. If Storage returns `TaskList`, only Storage (and `TaskList`'s internals) need to know the actual data structure so changes only have to be made there.
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 209. NICH.. KEE `@Frankwotfurters` (3 posts) <span class=text-warning>:octicon-eye:</span>
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/542" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: 💡Importing your tP with gradle working [for MacOS + VSCode]**
</div>

Hi all, just wanted to share a problem for those specifically running MacOS + VSCode, and how I managed to fix it.

### The Issue

When importing the AB3/tP project, VSCode does not automatically recognise the gradle project, and you lose functionality in the IDE such as running gradle tasks and the debugger. At the bottom of VSCode it will say `Gradle: Build Error`.

### The Solution

At the root directory of your project, create `.vscode/settings.json` if it doesn't exist yet, and add the following
```
``{``
  "java.jdt.ls.java.home": "/Users/[YOUR USERNAME]/.sdkman/candidates/java/17.0.14.fx-zulu",
  "java.import.gradle.java.home": "/Users/[YOUR USERNAME]/.sdkman/candidates/java/17.0.14.fx-zulu"
``}``
```

### Possible Explanation

Since Mac users were required to download Java 17 through SDKMan, it is not in the default location that VSCode searches for the Java binary, so we have to specifically direct it to the correct place!

### Don't push this to your teammates!

In .gitignore, add:
```
# VSCode stuff
.vscode/*
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/154" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: [iP] Will there be potential issues if most functions are static?**
</div>

In the iP, most of my methods are declared as static. Could this cause any issues down the road if this were a longer term project? Curious as the code snippets provided for `A-MoreOOP` seemed to have implemented them as non-static methods, as evident in `ui = new Ui();`.

Here are some of my examples:
```
public class Storage ``{``
    public final static String STORAGE_FILE_PATH = "tasks.txt";
    public static void storeTasks(TaskList taskList) ``{``
        try (FileOutputStream fos = new FileOutputStream(STORAGE_FILE_PATH);
            ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(fos)) ``{``
            
            oos.writeObject(taskList);
            // System.out.println("Object successfully saved to " + STORAGE_FILE_PATH);

        ``}`` catch (IOException e) ``{``
            System.err.println("Error saving object: " + e.getMessage());
            e.printStackTrace();
        ``}``
    ``}``
    // Other methods redacted
``}``
```

```
public class Ui ``{``
    protected static Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

    public static void printWelcomeMessage() ``{``
        System.out.println("----------------------------");
        System.out.println("What can I help you with?");
        System.out.println("----------------------------");
    ``}``

    public static String promptInput() ``{``
        System.out.print("Input > ");
        String command = sc.nextLine();
        return command;
    ``}``
``}``
```

So most of them will be called as `Ui.promptInput();` or `Storage.storeTasks(taskList);`.


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/497#issuecomment-4062680945" expanded>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815464141" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the key question in deciding whether a class should be static or non-static is whether the class is expected to maintain state or represent an object that needs to be instantiated.

If a class represents a “thing” or object with identity or state, then it should be non-static.

If a class represents pure behaviour, where its methods operate solely on input parameters and do not depend on or modify state, then I would usually declare the class as static.

For example, the Parser class in our IP that only processes and translates user input into commands for your chatbot would be declared as static to me since the class and its methods can operate well purely on input parameters and do not require any existing state or object instantiation. 

</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 215. ZHAN..UHAN `@Zhangshuhan05` (3 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/603" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Command error handling in parser**
</div>


Hello, while we were testing the app, if a user enters a command with a small mistake such as missing spaces (e.g. circleadd instead of circle add, the command word is technically still right), the app returns a generic “Unknown command” message. We are finding ways to make the error message more helpful to the users.

One approach we considered is simple normalization (e.g. removing whitespace in parser and matching against known commands), which would handle spacing issues.

Another approach is to implement fuzzy matching (e.g. using edit distance) to suggest the closest valid command even when there are minor typos (e.g. circl add → circle add).

IWe understand that ultimately its our own design decision, but we wanted to ask if considering such input mistakes is a reasonable design concern, and whether one approach might be more suitable to implement.

Thank you!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/589" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Accuracy of error messages**
</div>


Hello! For our tp, how precise and exact must our error handling messages be? Is it better to give more generic error handling or really specific to what the user did wrong? 

Thank you
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/589#issuecomment-4163737340" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

@jianrontan @damithc Thank you so much for the advices!
</panel>

</panel>

### 223. KEVI..RICK `@Kevin-129` (3 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/374#issuecomment-3935926338" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi. I’ve been testing Dylan on Windows 11 and I think the design is great. I like how the colors change when you mark, unmark, or delete a task. It is a really nice touch. It makes the CLI feel much more interactive and polished.

Everything works as intended for the most part, but I did find two issues you might want to look at:

Reverse Events: The bot currently allows an event to end before it starts (e.g., /from 26/2/2028 1600 /to 26/2/2026 1400).

Past Deadlines: It accepts deadlines for dates that have already passed without a warning (e.g., /by 1/1/1999 0000).

Overall, I think your app works great, good job!

<img width="587" height="1387" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d7d52d37-4740-49e1-ab3d-65a36e713437" />
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/334#issuecomment-3935978318" expanded>

### 224. BENJ..ENYU `@Benjumpin` (3 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/701" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Is the team liable for a bug if the default AB3 app already have the same issue.**
</div>

We have a bug that came from the original AB3 application. Our team tested on the starting AB3 application and was able to replicate the same bug. Are we liable for thiis.

Labels:
Medium, FunctionalityBug

Issue: 
App can become unresponsive after closing Help window after a series of commands. 

Steps to reproduce:

Launch App.
Put the main app window into full screen.
Enter help, and close the resulting Help window.
Exit from full screen mode for the app.
Enter help.
Close the Help window.
If the app still works, repeat steps 5 and 6 a few more times.
Alternative steps to reproduce:

Launch App.
Enter any command, such as list.
Put the main app window into full screen.
Enter help, and press the copy URL button, and close the resulting Help window.
Expected: The Help window closes and the main App window remains usable.  

Expected: 
The Help window closes and the main app window remains usable.

Actual: 
Sometimes, after closing the Help window, the app becomes unresponsive, and the process has to be terminated from the command line.

Notes: 
This is intermittent and sometimes only occurs after opening the Help window and closing it multiple times, while other times it happens after one opening-closing cycle. It appears tied to opening a detached Help window after the main JavaFX window has gone through full-screen and minimized states.

Only happens on MacOS 

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/701#issuecomment-4276081378" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

thanks @choppyback 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/701#issuecomment-4276103675" expanded>
<div slot="header">

* Things to check specifically:
Commands should work as expected
todo: adds into list, error handling works with bad inputs (e.g., empty description throws error)
deadline and event: adds into list, error handling works (checks for missing /by, /from, /to). Flexible datetime formats and chronological validation (end before start) works.

mark and unmark: works, error handling works for invalid indices.
find: works for both keywords and hashtags (e.g., find #work).
tag and untag: works, handles multi-tagging and normalization (adding # if missing).
exit: works, UI is disabled upon bye, then closes.

Tasks and multi-tags persist correctly in data/groot.txt.
Loader is robust against legacy data formats and handles corrupted lines without crashing.
UI/UX Verification
Resizing: Resizing of UI works, elements scale and wrap correctly.
Window Title: The title is "Groot"
Theming: Rocket Raccoon (user) and Groot (bot) icons display correctly in chat bubbles.


Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/425" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

* Link to JAR file: https://github.com/SuhanKim96/ip/releases/tag/A-Release
* Link to the User Guide: https://suhankim96.github.io/ip/
* Which OS'es to test on: Windows | Linux
* Things to check specifically:
  1. **Launch:** Run `java -jar kiki.jar` in the terminal and ensure the GUI opens smoothly without any crashes.
  2. **Basic Functionality:** Add a task (e.g., `todo test task`) and use the `list` command to verify it was added correctly.
  3. **Data Persistence:** Close the application (`bye` or closing the window) and relaunch it to confirm your tasks are saved and loaded automatically.
  4. **Error Handling:** Type a random invalid command to ensure the app catches the error gracefully instead of crashing.

Guide for testers:
* Post screenshots from your smoke tests
* Mention which OS you used for testing
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/198#issuecomment-3901107893" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the main goal here is just to get you comfortable with Gradle and make sure your environment is set up to package all your project files into a single JAR file correctly. Basically, it's just a check to see if you can put all the pieces together into one runnable file.

Since JARs are binary files, it’s standard practice to keep them out of Git to avoid messing up your repository with unnecessary history. The best practice is to upload it as a GitHub Release. It’s a much cleaner way to track your versions without cluttering your commits.

You can find the step-by-step instructions for that in the A-Jar section of the manual.
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 233. NIIK..SEAH `@niikseah` (2 posts)

OS: MacOS Sonoma

<img width="552" height="740" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/13ed75a3-f528-48d7-9467-291611a61adf" />

Features tested and no major issues have been found.

**On the UI**
The UI is vibrant and Bob has an interesting personality, making for an engaging chatbot.
The use of colours for differentiating valid, invalid, and different events makes it easy for identification.
However, I find that some colour contrasts, such as the red with the black text for invalid messages, can strain the eyes.

**On the UX**
As someone who opened the chatbot without clicking on the documentation of commands, it can be troublesome to refer to the commands on the webpage. I recommend a help function to list out all the commands you have implemented!
Also, I realise that the user's input text is shown on the left instead of on the right, this can be confusing as our existing mental model is our messages sent will be shown on the right side, maybe you want to consider changing this! 

I think this chatbot is well made and the suggestions I have are more cosmetic than functional. I really appreciate the personality this chatbot has!
</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 234. YAP ..YANG `@mingjelly` (2 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/163" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: TP Possible Routes**
</div>


Hi Prof @damithc ,

Our team has some questions regarding the tP, particular given the evolve constraint of the addressbook. We have generated many ideas but we wanted to clarify whether some of these ideas fit the "evolve" constraint:

1) Rather than contact details of "People", we considered storing places (E.g. Food/Travel destinations), for people to keep this as a record for future use. Mainly, the question here is whether or not the new address book has to explicitly store People and their contact details.
2) We have some other ideas which do fit the same purpose of keeping people's contact details for tracking. But we were also wondering whether, if the address/contact details is not really the primary purpose, would it be acceptable. E.g. The user does use the address but the main focus of the addressbook is for example, keep tracking of finances, tags, etc.


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815248781" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, apart from the really great points raised, I think the difference or choice between using static and non-static class becomes clearer if you think about how other people read and understand your code.

Creating an instance and non-static methods associated with it is communicating to the reader/others who are using your code that there is a supposed state that you will be manipulating within the instance. They might expect that this instance would hold meaningful data. Rather, if you use a static method, it is more simple and it tells people that there is no instance to be tracked and it is simply a self-contained operation. Creating a redundant object instance would mislead readers and also create unnecessary code.

Similarly, if a class contains static and non-static methods, it depends on whether you are manipulating the state within the instance object to be created. Non-static for state manipulation and static otherwise.
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

When the game notices a corrupted save file, it loads a backup of the last save while prompting the user that the current save file is corrupted. Although unfortunate the first save file cannot be recovered, at least there is a secondary backup that the user can load backup as a last resort.
Perhaps its something worth pondering about!

</div>

I used version 11.0.0 since I remember seeing somewhere that was the original version that the files were designed for. In my case I'm using the "Checkstyle for Java" VSCode plugin since that's the IDE I'm using, though. So probably not that helpful to you.

11.0.1 and 11.1.0 should still work, I think.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3882047499" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In the beginning, I only really used VSCode's inbuilt github copilot for minor coding tasks and bugfixes.

But form Level-10 onwards, I have been using AI to generate major refactors and features for my iP, then going through it in a second pass and checking it personally. For example, the entire switch from terminal UI to a graphical UI was completely AI generated. This included all the bug fixes, changes to OOP, and switching from terminal input to a more properly OOP'ed form of input handling.

For my tooling - I've been using Z.AI's GLM 4.7 from it's coding [plan](https://z.ai/subscribe?ic=1AUOZSMDNJ), as it was quite cheap. (On sale, 36$ for 12 months from last year's christmas sale, have never run into rate limit issues despite heavy usage). Since my IDE of choice is VSCode, I've been pairing it with Cline. 

My 'rate of development' has since skyrocketed exponentially. I've switched my Commands from simple functions to a more object-oriented format, something which I've been delaying for weeks, in just a few hours by heavily leveraging AI. Another nice thing is that by using the "Checkstyle for Java" extension in VSCode, Cline can also see the Checkstyle errors and fixes them autonomously as it generates new code.


The only issues I've encountered thus far with this model is in the JavaFX FXML. It seems to confuse it for HTML and creates syntax that is invalid. But I've fixed this by simply throwing the generated file into Gemini instead occasionally to 'repair' it.
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Request to use MCP Java SDK and Eclipse Jetty**
</div>

## Library

[MCP Java SDK](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/java-sdk)
[Eclipse Jetty](https://github.com/jetty/jetty.project)

## Purpose

To allow AI agents to interact with the app via the MCP protocol. Eclipse Jetty is used to host the web server for streamable HTTP transport. Feature is optional, and users are expected to use their own MCP client should they want to use the feature.

## License

- MCP Java SDK ([MIT License](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/java-sdk/blob/main/LICENSE))
- Eclipse Jetty ([EPL-2.0 OR Apache-2.0](https://github.com/jetty/jetty.project/blob/jetty-12.1.x/LICENSE))

</panel>

### 249. LAI ..NICE `@print-shanice` (2 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/686" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Clarification on use cases in DG**
</div>

Hi, the course website states that a possible DG bug is if important use cases are missing, as seen below.

<img width="678" height="392" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ea063f73-603f-4a71-bf96-a3a517029358" />


My question is this — what deems a use case to be important? For example, I would think that a use case to add a person would be considered important since it is a feature that a user MUST have to use the application (MVP feature) while on the other hand, a feature like clear is unlikely to be considered important. 

However, what about a feature that is not considered a MVP feature like editing a meeting/task? Would this be considered an important use case that if missing, is a bug?

I appreciate any input!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/603#issuecomment-4163260464" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi! I think this is quite subjective and both approaches are definitely worth considering. 

Personally, I’d lean more towards normalisation since it’s simpler and also less likely to introduce unexpected bugs. Fuzzy matching in practice can get tricky, you need to decide how “close” an input must be to a valid command before suggesting commands, and I'm not sure what your commands look like, but if an input ends up being equally similar to multiple commands, fuzzy matching can confuse users with inconsistent suggestions, affecting usability.

For either approach, I think the most important thing is to avoid accepting unclear, incorrect input and instead return a more informative error message, like “Unknown command — did you mean …”. That way, you guide the user and reduce the likelihood of introducing bugs and unexpected behaviour.

</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi, I tested on macOS. It runs but I saw this warning:
WARNING: Loading FXML document with JavaFX API of version 25 by JavaFX runtime of version 22.0.4

This means your FXML is using JavaFX 25, but your runtime is JavaFX 22. Since the course requires Java 17, I suggest aligning everything to JavaFX 17. (update JavaFX version in build.gradle and FXML files)
</panel>

</panel>

Hi @danielyap4103, I didn't manage to run the `atlas.jar` file.

OS: Linux

I believe it might be because you only load the dependencies for the current OS that you are running with (Windows). The javafx tutorial said that:

> Your build.gradle needs to include all JavaFX dependencies given above (not just the lines matching your OS), for your app to work on all three OSes."

Maybe you can try adding all 3 groups of dependencies instead of checking for `os.isWindows()`, etc.? Otherwise, maybe someone else not using Windows can test this?

Here is the error messages, if needed:

<div slot="header">

Hi @alastair-tan-cw !

Here's my smoke test findings!

<img width="419" height="797" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6fe88638-1ab7-45f0-9ddd-6a9966143296" />

Test Environment
OS tested on: macOS Sequoia 15.5.1
Java: OpenJDK 17

Launch Test
App launches successfully using java -jar peter.jar.

Basic Command Checks

Tested the following commands and they work as expected:

list

todo

deadline

event

mark / unmark

find

delete

snooze

bye

Creating, deleting, and snoozing tasks function correctly.
Deadlines and event dates update properly after snoozing.
Data file is created in the local folder and persists after relaunch.
Invalid index and incorrect date formats are handled gracefully without crashes.
The bye command exits the application successfully on macOS.

UI Feedback

The GUI looks very polished 👍

The circular user profile icons look neat and give the chat a modern feel.

The chat bubble boundaries are clean and consistent, making the conversation easy to read.

Spacing between messages feels balanced and readable.

Overall, the app is stable and visually well-designed on macOS. Great work! 🦆

</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/237" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Sequence diagram exercise solution**
</div>


For this sequence diagram exercise on finding notation mistakes,

<img width="1127" height="669" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b55e6699-477d-46c6-94d5-a1e33d284590" />

Some of the mistakes I found include:

- The loop should be labeled as loop instead of while
- The loop box should have the corner cut off
- Arrow that represents the call to the constructor of Bar should be labelled
- Arrow representing return from the constructor should be dashed arrows
- The activation bar of Foo is too long ( do() should be at the top of the activation bar)

Did I miss out any other mistakes in the sequence diagram?
</panel>

OS tested on: Windows | **Mac** (Tahoe 26.3) | Linux
Things to check specifically:
i. App can start - **Can start**.
ii. All commands works as described in the user guide - **ALL commands work as intended.**
iii. App resizes correctly - **App can only resize vertically, not horizontally (intended functionality?)**
iv. Data persistence - **Tasks persisted after restarting chatbot.**
v. No abnormal behaviour/errors
- As mentioned above, your `finddate` and `find` commands likely uses the task's index in your data structure to store tasks. Would you consider displaying user-friendly indexes with respect to the output list?
- Also, your UI still displays user inputs as a side effect, is that intended?

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/357" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2. :fas-info-circle: Request to help with smoke testing JAR file**
</div>

# Smoke Test Request

Hi everyone! I would appreciate it if you could help me perform a smoke test for my application.

##  Links
- **Link to JAR file:** [[Download JAR](https://github.com/LiaoYantong/ip/releases/download/v2.0/stewie.jar)](#)  
- **Link to the User Guide:** [[User Guide](https://liaoyantong.github.io/ip/)](#)  

## OS to test on
- Windows  
- Mac  
- Linux  

## Things to check specifically
- Commands work as specified in the User Guide  
- Data persistence for alias, task, and product  
- GUI works as expected  
- Error handling (invalid commands, missing files, etc.)

##  Guide for testers
- Post screenshots from your smoke tests  
- Mention which OS you used for testing  

Thank you very much for your help!


</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

### 283. YAP .. WEN `@yapshaowen` (1 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/93#issuecomment-3815453679" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I usually default to non-static classes unless there’s a strong reason not to. Even if static works, keeping things as objects feels cleaner to me because it’s easier to extend later and easier to reason about who owns what.

For iP specifically, I’m okay making Parser static since it can be pretty stateless, but I prefer Ui and Storage as non-static because they naturally carry stuff like input/output behaviour or file path/config. It’s just less painful if I need to change or test things later.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

### 288. CHON.. JUN `@dubbeyou` (1 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/140#issuecomment-3845138133" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hello, I faced the exact error twice after merging with `add-gradle-support` branch. From researching online, many would suggest running `Java: Clean Java Language Server Workspace`. However, this did not work for me.

I suspect my error had something to do with VS Code utilising extension cache files for the following extension: *Language Support for Java(TM) by Red Hat*

To fix the error (worked for me both times), I resorted to uninstalling and reinstalling VS Code.
Do delete the following directories too before installing VS Code again:
1. %APPDATA%\Code
2. %USERPROFILE%\.vscode

Hope this helps!
</panel>

</panel>


<panel type="info"  collapsed>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: Can we assign responsibilities unequally**
</div>

Hi my group is checking if it is possible to divide an issue's blame non-equally ie 25-25-16.7-16.7-16.7. As we have accepted a bug in our application but however we do not believe it's fair to split the blame equally and the circumstances resulting in the bug is very vague. Blame can be assigned but assigning it in equal parts of 5 or just to 2 people is deemed unfair in our discussions as other members had to take partial blame in this issue.

### 302. CHOW..G YU `@Cxyu629` (1 posts) <span class=text-warning>:octicon-eye:</span>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/198#issuecomment-3901018095" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As mentioned in the Week 3 iP task `A-Jar`, generally binary files are not committed or git tracked. The _intention_ for the task you've mentioned is to **check that gradle is working correctly** (i.e. bundling JavaFX libraries with the `.jar` file), rather than requiring you to add the new `.jar` file to GitHub.

If you do want to add it to GitHub, it's more ideal to add it as a release, according to the instructions below (screenshot taken from Week 3 `A-Jar` task). 

<img width="960" height="917" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bbc827a5-9b29-45cc-b738-57dc635cc28b" />
</panel>

</panel>

### 304. NGUY.. NAM `@ephtale` (1 posts)

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/182#issuecomment-3877713204" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I used AI to code the basic functionality since the logic is relatively simple and only requires patience (and basic Java knowledge) to code out. It is also useful for JavaDoc since it is formulaic. All that is left is to verify that it does its job correctly, with minimal bugs, and then we can check for coding standard. Note that if it tries to use an external library make sure that the library is the correct version, and sometimes you might have to check that thee syntax the AI is using is not deprecated / futuristic for that version.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/126" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1. :fas-info-circle: JavaFX cannot find symbol error**
</div>

Anyone else encountered this before? Almost every class and even @Override are all underlined in red saying "cannot find symbol". I have tried refreshing the file, cleaning Java Language Server Workspace, Restarting the Java language server. I even made a settings.json file (see code below) but still not working.

<img width="960" height="600" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d928bec3-284f-466e-b015-3c55e09f19de" />


settings.json:
``{``
  "java.configuration.updateBuildConfiguration": "disabled",
  "java.import.gradle.enabled": true,
  "java.project.referencedLibraries": [
    "~/.gradle/caches/**/javafx-*-17.0.7-win.jar"
  ]
``}``


</panel>

</panel>

### 322. RYAN..XUAN `@Creomeow` (1 posts) 
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/forum/issues/647#issuecomment-4231301337" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I agree that with the above points on documentation, and would like to bring up something else from the course website.

<img width="1144" height="710" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/05e248fa-3065-4684-b886-862f0f0cede1" />

Based on what you've stated, I believe this is considered more of a feature flaw as the behaviour is intentional and documented. The constraint is explicitly stated in the UG, so the app is working exactly as designed. A functionality bug implies the app is doing something unintended or broken. That's not the case here.

Based on this optional requirement under Deliverable: Developer Guide (DG), we can consider adding "varied parameter input" as an extension to further clarify that this is out-of-scope of "legitimate user behaviour" for this product specifically. 
</panel>

</panel>
